这是一部富有洞见、发人深省的数学史，讲述了神奇的数学如何改变我们的世界。《品茶的女士》并非枯燥乏味的统计数据堆砌，而是记录了那些敢于以全新视角看待世界的伟大人物的故事。

An insightful, revealing history of the magical mathematics that transformed our world. The Lady Tasting Tea is not a book of dry facts and figures, but the history of great individuals who dared to look at the world in a new way.

在英国剑桥的一次夏日茶会上，一位客人说，茶兑牛奶的味道和牛奶兑茶的味道不一样。她的说法遭到在场科学人士的强烈反对。但其中一位名叫罗纳德·费舍尔的男士提议用科学方法验证这个假设。费舍尔是统计学领域的先驱，因此没有人比他更适合进行这项实验。

At a summer tea party in Cambridge, England, a guest states that tea poured into milk tastes different from milk poured into tea. Her notion is shouted down by the scientific minds of the group. But one man, Ronald Fisher, proposes to scientifically test the hypothesis. There is no better person to conduct such an experiment, for Fisher is a pioneer in the field of statistics.

《品茶的女士》不仅聚焦费舍尔的理论，更展现了数十位男女的革命性思想，这些思想至今仍影响着我们的现代日常生活。大卫·萨尔斯堡文笔生动诙谐，追溯了从卡尔·皮尔逊理论的兴衰到战后重建日本经济的质量控制方法等一系列突破性进展，其中包括一项关于吉尼斯啤酒厂小型啤酒桶容量的早期关键研究。书中充满了引人入胜的趣闻轶事和鲜活的人物形象，《品茶的女士》向那些敢于以全新视角看待世界的人们致敬。

The Lady Tasting Tea spotlights not only Fisher's theories but also the revolutionary ideas of dozens of men and women which affect our modern everyday lives. Writing with verve and wit, David Salsburg traces breakthroughs ranging from the rise and fall of Karl Pearson's theories to the methods of quality control that rebuilt postwar Japan's economy, including a pivotal early study on the capacity of a small beer cask at the Guinness brewing factory. Brimming with intriguing tidbits and colorful characters, The Lady Tasting Tea salutes the spirit of those who dared to look at the world in a new way.

大卫·萨尔斯堡

David Salsburg

品茶的女士

The Lady Tasting Tea

统计学如何革新了二十世纪的科学

How Statistics Revolutionized Science in the Twentieth Century

ePub r1.0

ePub r1.0

Titivillus 2026年1月31日

Titivillus 31.01.2026

大卫·萨尔斯堡，2001年

David Salsburg, 2001

 

 

数字编辑：Titivillus

Digital editor: Titivillus

ePub 基础版 r2.1

ePub base r2.1

谨以此书献给我亲爱的妻子弗兰，我们相伴42年。在我职业生涯中，我积累了许多关于统计学革命先驱的故事，而她一直敦促我将它们写成一本非数学类的书籍。弗兰本人没有接受过任何数学训练，但她却在我多次修改本书的过程中给予了我莫大的支持，指出我解释不够清晰的地方。本书，尤其是那些通俗易懂的部分，都离不开她的坚持不懈。

Dedicated to Fran, my dear wife of 42 years. As I accumulated stories about the men and women who made the statistical revolution during my career, she kept urging me to put them in a nonmathematical book. Fran, who has no mathematical training, helped me through the several revisions of this book, pointing out to me sections where my explanations were not clear. This book, especially those parts that are clearly understandable, is due to her persistence.

你不可回答问卷调查

Thou shalt not answer questionnaires

或者关于世界事务的测验，

Or quizzes upon World Affairs,

也不符合规定

Nor with compliance

参加任何考试。你不可坐下

Take any test. Thou shalt not sit

既不与统计学家合作，也不承诺

With statisticians nor commit

社会科学。

A social science.

——WH A奥登

—W. H. AUDEN

要了解上帝的想法，我们必须研究统计学，因为统计学是衡量他旨意的尺度。

To understand God’s thoughts, we must study statistics, for these are the measure of his purpose.

——F ·洛伦斯· N ·奈廷格尔

—FLORENCE NIGHTINGALE

作者序​​

AUTHOR’S PREFACE

S十九世纪初，科学界带着一种被称为“机械宇宙”的坚定哲学理念进入社会。人们相信，只需少量数学公式（例如牛顿运动定律和玻意耳气体定律），就能描述现实并预测未来事件。而进行此类预测所需的仅仅是一套完整的公式以及一组足够精确的相关测量数据。大众文化花了四十多年才逐渐接受这种科学理念。

Science entered the nineteenth century with a firm philosophical vision that has been called the clockwork universe. It was believed that there were a small number of mathematical formulas (like Newton’s laws of motion and Boyle’s laws for gases) that could be used to describe reality and to predict future events. All that was needed for such prediction was a complete set of these formulas and a group of associated measurements that were taken with sufficient precision. It took over forty years for popular culture to catch up with this scientific vision.

这种文化滞后在十九世纪初拿破仑皇帝与皮埃尔·西蒙·拉普拉斯的交流中体现得淋漓尽致。拉普拉斯撰写了一部鸿篇巨著，阐述了如何根据地球上的少量观测数据来计算行星和彗星的未来位置。“拉普拉斯先生，我在您的论著中没有发现任何关于上帝的论述，”据说拿破仑曾这样说道。“我根本不需要那个假设，”拉普拉斯回答道。

Typical of this cultural lag is the exchange between Emperor Napoleon and Pierre Simon Laplace in the early years of the nineteenth century. Laplace had written a monumental and definitive book describing how to compute the future positions of planets and comets on the basis of a few observations from Earth. “I find no mention of God in your treatise, M. Laplace,” Napoleon is reported to have said. “I had no need for that hypothesis,” Laplace replied.

许多人对“无神论的机械宇宙永不停息地运转，无需神灵干预，所有未来事件都由过去事件决定”这一概念感到恐惧。从某种意义上说，十九世纪的浪漫主义运动正是对这种冷酷、精确的推理方式的一种反动。然而，19世纪40年代出现了对这一新兴科学的验证，令大众惊叹不已。牛顿的数学定律被用来预测另一颗行星的存在，而海王星也确实在定律预测的位置被发现。几乎所有对机械宇宙的抵触情绪都随之瓦解，这种哲学立场也成为了大众文化的重要组成部分。

Many people were horrified with the concept of a godless clockwork universe running forever without divine intervention, with all future events determined by events of the past. In some sense, the romantic movement of the nineteenth century was a reaction to this cold, exact use of reasoning. However, a proof of this new science appeared in the 1840s, which dazzled the popular imagination. Newton’s mathematical laws were used to predict the existence of another planet, and the planet Neptune was discovered in the place where these laws predicted it. Almost all resistance to the clockwork universe crumbled, and this philosophical stance became an essential part of popular culture.

然而，如果拉普拉斯的理论不需要上帝的介入，他确实需要他称之为“误差函数”的东西。从地球观测行星和彗星，其观测结果与预测位置并不完全吻合。拉普拉斯和他的同事们将此归因于观测误差，有时是由于地球大气层的扰动，有时是由于人为错误。拉普拉斯将所有这些误差都融入到一个额外的部分（误差函数）中，并将其附加到他的数学描述中。这个误差函数吸收了所有误差，只留下纯粹的运动定律来预测天体的真实位置。人们相信，随着测量精度的不断提高，对误差函数的需求将会减少。由于误差函数用于解释观测值和预测值之间的微小偏差，19世纪早期的科学深受哲学决定论的影响——这种观点认为，宇宙中发生的一切都是由宇宙的初始条件和描述其运动的数学公式预先决定的。

However, if Laplace did not need God in his formulation, he did need something he called the “error function.” The observations of planets and comets from this earthly platform did not fit the predicted positions exactly. Laplace and his fellow scientists attributed this to errors in the observations, sometimes due to perturbations in the earth’s atmosphere, other times due to human error. Laplace threw all these errors into an extra piece (the error function) he tacked onto his mathematical descriptions. This error function sopped them up and left only the pure laws of motion to predict the true positions of celestial bodies. It was believed that, with more and more precise measurements, the need for an error function would diminish. With the error function to account for slight discrepancies between the observed and the predicted, early-nineteenth-century science was in the grip of philosophical determinism—the belief that everything that happens is determined in advance by the initial conditions of the universe and the mathematical formulas that describe its motions.

到了十九世纪末，误差不但没有减少，反而不断增加。随着测量精度的不断提高，误差也越来越多。原本井然有序的宇宙模型摇摇欲坠。人们试图发现生物学和社会学规律的努力均以失败告终。在物理学和化学等传统科学领域，牛顿和拉普拉斯曾经使用的定律也被证明仅仅是粗略的近似值。科学逐渐开始采用一种新的范式——统计模型。到了二十世纪末，几乎所有科学领域都转向了统计模型。

By the end of the nineteenth century, the errors had mounted instead of diminishing. As measurements became more and more precise, more and more error cropped up. The clockwork universe lay in shambles. Attempts to discover the laws of biology and sociology had failed. In the older sciences like physics and chemistry, the laws that Newton and Laplace had used were proving to be only rough approximations. Gradually, science began to work with a new paradigm, the statistical model of reality. By the end of the twentieth century, almost all of science had shifted to using statistical models.

大众文化未能跟上这场科学革命的步伐。一些模糊的概念和表达（例如“相关性”、“概率”和“风险”）已逐渐融入大众词汇，大多数人也意识到医学和经济学等某些科学领域存在不确定性，但鲜有非科学家理解这场革命所引发的深刻哲学观念转变。这些统计模型究竟是什么？它们是如何产生的？它们在现实生活中意味着什么？它们是否真实地描述了现实？本书旨在解答这些问题。同时，我们还将探究一些参与这场革命的男男女女的生平。

Popular culture has failed to keep up with this scientific revolution. Some vague ideas and expressions (like “correlation,” “odds,” and “risk”) have drifted into the popular vocabulary, and most people are aware of the uncertainties associated with some areas of science like medicine and economics, but few nonscientists have any understanding of the profound shift in philosophical view that has occurred. What are these statistical models? How did they come about? What do they mean in real life? Are they true descriptions of reality? This book is an attempt to answer these questions. In the process, we will also look at the lives of some of the men and women who were involved in this revolution.

在探讨这些问题时，有必要区分三个数学概念：随机性、概率和统计。对大多数人来说，随机性只是不可预测性的另一种说法。《塔木德》中的一句格言表达了这种普遍的观念：“人们不应该去寻找埋藏的宝藏，因为埋藏的宝藏是随机出现的，而根据定义，人们不可能去寻找随机出现的东西。”但是，对于现代科学家来说，随机性有很多不同的类型。概率分布的概念（将在本书第二章中介绍）允许我们对这种随机性施加约束，并赋予我们预测未来随机事件的有限能力。因此，对于现代科学家来说，随机事件并非仅仅是狂野、意外和不可预测的。它们具有可以用数学描述的结构。

In dealing with these questions, it is necessary to distinguish among three mathematical ideas: randomness, probability, and statistics. To most people, randomness is just another word for unpredictability. An aphorism from the Talmud conveys this popular notion: “One should not go hunting for buried treasure, because buried treasure is found at random, and, by definition, one cannot go searching for something which is found at random.” But, to the modern scientist, there are many different types of randomness. The concept of a probability distribution (which will be described in chapter 2 of this book) allows us to put constraints on this randomness and gives us a limited ability to predict future but random events. Thus, to the modern scientist, random events are not simply wild, unexpected, and unpredictable. They have a structure that can be described mathematically.

概率是用来描述一个非常古老的概念的现代词汇。它最早出现在亚里士多德的著作中，亚里士多德曾说过：“概率的本质在于，不可能的事情也会发生。” 最初，概率指的是人们对可能发生的事情的预判。在十七、十八世纪，一群数学家，包括伯努利家族（两代人）、费马、棣莫弗和帕斯卡，致力于发展概率的数学理论，他们的研究始于机会游戏。他们发展出一些非常精妙的方法来计算等概率事件。棣莫弗成功地将微积分的方法融入到这些技巧中，而伯努利家族则从中提炼出一些深刻的基本定理，即所谓的“大数定律”。到了十九世纪末，数学概率主要由一些复杂的技巧构成，但缺乏坚实的理论基础。

Probability is the current word for a very ancient concept. It appears in Aristotle, who stated that “It is the nature of probability that improbable things will happen.” Initially, it involved a person’s sense of what might be expected. In the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, a group of mathematicians, among them the Bernoullis, a family of two generations, Fermat, de Moivre, and Pascal, all worked on a mathematical theory of probability, which started with games of chance. They developed some very sophisticated methods for counting equally probable events. de Moivre managed to insert the methods of calculus into these techniques, and the Bernoullis were able to discern some deep fundamental theorems, called “laws of large numbers.” By the end of the nineteenth century, mathematical probability consisted primarily of sophisticated tricks but lacked a solid theoretical foundation.

尽管概率论并不完善，但它在统计分布概念的发展过程中发挥了重要作用。当我们考虑某个具体的科学问题时，就会用到统计分布。例如，1971年，哈佛大学公共卫生学院的一项研究发表在英国医学期刊《柳叶刀》上，该研究探讨了饮用咖啡是否与下尿路癌相关。这项研究报告了一组患者的情况，其中一些人患有下尿路癌，另一些人患有其他疾病。报告的作者还收集了这些患者的其他数据，例如年龄、性别和癌症家族史。并非所有喝咖啡的人都会患上尿路癌，也并非所有患上尿路癌的人都喝咖啡，因此有些结果与他们的假设相悖。然而，25%的尿路癌患者习惯性地每天喝四杯或更多咖啡。而未患癌症的患者中，只有10%的人有如此大的咖啡饮用量。这似乎为该假设提供了一些证据。

In spite of the incomplete nature of probability theory, it proved useful in the developing idea of a statistical distribution. A statistical distribution occurs when we are considering a specific scientific problem. For instance, in 1971, a study from the Harvard School of Public Health was published in the British medical journal Lancet, which examined whether coffee drinking was related to cancer of the lower urinary tract. The study reported on a group of patients, some of whom had cancer of the lower urinary tract and some of whom had other diseases. The authors of the report also collected additional data on these patients, such as age, sex, and family history of cancer. Not everyone who drinks coffee gets urinary tract cancer, and not everyone who gets urinary tract cancer is a coffee drinker, so there are some events that contradict their hypothesis. However, 25 percent of the patients with this cancer habitually drank four or more cups of coffee a day. Only 10 percent of the patients without cancer were such heavy drinkers of coffee. There would seem to be some evidence in favor of the hypothesis.

这组数据为作者提供了一个统计分布。他们运用数学概率的工具，构建了一个描述该分布的理论公式，称为“概率分布函数”，或简称分布函数，并用它来检验问题。它类似于拉普拉斯误差函数，但要复杂得多。理论分布函数的构建运用了概率论，用于描述从同一人群中随机抽取的未来数据可能呈现的分布情况。

This collection of data provided the authors with a statistical distribution. Using the tools of mathematical probability, they constructed a theoretical formula for that distribution, called the “probability distribution function,” or just distribution function, which they used to examine the question. It is like Laplace’s error function, but much more complicated. The construction of the theoretical distribution function makes use of probability theory, and it is used to describe what might be expected from future data taken at random from the same population of people.

本书并非探讨概率和概率论——这些是抽象的数学概念——而是探讨如何将一些概率定理应用于科学问题、统计分布以及分布函数领域。仅凭概率论不足以描述统计方法，而且科学中的统计方法有时会违反某些概率定理。读者会发现概率概念在各章节中时隐时现，在需要时运用，在不需要时则被忽略。

This is not a book about probability and probability theory, which are abstract mathematical concepts. This is a book about the application of some of the theorems of probability to scientific problems, the world of statistical distributions, and distribution functions. Probability theory alone is insufficient to describe statistical methods, and it sometimes happens that statistical methods in science violate some of the theorems of probability. The reader will find probability drifting in and out of the chapters, being used where needed and ignored when not.

由于现实的统计模型本质上是数学模型，因此只有通过数学公式和符号才能完全理解它们。本书旨在尝试做一些不那么雄心勃勃的事情。我试图从参与二十世纪科学统计革命的几位人物（其中许多人仍然健在）的角度来描述这场革命。我只是略微提及了他们的工作，力求让读者了解他们的个人发现如何融入整体图景。

Because statistical models of reality are mathematical ones, they can be fully understood only in terms of mathematical formulas and symbols. This book is an attempt to do something a little less ambitious. I have tried to describe the statistical revolution in twentieth-century science in terms of some of the people (many of them still living) who were involved in that revolution. I have only touched on the work they created, trying to give the reader a taste of how their individual discoveries fit into the overall picture.

本书读者无法学到足以进行科学数据统计分析的知识。那需要数年的研究生学习。但我希望读者能从中理解统计学视角所代表的基本哲学理念的深刻转变。那么，非数学专业人士该如何理解这场科学革命呢？我认为，一个好的起点或许是看一位女士品茶……

The reader of this book will not learn enough to engage in the statistical analysis of scientific data. That would require several years of graduate study. But I hope the reader will come away with some understanding of the profound shift in basic philosophy that is represented by the statistical view of science. So, where does the nonmathematician go to understand this revolution in science? I think that a good place to start is with a lady tasting tea … .

C章​

CHAPTER 1

女士品茶

THE LADY TASTING TEA

我那是二十世纪二十年代末英国剑桥的一个夏日午后。一群大学教授、他们的夫人和一些客人围坐在户外的一张桌子旁享用下午茶。一位女士坚持说，茶的味道取决于茶先倒进牛奶里还是牛奶先倒进茶里。在场的男士们，那些科学精英们对此嗤之以鼻，认为这纯属无稽之谈。这之间有什么区别呢？他们无法想象这两种混合物在化学成分上会有什么不同。这时，一位身材瘦小、戴着厚眼镜、留着开始泛白的范戴克式胡须的男士，突然抓住了这个问题。

It was a summer afternoon in Cambridge, England, in the late 1920s. A group of university dons, their wives, and some guests were sitting around an outdoor table for afternoon tea. One of the women was insisting that tea tasted different depending upon whether the tea was poured into the milk or whether the milk was poured into the tea. The scientific minds among the men scoffed at this as sheer nonsense. What could be the difference? They could not conceive of any difference in the chemistry of the mixtures that could exist. A thin, short man, with thick glasses and a Vandyke beard beginning to turn gray, pounced on the problem.

“让我们来验证一下这个说法，”他兴奋地说。他开始构思一个实验：让那位坚持认为牛奶和茶有区别的女士品尝一系列茶，其中一些茶里加了牛奶，另一些茶里则加了牛奶。

“Let us test the proposition,” he said excitedly. He began to outline an experiment in which the lady who insisted there was a difference would be presented with a sequence of cups of tea, in some of which the milk had been poured into the tea and in others of which the tea had been poured into the milk.

我仿佛已经听到一些读者对这项研究不屑一顾，认为它不过是夏日午后消遣的无聊之作。“这位女士能否分辨出不同的输液有什么意义呢？”他们会这样问。“这个问题毫无重要性，也没有什么科学价值，”他们会嗤之以鼻。“这些伟大的头脑应该把他们渊博的学识用在真正造福人类的事情上。”

I can just hear some of my readers dismissing this effort as a minor bit of summer afternoon fluff. “What difference does it make whether the lady could tell one infusion from another?” they will ask. “There is nothing important or of great scientific merit in this problem,” they will sneer. “These great minds should have been putting their immense brain power to something that would benefit mankind.”

不幸的是，无论非科学家如何看待科学及其重要性，我的经验是，大多数科学家从事研究是因为他们对结果感兴趣，并且能从工作中获得智力上的满足感。优秀的科学家很少会考虑他们工作最终的重要性。那是一个阳光明媚的夏日午后，在剑桥。那位女士对茶的冲泡方法的判断或许正确，或许错误。乐趣在于找到一种方法来验证她的说法是否正确，于是，在一位留着范戴克式胡须的男士的指导下，他们开始讨论如何才能做出判断。

Unfortunately, whatever nonscientists may think about science and its importance, my experience has been that most scientists engage in their research because they are interested in the results and because they get intellectual excitement out of the work. Seldom do good scientists think about the eventual importance of their work. So it was that sunny summer afternoon in Cambridge. The lady might or might not have been correct about the tea infusion. The fun would be in finding a way to determine if she was right, and, under the direction of the man with the Vandyke beard, they began to discuss how they might make that determination.

他们兴致勃勃地和他一起布置实验。几分钟之内，他们就摆弄着各种不同的茶水，让那位女士根本看不清哪个杯子是哪个。然后，那位留着范戴克式胡须的男士带着一种决绝的神情，把第一杯茶递给了她。她抿了一口，说道这是加了牛奶的茶。他默默地听着她的反应，又递上了第二杯……

Enthusiastically, many of them joined with him in setting up the experiment. Within a few minutes, they were pouring different patterns of infusion in a place where the lady could not see which cup was which. Then, with an air of finality, the man with the Vandyke beard presented her with her first cup. She sipped for a minute and declared that it was one where the milk had been poured into the tea. He noted her response without comment and presented her with the second cup … .

科学的合作本质

THE COOPERATIVE NATURE OF SCIENCE

我在20世纪60年代末从一位当天下午也在场的人那里听到了这个故事。他名叫休·史密斯，但他发表科学论文时使用的是H·费尔菲尔德·史密斯这个笔名。我认识他的时候，他是康涅狄格大学斯托尔斯分校的统计学教授。两年前，我从康涅狄格大学获得了统计学博士学位。在宾夕法尼亚大学任教之后，我加入了辉瑞公司（一家大型制药公司）的临床研究部门。辉瑞位于康涅狄格州格罗顿的研究园区距离斯托尔斯大约一个小时车程。在辉瑞，我当时要处理许多棘手的数学问题。那时我是那里唯一的统计学家，我需要和别人讨论这些问题以及我的“解决方案”。

I heard this story in the late 1960s from a man who had been there that afternoon. He was Hugh Smith, but he published his scientific papers under the name H. Fairfield Smith. When I knew him, he was a professor of statistics at the University of Connecticut, in Storrs. I had received my Ph.D. in statistics from the University of Connecticut two years before. After teaching at the University of Pennsylvania, I had joined the clinical research department at Pfizer, Inc., a large pharmaceutical firm. Its research campus in Groton, Connecticut, was about an hour’s drive from Storrs. I was dealing with many difficult mathematical problems at Pfizer. I was the only statistician there at that time, and I needed to talk over these problems and my “solutions” to them.

我在辉瑞工作期间发现，很少有科学研究能够独自完成。它通常需要多人协作。这是因为很容易出错。当我提出一个数学公式来解决问题时，模型有时会不适用，或者我可能对情况做出了错误的假设，或者我找到的“解”可能来自方程的错误分支，甚至我可能在计算中犯了错误。

What I had discovered working at Pfizer was that very little scientific research can be done alone. It usually requires a combination of minds. This is because it is so easy to make mistakes. When I would propose a mathematical formula as a means of solving a problem, the model would sometimes be inappropriate, or I might have introduced an assumption about the situation that was not true, or the “solution” I found might have been derived from the wrong branch of an equation, or I might even have made a mistake in arithmetic.

每当我去斯托尔斯大学和史密斯教授探讨问题，或者和辉瑞公司的化学家或药理学家们坐下来讨论难题时，我提出的问题通常都会受到欢迎。他们总是热情地参与讨论，表现出浓厚的兴趣。大多数科学家对工作的兴趣通常来自于解决问题的兴奋感。他们期待着在研究问题、试图理解问题的过程中与他人交流互动。

Whenever I would visit the university at Storrs to talk things over with Professor Smith, or whenever I would sit around and discuss problems with the chemists or pharmacologists at Pfizer, the problems I brought out would usually be welcomed. They would greet these discussions with enthusiasm and interest. What makes most scientists interested in their work is usually the excitement of working on a problem. They look forward to the interactions with others as they examine a problem and try to understand it.

实验设计

THE DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS

剑桥的那个夏日午后，就发生了这样的事。那位留着范戴克式胡须的男士名叫罗纳德·艾尔默·费舍尔，当时他年近四十。后来，他被授予爵士头衔，成为罗纳德·费舍尔爵士。1935年，他出版了《实验设计》一书，并在该书第二章中描述了这位女士品尝茶的实验。在书中，费舍尔将这位女士及其判断视为一个假设性问题。他探讨了设计实验的各种方法，以确定她是否能够分辨出两种茶的区别。设计这个实验的难点在于，如果只给她一杯茶，即使她无法分辨出两种茶的区别，她也有50%的概率猜对。如果给她两杯茶，她仍然有可能猜对。事实上，如果她知道这两杯茶分别用不同的茶汤冲泡，那么她的一次猜测可能完全正确（也可能完全错误）。

And so it was that summer afternoon in Cambridge. The man with the Vandyke beard was Ronald Aylmer Fisher, who was in his late thirties at the time. He would later be knighted Sir Ronald Fisher. In 1935, he wrote a book entitled The Design of Experiments, and he described the experiment of the lady tasting tea in the second chapter of that book. In his book, Fisher discusses the lady and her belief as a hypothetical problem. He considers the various ways in which an experiment might be designed to determine if she could tell the difference. The problem in designing the experiment is that, if she is given a single cup of tea, she has a 50 percent chance of guessing correctly which infusion was used, even if she cannot tell the difference. If she is given two cups of tea, she still might guess correctly. In fact, if she knew that the two cups of tea were each made with a different infusion, one guess could be completely right (or completely wrong).

同样，即使她能分辨出来，也有可能出错，比如其中一杯茶没有搅拌均匀，或者茶水不够热就冲泡了。即使她能分辨出不同之处，面对十杯茶，她也可能只能正确识别出九杯。

Similarly, even if she could tell the difference, there is some chance that she might have made a mistake, that one of the cups was not mixed as well or that the infusion was made when the tea was not hot enough. She might be presented with a series of ten cups and correctly identify only nine of them, even if she could tell the difference.

在书中，费舍尔探讨了此类实验的各种可能结果。他描述了如何决定应该呈上多少杯茶，以及呈上的顺序，以及如何向女士说明呈上顺序。他还计算了不同结果的概率，取决于女士的判断是否正确。然而，在这些讨论中，他从未提及此类实验是否实际进行过，也没有描述实际实验的结果。

In his book, Fisher discusses the various possible outcomes of such an experiment. He describes how to decide how many cups should be presented and in what order and how much to tell the lady about the order of presentations. He works out the probabilities of different outcomes, depending upon whether the lady is or is not correct. Nowhere in this discussion does he indicate that such an experiment was ever run. Nor does he describe the outcome of an actual experiment.

费舍尔的实验设计著作是20世纪上半叶席卷所有科学领域的革命性变革的重要组成部分。早在费舍尔出现之前，科学实验就已经进行了数百年。16世纪后期，英国医生威廉·哈维就曾以动物为实验对象，阻断不同静脉和动脉的血液流动，试图追踪血液从心脏流向肺部、再返回心脏、流遍全身、最终返回心脏的循环过程。

The book on experimental design by Fisher was an important element in a revolution that swept through all fields of science in the first half of the twentieth century. Long before Fisher came on the scene, scientific experiments had been performed for hundreds of years. In the later part of the sixteenth century, the English physician William Harvey experimented with animals, blocking the flow of blood in different veins and arteries, trying to trace the circulation of blood as it flowed from the heart to the lungs, back to the heart, out to the body, and back to the heart again.

费舍尔并非实验增进知识的先驱。在费舍尔之前，实验方法因科学家而异。优秀的科学家能够设计出产生新知识的实验。而水平稍逊的科学家则常常进行一些积累了大量数据但对增进知识毫无用处的“实验”。例如，19世纪后期人们曾多次尝试测量光速，但都以失败告终。直到美国物理学家阿尔伯特·迈克尔逊利用光和镜子设计了一系列极其精密的实验，人们才首次对光速进行了较为准确的估算。

Fisher did not discover experimentation as a means of increasing knowledge. Until Fisher, experiments were idiosyncratic to each scientist. Good scientists would be able to construct experiments that produced new knowledge. Lesser scientists would often engage in “experimentation” that accumulated much data but was useless for increasing knowledge. An example of this can be seen in the many inconclusive attempts that were made during the late nineteenth century to measure the speed of light. It was not until the American physicist Albert Michelson constructed a highly sophisticated series of experiments with light and mirrors that the first good estimates were made.

十九世纪，科学家很少发表实验结果。他们通常描述结论，并发表一些数据来“证明”这些结论的正确性。格雷戈尔·孟德尔并没有展示他所有豌豆育种实验的结果。他描述了实验的顺序，然后写道：“两组实验的前十个成员可以作为示例……”。（20世纪40年代，罗纳德·费舍尔研究了孟德尔的数据“示例”，发现这些数据好得令人难以置信。它们并没有展现出应有的随机性。）

In the nineteenth century, scientists seldom published the results of their experiments. Instead, they described their conclusions and published data that “demonstrated” the truth of those conclusions. Gregor Mendel did not show the results of all his experiments in breeding peas. He described the sequence of experiments and then wrote: “The first ten members of both series of experiments may serve as an illustration … .” (In the 1940s, Ronald Fisher examined Mendel’s “illustrations” of data and discovered that the data were too good to be true. They did not display the degree of randomness that should have occurred.)

虽然科学是通过仔细思考、观察和实验发展起来的，但如何进行实验却一直不太清楚，实验的完整结果通常也不会呈现给读者。

Although science has been developed from careful thought, observations, and experiments, it was never quite clear how one should go about experimenting, nor were the complete results of experiments usually presented to the reader.

这一点在十九世纪末二十世纪初的农业研究中尤为突出。费舍尔在二十世纪初工作的罗瑟姆斯特德农业实验站，在他到来之前已经对不同的肥料成分（称为“人工肥料”）进行了近九十年的试验。在一个典型的实验中，研究人员会将磷酸盐和氮盐的混合物撒在整块田地上，播种谷物，然后测量收成以及当年夏季的降雨量。为了将某一年或某一块田地的产量与另一块田地或同一块田地在不同年份的产量进行比较，人们使用复杂的公式来“调整”产量。这些公式被称为“肥力指数”，每个农业实验站都有自己的肥力指数，并认为自己的指数比其他任何指数都更准确。

This was particularly true for agricultural research in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Rothamsted Agricultural Experimental Station, where Fisher worked during the early years of the twentieth century, had been experimenting with different fertilizer components (called “artificial manures”) for almost ninety years before he arrived. In a typical experiment, the workers would spread a mixture of phosphate and nitrogen salts over an entire field, plant grain, and measure the size of the harvest, along with the amount of rainfall during that summer. There were elaborate formulas used to “adjust” the output of one year or one field, in order to compare it to the output of another field or of the same field in another year. These were called “fertility indexes,” and each agricultural experimental station had its own fertility index, which it believed was more accurate than any other.

这九十年的实验结果却是一团乱麻，留下了大量未发表且毫无用处的数据。似乎某些小麦品种对某种肥料的反应比其他品种更好，但这种情况只在降雨过多的年份才会出现。其他实验似乎表明，第一年施用硫酸钾，第二年施用硫酸钠，可以提高某些马铃薯品种的产量，但对其他品种则没有效果。对于这些人工肥料，我们最多只能说，它们有时可能有效，或者说，也许有效。

The result of these ninety years of experimentation was a mess of confusion and vast troves of unpublished and useless data. It seemed as if some strains of wheat responded better than other strains to one fertilizer, but only in years when rainfall was excessive. Other experiments seemed to show that sulfate of potash one year, followed by sulfate of soda for the next year, produced an increase in some varieties of potatoes but not others. The most that could be said of these artificial manures was that some of them worked sometimes, perhaps, or maybe.

费舍尔是一位造诣颇深的数学家。他研究了罗瑟姆斯特德农业研究所的农业科学家们用来修正实验结果、以消除年际气候差异的肥力指数。他还考察了其他农业试验站使用的各种指数。当这些指数简化为基本的代数形式时，它们实际上都是同一个公式的不同版本。换句话说，两个支持者激烈争论的指数，实际上都在进行完全相同的修正。1921年，他在顶尖农业期刊《应用生物学年鉴》上发表了一篇论文，指出使用哪个指数都无关紧要。文章还表明，所有这些修正都不足以弥补不同田地肥力的差异。这篇意义非凡的论文终结了长达二十多年的科学争论。

Fisher, a consummate mathematician, looked at the fertility index that the agricultural scientists at Rothamsted used to correct the results of experiments to account for differences due to the weather from year to year. He examined the competing indexes used by other agricultural experimental stations. When reduced to their elemental algebra, they were all versions of the same formula. In other words, two indexes, whose partisans were hotly contending, were really making exactly the same correction. In 1921, he published a paper in the leading agricultural journal, the Annals of Applied Biology, in which he showed that it did not make any difference what index was used. The article also showed that all these corrections were inadequate to adjust for differences in the fertility of different fields. This remarkable paper ended over twenty years of scientific dispute.

费舍尔随后分析了过去九十年的降雨量和作物产量数据，结果表明，年际天气差异的影响远大于不同肥料的影响。用费舍尔后来在其实验设计理论中提出的一个词来说，年际天气差异和年际人工肥料差异是“混淆的”。这意味着，利用这些实验数据根本无法将它们区分开来。九十年的实验和二十多年的科学争论几乎都成了徒劳的努力！

Fisher then examined the data on rainfall and crop production over the previous ninety years and showed that the effects of different weather from year to year were far greater than any effect of different fertilizers. To use a word Fisher developed later in his theory of experimental design, the year-to-year differences in weather and the year-to-year differences in artificial manures were “confounded.” This means that there was no way to pull them apart using data from these experiments. Ninety years of experimentation and over twenty years of scientific dispute had been an almost useless waste of effort!

这促使费舍尔开始思考实验和实验设计。他得出结论：科学家需要从构建潜在实验结果的数学模型开始。数学模型是一组方程，其中一些符号代表将从实验中收集的数据，而另一些符号则代表实验的总体结果。科学家从实验数据出发，计算出与所研究的科学问题相符的结果。

This set Fisher thinking about experiments and experimental design. He concluded that the scientist needs to start with a mathematical model of the outcome of the potential experiment. A mathematical model is a set of equations, in which some of the symbols stand for numbers that will be collected as data from the experiments and other symbols stand for the overall outcomes of the experiment. The scientist starts with the data from the experiment and computes outcomes appropriate to the scientific question being considered.

以一位老师辅导某个学生的经历为例。老师想了解孩子学习的程度。为此，老师做了一个“实验”，给孩子做了一系列测试。每个测试的分数范围是0到100。任何一次测试都无法准确反映孩子的知识水平。孩子可能没有复习测试中涉及的几个知识点，但却掌握了很多其他知识。孩子可能在考试当天头疼，或者早上和父母吵架。由于种种原因，一次测试无法准确评估孩子的知识水平。因此，老师安排了一系列测试。所有测试的平均分被认为是衡量孩子知识水平的更准确指标。孩子掌握了多少知识是结果，而每次测试的分数则是数据。

Consider a simple example from the experience of a teacher with a particular student. The teacher is interested in finding some measure of how much the child has learned. To this end, the teacher “experiments” by giving the child a group of tests. Each test is marked on a scale from 0 to 100. Any one test provides a poor estimate of how much the child knows. It may be that the child did not study the few things that were on that test but knows a great deal about things that were not on the test. The child may have had a headache the day she took a particular test. The child may have had an argument with parents the morning of a particular test. For many reasons, one test does not provide a good estimate of knowledge. So, the teacher gives a set of tests. The average score from all those tests is taken as a better estimate of how much the child knows. How much the child knows is the outcome. The scores on individual tests are the data.

教师应该如何安排这些测试？测试内容应该仅限于过去几天所学的知识点吗？还是应该涵盖迄今为止所学的所有内容？测试应该每周进行一次，还是每天进行一次，还是在每个单元教学结束后进行一次？所有这些都是实验设计中需要考虑的问题。

How should the teacher structure those tests? Should they be a sequence of tests that cover only the material taught over the past couple of days? Should they each involve something from all the material taught until now? Should the tests be given weekly, or daily, or at the end of each unit being taught? All of these are questions involved in the design of the experiment.

当农业科学家想要了解某种人工肥料对小麦生长的影响时，必须设计一个实验来收集数据以评估这种影响。费舍尔指出，实验设计的第一步是建立一组数学方程，描述待收集数据与预期结果之间的关系。然后，任何有用的实验都必须能够评估这些结果。实验必须具体明确，使科学家能够区分结果差异是由天气因素造成的，还是由不同肥料的使用造成的。尤其重要的是，必须将所有要比较的处理方法都纳入同一实验中，这被称为“对照组”。

When the agricultural scientist wants to know the effect of a particular artificial fertilizer on the growth of wheat, an experiment has to be constructed that will provide data to estimate that effect. Fisher showed that the first step in the design of that experiment is to set up a group of mathematical equations describing the relationship between the data that will be collected and the outcomes that are being estimated. Then, any useful experiment has to be one that allows for estimation of those outcomes. The experiment has to be specific and enable the scientist to determine the difference in outcome that is due to weather versus the difference that is due to the use of different fertilizers. In particular, it is necessary to include all the treatments being compared in the same experiment, something that came to be called “controls.”

在《实验设计》一书中，费舍尔列举了一些优秀的实验设计案例，并推导出了一些通用的实验设计规则。然而，费舍尔方法中涉及的数学非常复杂，大多数科学家除非遵循书中已有的设计模式，否则无法自行设计实验。

In his book, The Design of Experiments, Fisher provided a few examples of good experimental designs, and derived general rules for good designs. However, the mathematics involved in Fisher’s methods were very complicated, and most scientists were unable to generate their own designs unless they followed the pattern of one of the designs Fisher derived in his book.

农业科学家们认识到费舍尔在实验设计方面的工作具有巨大价值，费舍尔方法很快便在大多数英语国家的农业院校中占据主导地位。在费舍尔的早期工作基础上，大量的科学文献发展起来，描述了各种不同的实验设计。这些设计已被应用于农业以外的领域，包括医学、化学和工业质量控制。在许多情况下，其中涉及的数学知识既深奥又复杂。但是，我们暂且就此打住，不要以为科学家可以随意“做实验”。这需要长时间的深思熟虑——而且往往需要大量的复杂数学知识。

Agricultural scientists recognized the great value of Fisher’s work on experimental design, and Fisherian methods were soon dominating schools of agriculture in most of the English-speaking world. Taking off from Fisher’s initial work, an entire body of scientific literature has developed to describe different experimental designs. These designs have been applied to fields other than agriculture, including medicine, chemistry, and industrial quality control. In many cases, the mathematics involved are deep and complicated. But, for the moment, let us stop with the idea that the scientist cannot just go off and “experiment.” It takes some long and careful thought—and often a strong dose of difficult mathematics.

那位品尝茶的女士后来怎么样了？费舍尔并没有描述那个阳光明媚的夏日午后在剑桥进行的实验结果。但史密斯教授告诉我，那位女士正确地辨认出了每一杯茶。

And the lady tasting tea, what happened to her? Fisher does not describe the outcome of the experiment that sunny summer afternoon in Cambridge. But Professor Smith told me that the lady identified every single one of the cups correctly.

C章2

CHAPTER 2

偏态分布

THE SKEW DISTRIBUTIONS

一个如同人类思想史上的许多革命一样，统计模型的概念究竟何时融入科学，难以确切考证。我们可以在十九世纪初德国和法国数学家的著作中找到一些具体的例子，甚至在十七世纪伟大的天文学家约翰内斯·开普勒的论文中也能窥见一斑。正如本书序言所述，拉普拉斯发明了他所谓的误差函数，用以解释天文学中的统计问题。但我更倾向于将统计革命的开端追溯到19世纪90年代卡尔·皮尔逊的工作。查尔斯·达尔文认识到生物变异是生命的基本特征，并以此为基础提出了适者生存的理论。然而，正是他的英国同胞卡尔·皮尔逊，率先认识到统计模型的本质，以及它们如何与十九世纪科学的决定论观点相区别。

As with many revolutions in human thought, it is difficult to find the exact moment when the idea of a statistical model became part of science. One can find possible specific examples of it in the work of the German and French mathematicians of the early nineteenth century, and there is even a hint of it in the papers of Johannes Kepler, the great seventeenth-century astronomer. As indicated in the preface to this book, Laplace invented what he called the error function to account for statistical problems in astronomy. I would prefer to date the statistical revolution to the work of Karl Pearson in the 1890s. Charles Darwin recognized biological variation as a fundamental aspect of life and made it the basis of his theory of the survival of the fittest. But it was his fellow Englishman Karl Pearson who first recognized the underlying nature of statistical models and how they offered something different from the deterministic view of nineteenth-century science.

上世纪六十年代我开始学习数理统计时，皮尔逊的名字很少在课堂上被提及。当我与该领域的主要人物交流时，也从未听到过对皮尔逊及其工作的提及。他要么被忽视，要么被视为一个早已过时的次要人物。例如，美国国家标准局的丘吉尔·艾森哈特在卡尔·皮尔逊生命的最后几年就读于伦敦大学学院。他回忆说，皮尔逊是一位意志消沉的老人。统计学研究的飞速发展让他应接不暇，他和他的大部分研究成果都被抛入了历史的尘埃中。伦敦大学学院里那些才华横溢的年轻学子们蜂拥而至，拜师于那些崭新的学术巨匠门下，其中一位正是卡尔·皮尔逊的儿子，但却没有人会去拜访这位远离喧嚣、独自待在办公室里的老人。

When I began the study of mathematical statistics in the 1960s, Pearson was seldom mentioned in my classes. As I met and talked with the major figures in the field, I heard no references to Pearson or his work. He was either ignored or treated as a minor figure whose activities had long since been outmoded. Churchill Eisenhart, from the U.S. National Bureau of Standards, for instance, was studying at University College, London, during the final years of Karl Pearson’s life. He remembered Pearson as a dispirited old man. The pace of statistical research had swept him by, dashing him and most of his work into the dustbin of the past. The bright young students at University College were flocking to study at the feet of the newer great men, one of them Karl Pearson’s own son, but no one was coming to see old Karl in his lonely office far from the bustle of new, exciting research.

并非一直如此。19世纪70年代，年轻的卡尔·皮尔逊（Carl Pearson）离开英国前往德国攻读政治学研究生。在那里，他深深迷恋上了卡尔·马克思的著作。为了向马克思致敬，他将自己的名字拼写改为卡尔（Karl）。他带着政治学博士学位回到伦敦，并撰写了两部颇具声望的著作。在维多利亚时代沉闷压抑的英国，他竟大胆地组织了一个青年男女讨论俱乐部。在这个俱乐部里，年轻男女们（无需监护人）聚集在一起，按照德法上流社会沙龙的模式，男女平等地探讨着世界上的重大政治和哲学问题。皮尔逊正是在这个环境中遇到了他的妻子，这表明他创办这个俱乐部或许另有隐情。这个小小的社交活动让我们得以窥见卡尔·皮尔逊的独创思想以及他对既定传统的全然不屑。

It wasn’t always like this. In the 1870s, young Carl [sic] Pearson had left England to pursue his graduate studies in political science in Germany. There he became enamored of the work of Karl Marx. In tribute to Marx, he changed the spelling of his own first name to Karl. He returned to London with a doctorate in political science, having written two respectable books in the field. In the very heart of stuffy Victorian England, he had the audacity to organize a Young Men’s and Women’s Discussion Club. At the club, young men and women gathered together (unchaperoned), in an equality of the sexes modeled after. the salons of upper-class German and French society. There they discussed the great political and philosophical problems of the world. The fact that Pearson met his wife in this environment suggests that there may have been more than one motive for founding the club. This little social venture provides some insight into Karl Pearson’s original mind and his utter disregard for established tradition.

尽管皮尔逊的博士学位是政治学，但他主要的研究兴趣在于科学哲学和数学建模的本质。19世纪80年代，他出版了《科学的语法》，该书多次再版。在第一次世界大战前的大部分时间里，它被认为是探讨科学和数学本质的伟大著作之一。书中充满了精辟独到的见解，使其成为科学哲学领域的重要作品。此外，该书文笔流畅简洁，通俗易懂，任何人都能轻松阅读。即使不懂数学，也能读懂《科学的语法》。尽管本书出版至今已逾百年，但其中的洞见和思想对21世纪的许多数学研究仍然具有现实意义，并为我们理解科学的本质提供了至今仍然适用的视角。

Although his doctorate was in political science, Pearson’s main interests were in the philosophy of science and the nature of mathematical modeling. In the 1880s, he published The Grammar of Science, which went through a number of editions. For much of the period prior to World War I, this was considered one of the great books on the nature of science and mathematics. It is filled with brilliant, original insights, which make it an important work in the philosophy of science. It was also written in a smooth, simple style that makes it accessible to anyone. You don’t have to know mathematics to read and understand The Grammar of Science. Although, at this writing, the book is over a hundred years old, the insights and the ideas found in it are pertinent to much mathematical research of the twenty-first century and provide an understanding of the nature of science that holds true even today.

卡尔·皮尔逊（1857-1936 年），背景是拉斐尔·韦尔登的半身像。

Karl Pearson, 1857-1936, with a bust of Raphael Weldon in the background.

高尔顿生物识别实验室

THE GALTON BIOMETRICAL LABORATORY

在人生的这个阶段，皮尔逊受到了英国科学家弗朗西斯·高尔顿爵士的影响。大多数人听说过高尔顿，都知道他是指纹的“发现者”。指纹的独特性以及通常用于分类和识别指纹的方法都出自高尔顿之手。指纹的独特性在于指纹中不规则的痕迹和切口，这些痕迹和切口被称为“高尔顿印记”。高尔顿的贡献远不止于此。他家境富裕，是一位业余科学家，致力于通过研究数字模式，将数学的严谨性引入生物学。他最早的研究之一是天才的遗传。他收集了一些据称智力超群的父子的信息。然而，他发现这个问题非常棘手，因为当时还没有有效的智力测量方法。于是，他决定研究一些更容易测量的特征的遗传，例如身高。

At this point in his life, Pearson fell under the influence of the English scientist Sir Francis Galton. Most people who have heard of Galton know him as the “discoverer” of fingerprints. The realization that fingerprints are unique to each individual and the methods usually used to classify and identify them are Galton’s. The uniqueness of fingerprints lies in the occurrence of irregular marks and cuts in the finger patterns, which are called “Galton Marks.” Galton did far more. Independently wealthy, he was a dilettante scientist who sought to bring mathematical rigor into the science of biology through the study of patterns of numbers. One of his first investigations involved the inheritance of genius. He collected information about pairs of fathers and sons who were reputed to be highly intelligent. He found the problem very difficult, however, because there was no good measure of intelligence at the time. He decided to look at the inheritance of traits that were more easily measured, like height.

高尔顿在伦敦建立了一个生物测量实验室（bio代表生物学，metric代表测量），并刊登广告邀请家庭前来测量。在生物测量实验室里，他收集了家庭成员的身高、体重、特定骨骼的尺寸以及其他特征。他和他的助手们整理了这些数据，并反复检查。他试图找到一种方法，通过父母的身高来预测子女的身高。例如，很明显，个子高的父母往往会生出个子高的孩子，但是是否存在某种数学公式，仅凭父母的身高就能预测孩子的身高呢？

Galton set up a biometrical laboratory (bio for biology, metric for measurement) in London and advertised for families to come and be measured. At the biometrical laboratory, he collected heights, weights, measurements of specific bones, and other characteristics of family members. He and his assistants tabulated these data and examined and reexamined them. He was looking for some way to predict measures from parents to children. It was obvious, for instance, that tall parents tended to have tall children, but was there some mathematical formula that would predict how tall the children would be, using only the heights of the parents?

相关与回归

CORRELATION AND REGRESSION

就这样，高尔顿发现了一种他称之为“均值回归”的现象。结果表明，父亲个子很高的儿子往往比父亲矮，而父亲个子很矮的儿子往往比父亲高。仿佛某种神秘的力量使人类的身高偏离极端值，趋向于所有人的平均值。均值回归现象不仅适用于人类身高，几乎所有科学观察都受到均值回归的影响。我们将在第五章和第七章看到，费舍尔是如何将高尔顿的均值回归理论转化为统计模型，而这些模型如今主导着经济学、医学研究和许多工程领域。

In this way, Galton discovered a phenomenon he called “regression to the mean.” It turned out that sons of very tall fathers tended to be shorter than their fathers and sons of very short fathers tended to be taller than their fathers. It was as if some mysterious force were causing human heights to move away from the extremes and toward the mean or average of all humans. The phenomenon of regression to the mean holds for more than human heights. Almost all scientific observations are bedeviled by regression to the mean. We shall see in chapters 5 and 7 how R. A. Fisher was able to turn Galton’s regression to the mean into statistical models that now dominate economics, medical research, and much of engineering.

高尔顿仔细思考了他惊人的发现，然后意识到这必然是正确的，而且在他进行所有观察之前就应该可以预测到。他设想，假设均值回归没有发生。那么，平均而言，高个父亲的儿子会和他们的父亲一样高。在这种情况下，一些儿子必然会比他们的父亲更高（为了平衡那些较矮儿子的身高）。这一代高个子的儿子们的身高又会趋于平均，因此一些儿子会更高。这种情况会代代相传。同样地，也会有一些儿子比他们的父亲矮，一些孙子甚至更矮，以此类推。经过不多的几代之后，人类的种族构成将呈现两极分化：一端是越来越高的人，另一端是越来越矮的人。

Galton thought about his remarkable finding and then realized that it had to be true, that it could have been predicted before making all his observations. Suppose, he said, that regression to the mean did not occur. Then, on the average, the sons of tall fathers would be as tall as their fathers. In this case, some of the sons would have to be taller than their fathers (in order to average out the ones who are shorter). The sons of this generation of taller men would then average their heights, so some sons would be even taller. It would go on, generation after generation. Similarly, there would be some sons shorter than their fathers, and some grandsons even shorter, and so on. After not too many generations, the human race would consist of ever taller people at one end and ever shorter ones at the other.

这种情况不会发生。人类的平均身高往往保持稳定。只有当身材高大的父亲的儿子平均身高较矮，而身材矮小的父亲的儿子平均身高较高时，这种现象才有可能发生。均值回归是一种维持物种稳定性的现象，它使特定物种在世代之间保持基本“相同”。

This does not happen. The heights of humans tend to remain stable, on the average. This can only happen if the sons of very tall fathers average shorter heights and the sons of very short fathers average greater heights. Regression to the mean is a phenomenon that maintains stability and keeps a given species pretty much the “same” from generation to generation.

高尔顿发现了一种衡量这种关系的数学方法，他称之为“相关系数”。高尔顿给出了一个具体的公式，可以根据他在生物统计实验室收集的数据类型来计算这个数值。这是一个非常具体的公式，用于衡量均值回归的某个方面，但它对这种现象的成因却没有任何解释。高尔顿最初就是以这种含义使用“相关性”一词的。此后，“相关性”一词逐渐被大众所接受。如今，“相关性”一词的含义往往比高尔顿所定义的特定相关系数要模糊得多。它听起来很科学，非专业人士也常常随意使用这个词，仿佛它能描述两个事物之间的关系。但是，除非你指的是高尔顿的数学方法，否则使用“相关性”这个词（高尔顿最初就是出于这个特定目的而使用这个词的）就显得不够精确或科学。

Galton discovered a mathematical measure of this relationship. He called it the “coefficient of correlation.” Galton gave a specific formula for computing this number from the type of data he collected at the biometrical laboratory. It is a highly specific formula for measuring one aspect of regression to the mean, but tells us nothing whatsoever about the cause of that phenomenon. Galton first used the word correlation in this sense. It has since moved into popular language. Correlation is often used to mean something much more vague than Galton’s specific coefficient of correlation. It has a scientific ring to its sound, and nonscientists often bandy the word around as if it described how two things are related. But unless you are referring to Galton’s mathematical measure, you are not being very precise or scientific if you use the word correlation, which Galton used for this specific purpose.

分布和参数

DISTRIBUTIONS AND PARAMETERS

凭借相关性公式，高尔顿已经非常接近于这一革命性的新思想，这一思想将在二十世纪改变几乎所有科学领域。但正是他的学生卡尔·皮尔逊，第一个将这一思想以最完整的形式表达出来。

With the formula for correlation, Galton was getting very close to this new revolutionary idea that was to modify almost all science in the twentieth century. But it was his disciple, Karl Pearson, who first formulated the idea in its most complete form.

要理解这个革命性的想法，你必须抛开所有关于科学的先入之见。我们通常被教导，科学就是测量。我们进行精确的测量，并用这些测量结果来寻找描述自然的数学公式。在高中物理课上，我们学习到物体自由落体运动的距离与时间的关系可以用一个包含符号 g 的公式来表示，其中 g 是加速度常数。我们还学习到可以通过实验来确定 g 的值。然而，当高中生进行一系列实验来确定 g 的值时，比如让小砝码沿着斜面滚动，并测量它们到达斜面上不同位置所需的时间，结果会怎样呢？很少能得到正确的结果。学生进行实验的次数越多，就越感到困惑，因为不同的实验会得出不同的 g 值。老师以高人一等的姿态俯视着学生，并向他们保证，他们得不到正确答案并不是因为他们粗心大意、粗心大意或者抄错了数据。

To understand this revolutionary idea, you have to cast aside all preconceived notions about science. Science, we are often taught, is measurement. We make careful measurements and use them to find mathematical formulas that describe nature. In high school physics, we are taught that the distance a falling body will travel versus time is given by a formula involving a symbol g, where g is the constant of acceleration. We are taught that experiments can be used to determine the value of g. Yet, when the high school student runs a sequence of experiments to determine the value of g, rolling small weights along an inclined plane and measuring how long it takes them to get to different places on the ramp, what happens? It seldom comes out right. The more times the student runs the experiment, the more confusion occurs, as different values of g emerge. from different experiments. The teacher looks down from his superior knowledge and assures the students that they are not getting the right answer not because they are sloppy or being careless or have copied incorrect numbers.

他没有告诉他们的是，所有实验都存在误差，即使是最严谨的科学家也很少能得到完全正确的结果。每个实验都会出现一些意想不到的、难以察觉的小故障。比如，房间里的空气可能太热，导致滑动的砝码在开始滑动前卡住一微秒。一只飞过的蝴蝶吹来的微风也可能产生影响。实验的真正结果是一系列分散的数据，其中没有一个是正确的，但所有这些数据都可以用来估算出一个接近正确值的结果。

What he does not tell them is that all experiments are sloppy and that very seldom does even the most careful scientist get the number right. Little unforeseen and unobservable glitches occur in every experiment. The air in the room might be too warm and the sliding weight might stick for a microsecond before it begins to slide. A slight breeze from a passing butterfly might have an effect. What one really gets out of an experiment is a scatter of numbers, not one of which is right but all of which can be used to get a close estimate of the correct value.

有了皮尔逊的革命性思想，我们不再将实验结果视为独立存在的精确测量数字。相反，它们只是数字分散的一个例子，或者用更通俗的说法，是数字分布。这种数字分布可以用数学公式表示，该公式告诉我们观测到的数字取特定值的概率。在特定的实验中，这个数字实际取什么值是无法预测的。我们只能讨论数值的概率，而不能讨论数值的确定性。从某种意义上说，单个实验的结果是随机的，因为它们是不可预测的。然而，分布的统计模型使我们能够描述这种随机性的数学本质。

Armed with Pearson’s revolutionary idea, we do not look upon experimental results as carefully measured numbers in their own right. Instead, they are examples of a scatter of numbers, a distribution of numbers, to use the more accepted term. This distribution of numbers can be written as a mathematical formula that tells us the probability that an observed number will be a given value. What value that number actually takes in a specific experiment is unpredictable. We can only talk about probabilities of values and not about certainties of values. The results of individual experiments are random, in the sense that they are unpredictable. The statistical models of distributions, however, enable us to describe the mathematical nature of that randomness.

科学界花了很长时间才意识到观测结果固有的随机性。在十八、十九世纪，天文学家和物理学家创建了数学公式，以可接受的精度描述他们的观测结果。由于测量仪器的基本不精确性，观测值与预测值之间的偏差是预料之中的，因此被忽略了。行星和其他天体被认为遵循由基本运动方程确定的精确路径。不确定性是由于仪器的缺陷造成的，而不是自然界固有的。

It took some time for science to realize the inherent randomness of observations. In the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, astronomers and physicists created mathematical formulas that described their observations to a degree of accuracy that was acceptable. Deviations between observed and predicted values were expected because of the basic imprecision of the measuring instruments, and were ignored. Planets and other astronomical bodies were assumed to follow precise paths determined by the fundamental equations of motion. Uncertainty was due to poor instrumentation. It was not inherent in nature.

随着物理学中测量仪器的日益精密发展，以及人们尝试将测量科学扩展到生物学和社会学领域，自然界固有的随机性变得越来越清晰。该如何处理这种随机性呢？一种方法是保留精确的数学公式，并将观测值与预测值之间的偏差视为微小的、无关紧要的误差。事实上，早在1820年，拉普拉斯的数学论文就描述了第一个概率分布——误差分布，即与这些微小误差相关的概率的数学表达式。这种误差分布后来被人们熟知为“钟形曲线”或正态分布。[1]

With the development of ever more precise measuring instruments in physics, and with attempts to extend this science of measurement to biology and sociology, the inherent randomness of nature became more and more clear. How could this be handled? One way was to keep the precise mathematical formulas and treat the deviations between the observed values and the predicted values as a small, unimportant error. In fact, as early as 1820, mathematical papers by Laplace describe the first probability distribution, the error distribution, that is a mathematical formulation of the probabilities associated with these small, unimportant errors. This error distribution has entered popular parlance as the “bell-shaped curve,” or the normal distribution.[1]

皮尔逊超越了正态分布或误差分布的范畴。他审视生物学中积累的数据，认为测量本身，而非测量误差，才具有概率分布。我们所测量的任何事物实际上都是随机散布的一部分，其概率由一个数学函数——分布函数——来描述。皮尔逊发现了一类分布函数，他称之为“偏态分布”，并声称这类分布函数可以描述科学家在数据中可能观察到的任何类型的散布。该分布族中的每个分布都由四个数字标识。

It took Pearson to go one step beyond the normal, or error, distribution. Looking at the data accumulated in biology, Pearson conceived of the measurements themselves, rather than errors in the measurement, as having a probability distribution. Whatever we measure is really part of a random scatter, whose probabilities are described by a mathematical function, the distribution function. Pearson discovered a family of distribution functions that he called the “skew distributions” and that, he claimed, would describe any type of scatter a scientist might see in data. Each of the distributions in this family is identified by four numbers.

用于描述分布函数的数值与测量值并非同一类型的“数值”。这些数值无法直接观测，只能根据测量值的离散程度推断得出。这些数值后来被称为参数——源自希腊语，意为“近似测量值”。能够完整描述皮尔逊分布系统中一个成员的四个参数被称为

The numbers that identify the distribution function are not the same type of “number” as the measurements. These numbers can never be observed but can be inferred from the way in which the measurements scatter. These numbers were later to be called parameters—from the Greek for “almost measurements.” The four parameters that completely describe a member of the Pearson System are called

  1. 均值——测量值围绕其分散的中心值，
  2. the mean—the central value about which the measurements scatter,
  3. 标准差——大多数测量值围绕平均值的离散程度，
  4. the standard deviation—how far most of the measurements scatter about the mean,
  5. 对称性——测量值仅在平均值一侧堆积的程度，
  6. symmetry—the degree to which the measurements pile up on only one side of the mean,
  7. 峰度——稀有测量值与平均值的偏离程度。
  8. kurtosis—how far rare measurements scatter from the mean.

皮尔逊的偏态分布系统带来了一种微妙的思维转变。在皮尔逊之前，科学研究的“事物”是真实可触的。开普勒试图发现描述行星在太空中运动的数学规律。威廉·哈维的实验试图确定血液如何在特定动物的静脉和动脉中流动。化学研究元素以及由元素构成的化合物。然而，开普勒试图驯服的“行星”实际上是一组数字，它们标识了地球上观测者所看到的闪烁光点在天空中的位置。一匹马的血液在血管中的流动路径与另一匹马或同一个人的血液流动路径可能有所不同。尽管人们知道铁是一种元素，但没有人能够制备出纯净的铁样本。

There is a subtle shift in thinking with Pearson’s system of skew distributions. Before Pearson, the “things” that science dealt with were real and palpable. Kepler attempted to discover the mathematical laws that described how the planets moved in space. William Harvey’s experiments tried to determine how blood moved through the veins and arteries of a specific animal. Chemistry dealt with elements and compounds made up of elements. However, the “planets” that Kepler tried to tame were really a set of numbers identifying the positions in the sky where shimmering lights were seen by observers on earth. The exact course of blood through the veins of a single horse was different from what might have been seen with a different horse, or with a specific human being. No one was able to produce a pure sample of iron, although it was known to be an element.

皮尔逊提出，这些可观察的现象仅仅是随机的反映：真实存在的是概率分布。科学的真正“对象”并非我们能够观察和掌控的事物，而是描述我们所能观察事物的随机性的数学函数。分布的四个参数才是我们在科学研究中真正想要确定的。在某种意义上，我们永远无法真正确定这四个参数。我们只能根据数据对其进行估计。

Pearson proposed that these observable phenomena were only random reflections: What was real was the probability distribution. The real “things” of science were not things that we could observe and hold but mathematical functions that described the randomness of what we could observe. The four parameters of a distribution are what we really want to determine in a scientific investigation. In some sense, we can never really determine those four parameters. We can only estimate them from the data.

皮尔逊未能认识到这最后一点区别。他认为，只要收集到足够的数据，参数的估计值就能提供参数的真实值。直到他的年轻竞争对手罗纳德·费舍尔指出，皮尔逊的许多估计方法并非最优。20世纪30年代末，卡尔·皮尔逊步入晚年之际，一位才华横溢的波兰年轻数学家耶日·内曼证明，皮尔逊的偏态分布体系并不能涵盖所有可能的分布，许多重要问题无法用皮尔逊体系解决。

Pearson failed to recognize this last distinction. He believed that if we collected enough data the estimates of the parameters would provide us with true values of the parameters. It took his younger rival, Ronald Fisher, to show that many of Pearson’s methods of estimation were less than optimal. In the late 1930s, as Karl Pearson was approaching the end of his long life, a brilliant young Polish mathematician, Jerzy Neyman, showed that Pearson’s system of skew distributions did not cover the universe of possible distributions and that many important problems could not be solved using the Pearson system.

但我们暂且放下1934年那个年迈体衰、被遗弃的卡尔·皮尔逊，回到那个精力充沛、年近四十、对偏态分布的发现充满热情的年轻人。1897年，他接管了高尔顿位于伦敦的生物统计实验室，并招募了大批年轻女性（被称为“计算员”）来计算与高尔顿积累的人体测量数据相关的分布参数。在新世纪之交，高尔顿、皮尔逊和拉斐尔·韦尔登联手创办了一份新的科学期刊，旨在将皮尔逊的理论应用于生物数据。高尔顿用自己的财富设立了一个信托基金来支持这份新期刊。在创刊号上，编辑们提出了一个雄心勃勃的计划。

But let us leave the old, abandoned Karl Pearson of 1934 and return to the vigorous man in his late thirties, who was filled with enthusiasm over his discovery of skew distributions. Ln 1897, he took over Galton’s biometrical laboratory in London and marshaled legions of young women (called “calculators”) to compute the parameters of distributions associated with the data Galton had been accumulating on human measurements. At the turn of the new century, Galton, Pearson, and Raphael Weldon combined their efforts to found a new scientific journal that would apply Pearson’s ideas to biological data. Galton used his wealth to create a trust fund that would support this new journal. In the first issue, the editors set forth an ambitious plan.

生物统计学计划

THE PLAN OF BIOMETRIKA

高尔顿、皮尔逊和韦尔登是英国一批杰出科学家中的一员，他们致力于研究其中最杰出的成员之一——查尔斯·达尔文的洞见。达尔文的进化论假定生命形式会因环境压力而发生改变。他提出，不断变化的环境会使那些更适应新环境的随机变异略占优势。随着环境的持续变化和生命形式的不断随机突变，最终会涌现出更适应新环境生存和繁衍的新物种。这一理论被简称为“适者生存”。然而，当一些傲慢的政治学家将其应用于社会生活时，却对社会产生了不幸的影响。他们宣称，在经济财富争夺战中获胜的人比那些陷入贫困的人更“适者生存”。“适者生存”成为资本主义猖獗的辩护理由，富人被赋予了凌驾于穷人之上的道德权威。

Galton, Pearson, and Weldon were part of an exciting cadre of British scientists who were exploiting the insights of one of their most prominent members, Charles Darwin. Darwin’s theories of evolution postulated that life forms change in response to environmental stress. He proposed that changing environments gave a slight advantage to those random changes that fit better into the new environment. Gradually, as the environment changed and life forms continued to have random mutations, a new species would emerge that was better fit to live and procreate in the new environment. This idea was given the shorthand designation “survival of the fittest.” It had an unfortunate effect on society when arrogant political scientists adapted it to social life, declaring that those who emerged triumphant from the economic battle over riches were more fit than those who plunged into poverty. Survival of the fittest became a justification for rampant capitalism in which the rich were given the moral authority to ignore the poor.

在生物科学领域，达尔文的观点似乎很有道理。他指出，相关物种之间的相似性表明，现代物种是由一个更古老的物种演化而来。达尔文证明，生活在孤立岛屿上的小型鸟类，即使物种略有不同，也拥有许多共同的解剖结构。他还指出，不同物种的胚胎之间存在相似之处，包括人类胚胎，其发育初期就长有尾巴。

In the biological sciences, Darwin’s ideas seemed to have great validity. Darwin could point to the resemblances among related species as suggesting a previous species out of which these modern ones had emerged. Darwin showed how small birds of slightly different species and living on isolated islands had many anatomical commonalities. He pointed to the similarities among embryos of different species, including the human embryo, which starts with a tail.

达尔文唯一未能证明的是，在人类历史的尺度上，究竟是否存在新物种出现的例子。他假设新物种的出现是适者生存的结果，但这一假设并没有证据。他所能展示的，仅仅是那些看似“适应”其环境的现代物种。达尔文的理论似乎能够解释已知的现象，而且其逻辑结构也颇具吸引力。但是，正如一句古老的意第绪语谚语所说：“举例并不能证明什么。”

The one thing Darwin was unable to show was an example of a new species actually emerging within the time frame of human history. Darwin postulated that new species emerge because of the survival of the fittest, but there was no proof of this. All he had to display were modern species that appeared to “fit” well within their environment. Darwin’s proposals seemed to account for what was known, and they had an attractive logical structure to them. But, to translate an old Yiddish expression, “For instance is no proof.”

皮尔逊、高尔顿和韦尔登在他们的新期刊上着手纠正这一观点。在皮尔逊看来，现实是概率分布，因此达尔文雀（他在书中举的一个重要例子）并非科学研究的对象。研究对象是某一物种所有雀类喙长的随机分布。如果能够测量某一物种所有雀类的喙长，那么这些喙长的分布函数将有四个参数，而这四个参数就是该物种的喙长分布。

Pearson, Galton, and Weldon set out in their new journal to rectify this. In Pearson’s view of reality as probability distributions, Darwin’s finches (an important example he used in his book) were not the objects of scientific investigation. The random distribution of all finches of a species was the object. If one could measure the beak lengths of all the finches in a given species, the distribution function of those beak lengths would have its own four parameters, and those four parameters would be the beak length of the species.

皮尔逊说，假设存在某种环境力量，它通过赋予物种比某些特定随机突变更高的存活率来改变该物种。我们或许无法活到亲眼目睹新物种出现，但我们或许能够观察到分布的四个参数的变化。在创刊号中，三位编辑宣布，他们的新期刊将收集来自世界各地的数据，并确定其分布的参数，最终希望能够展示与环境变化相关的参数变化实例。

Suppose, Pearson said, that there was an environmental force changing a given species by providing superior survivorship to certain specific random mutations. We might not be able to live long enough to see a new species emerge, but we might be able to see a change in the four parameters of the distribution. In their first issue, the three editors declared that their new journal would collect data from all over the world and determine the parameters of their distributions, with the eventual hope of showing examples of shifts in parameters associated with environmental change.

他们将新期刊命名为《生物统计学》（Biometrika）。该期刊由高尔顿设立的生物统计学信托基金慷慨资助，资金雄厚，是第一份刊登全彩照片和带有精美图画的折叠玻璃纸页的期刊。期刊采用优质棉纸印刷，并刊登最复杂的数学公式，即使这意味着极其复杂且昂贵的排版。

They named their new journal Biometrika. It was funded lavishly by the Biometrika Trust that Galton set up, and was so well funded that it was the first journal to publish full color photographs and foldout glassine sheets with intricate drawings. It was printed on high-quality rag paper, and the most complicated mathematical formulas were displayed, even if they meant extremely complicated and expensive typesetting.

在接下来的二十五年里，《生物统计学》杂志刊登了来自通讯员的数据，他们深入非洲丛林测量当地土著的胫骨和腓骨；寄来在中美洲雨林捕获的珍稀热带鸟类的喙长；或者挖掘古代墓地，找到人类头骨，并在其中灌入霰弹以测量颅容量。1910年，该杂志刊登了几张全彩照片，照片上是俾格米男子的疲软阴茎平放在平面上，用测量尺进行测量。

For the next twenty-five years, Biometrika printed data from correspondents who plunged into the jungles of Africa to measure tibia and fibula of the natives; sent in beak lengths of exotic tropical birds caught in the rain forests of Central America; or raided ancient cemeteries to uncover human skulls, into which they poured buckshot to measure cranial capacity. In 1910, the journal published several sheets of full color photographs of flaccid penises of pygmy men, laid on a flat surface against measuring sticks.

1921年，一位名叫朱莉娅·贝尔的年轻女记者描述了她为阿尔巴尼亚军队新兵测量人体尺寸时遇到的种种困难。她离开维也纳前往阿尔巴尼亚一个偏远的前哨站，确信那里会有会说德语的军官帮助她。然而，当她到达时，却只有一名只会说三个德语单词的士官。她毫不气馁，拿出她的青铜测量杆，通过挠痒痒的方式让这些年轻人明白她的意图，直到他们按照她的要求抬起胳膊或腿。

In 1921, a young female correspondent, Julia Bell, described the troubles she underwent when she tried to get anthropomorphic measurements of recruits for the Albanian army. She left Vienna for a remote outpost in Albania, assured that she would find German-speaking officers to help her. When she arrived, there was only a sergeant, who spoke three words of German. Undaunted, she took out her bronze measuring rods and got the young men to understand what she wanted by tickling them until they lifted their arms or legs as she desired.

对于每一组数据，皮尔逊都用计算器计算出了分布的四个参数。文章会以图形方式展示最佳拟合分布，并对该分布与其他相关数据分布的差异进行一些评述。现在回想起来，很难看出所有这些活动是如何帮助证明达尔文的理论的。阅读这些《生物统计学》期刊后，我感觉这项工作很快就变成了纯粹为了研究而研究，除了估计给定数据集的参数之外，并没有其他实际目的。

For each of these data sets, Pearson and his calculators computed the four parameters of the distributions. The articles would display a graphical version of the best-fitting distribution and some comments about how this distribution differed from the distribution of other related data. In retrospect, it is difficult to see how all this activity helped prove Darwin’s theories. Reading through these issues of Biometrika, I get the impression that it soon became an effort that was done for its own sake and had no real purpose other than estimating parameters for a given set of data.

期刊中还散布着其他文章。其中一些涉及理论数学，探讨概率分布发展过程中出现的问题。例如，1908年，一位笔名为“学生”（Student）的匿名作者发表了一篇论文，其成果——“学生”t检验——在几乎所有现代科学工作中都发挥了作用。我们将在后面的章节中介绍这位匿名作者，并讨论他在卡尔·皮尔逊和罗纳德·费舍尔之间扮演的不幸的调解角色。

Scattered throughout the journal are other articles. Some of them involve theoretical mathematics dealing with problems that arise from the development of probability distributions. In 1908, for instance, an unknown author, publishing under the pseudonym of “Student,” produced a result that plays a role in almost all modern scientific work, “Student”’s t-test. We will meet this anonymous author in later chapters and discuss his unfortunate role in mediating between Karl Pearson and Ronald Fisher.

高尔顿于1911年去世，韦尔登则在此之前死于阿尔卑斯山的一次滑雪事故。这使得皮尔逊成为《生物统计学》杂志的唯一编辑，也是信托基金的唯一分配人。在接下来的二十年里，《生物统计学》成了皮尔逊的个人日记，刊登他认为重要的内容，不刊登他认为不重要的内容。杂志上充斥着皮尔逊撰写的社论，他在这些社论中尽情发挥其丰富的想象力，探讨各种各样的问题。在翻修一座古老的爱尔兰教堂时，人们在墙壁中发现了骨头，皮尔逊运用复杂的数学推理和对这些骨头的测量，来确定它们是否真的是某位中世纪圣人的遗骸。人们发现了一个据称是奥利弗·克伦威尔的头骨。皮尔逊在一篇引人入胜的文章中对此进行了调查，文章描述了克伦威尔遗体的已知下落，然后将克伦威尔画像上的测量数据与头骨上的测量数据进行了比较。[2]在其他文章中，皮尔逊研究了古罗马国王的统治时长和贵族阶级的衰落，并涉足社会学、政治学和植物学等领域，所有这些都带有复杂的数学色彩。

Galton died in 1911, and Weldon had died in a skiing accident in the Alps before that. This left Pearson as the sole editor of Biometrika and the sole dispenser of the trust’s money. In the next twenty years, it was Pearson’s personal journal, which published what Pearson thought was important and did not publish what Pearson thought was unimportant. It was filled with editorials written by Pearson, in which he let his fertile imagination range over all sorts of issues. Renovation of an ancient Irish church uncovered bones in the walls, and Pearson used involved mathematical reasoning and measurements made on those bones to determine whether they were, in fact, the bones of a particular medieval saint. A skull was found that was purported to be the skull of Oliver Cromwell. Pearson investigated this in a fascinating article that described the known fate of Cromwell’s body, and then compared measurements made on pictures painted of Cromwell to measurements made on the skull.[2] In other articles, Pearson examined the lengths of reigns of kings and the decline of the patrician class in ancient Rome, and made other forays into sociology, political science, and botany, all of them with a complicated mathematical gloss.

卡尔·皮尔逊临终前发表了一篇题为《论犹太人与非犹太人的关系》的短文，文中他分析了来自世界各地的犹太人和非犹太人的人体测量数据。他得出结论：纳粹（即国家社会主义者）的种族理论纯属无稽之谈，根本不存在所谓的犹太种族，也不存在所谓的雅利安种族。这篇遗作延续了他以往作品清晰、逻辑严密、论证严谨的风格。

Just before his death, Karl Pearson published a short article entitled “On Jewish-Gentile Relationships,” in which he analyzed anthropomorphic data on Jews and Gentiles from various parts of the world. He concluded that the racial theories of the National Socialists, the official name of the Nazis, were sheer nonsense, that there was no such thing as a Jewish race or, for that matter, an Aryan race. This final paper was well within the clear, logical, carefully reasoned tradition of his previous work.

皮尔逊运用数学探索了人类思想的诸多领域，而这些领域鲜有人会将其视为科学的常规范畴。阅读他在《生物统计学》（Biometrika）杂志上发表的社论，你会发现他兴趣广泛，且拥有令人叹服的能力，能够直击任何问题的核心，并找到相应的数学模型来解决问题。同时，阅读他的社论，你也会发现他是一位意志坚定、观点鲜明的人，他将下属和学生视为自己意志的延伸。我想，如果不必与他意见相左，我一定会很乐意与卡尔·皮尔逊共度一天。

Pearson used mathematics to investigate many areas of human thought that few would consider the normal business of science. To read through his editorials in Biometrika is to meet a man with a universal range of interests and a fascinating capacity to cut to the heart of any problem and find a mathematical model with which to attack it. To read through his editorials is also to meet a strong-willed, highly opinionated man, who viewed subordinates and students as extensions of his own will. I think I would have enjoyed spending a day with Karl Pearson—provided I did not have to disagree with him.

他们是否证明了达尔文的适者生存进化论？或许是的。通过比较古代墓地出土头骨与现代男女的颅容量分布，他们成功地证明了人类物种在数千年来一直保持着惊人的稳定性。他们发现，对土著居民进行的人体测量与对欧洲人的测量结果具有相同的分布，从而驳斥了一些澳大利亚人关于土著居民并非人类的说法。基于这项研究，皮尔逊开发了一种名为“拟合优度检验”的基本统计工具，它是现代科学不可或缺的工具。该检验使科学家能够确定一组给定的观测数据是否符合特定的数学分布函数。在第十章中，我们将看到皮尔逊的儿子是如何利用这种拟合优度检验来推翻他父亲的大部分成就的。

Did they prove Darwin’s theory of evolution through survival of the fittest? Perhaps they did. By comparing the distributions of cranial capacity from skulls in ancient cemeteries to those of modern men and women, they managed to show that the human species has been remarkably stable across many thousands of years. By showing that anthropomorphic measurements on aborigines had the same distribution as measurements taken on Europeans, they disproved the claims of some Australians that the aborigines were not human. Out of this work, Pearson developed a basic statistical tool known as the “goodness of fit test,” which is an indispensable tool for modern science. It enables the scientist to determine whether a given set of observations is appropriate to a particular mathematical distribution function. In chapter 10, we shall see how Pearson’s own son used this goodness of fit test to undermine much of what his father had accomplished.

随着二十世纪的推进，《生物统计学》（Biometrika）杂志上越来越多的文章探讨数理统计的理论问题，而涉及具体数据分布的文章则越来越少。当卡尔·皮尔逊的儿子埃贡·皮尔逊接任主编后，杂志彻底转向了理论数学领域。如今，《生物统计学》已成为该领域首屈一指的期刊。

As the twentieth century advanced, more and more of the articles in Biometrika dealt with theoretical problems in mathematical statistics and fewer dealt with distributions of specific data. When Karl Pearson’s son, Egon Pearson, took over as editor, the shift to theoretical mathematics was complete, and today Biometrika is a preeminent journal in that field.

但它们真的证明了适者生存吗？最接近这一结论的实验发生在二十世纪初。拉斐尔·韦尔登构思了一个宏大的实验。十八世纪，英格兰南部瓷器厂的发展导致一些河流淤积了黏土，普利茅斯和达特茅斯的港口也因此发生了变化，内陆地区的淤积比靠近海岸的地区更为严重。韦尔登从这些港口采集了数百只螃蟹，并将它们分别放入玻璃罐中。一半的罐子里装的是来自内港的淤积水，另一半罐子里装的是来自外港的清澈水。一段时间后，他测量了存活下来的螃蟹的甲壳大小，并确定了两种螃蟹分布情况的参数：一种是在清澈水中存活的螃蟹，另一种是在淤积水中存活的螃蟹。

But did they prove survival of the fittest? The closest they came to it occurred early in the twentieth century. Raphael Weldon conceived of a grand experiment. The development of china factories in southern England in the eighteenth century had caused some of the rivers to become silted with clay, so the harbors of Plymouth and Dartmouth had changed, with the interior regions more silted than those closer to the sea. Weldon took several hundred crabs from these harbors and put them into individual glass jars. In half the jars he used the silted water from the inner harbors. In the other half of the jars he used clearer water from the outer harbors. He then measured the carapaces of the crabs that survived after a period of time and determined the parameters of the two distributions of crabs: those that survived in clear water and those that survived in silted water.

正如达尔文所预言的那样，在淤泥罐中幸存下来的螃蟹的分布参数发生了变化！这是否证明了进化论？可惜的是，韦尔登在完成实验报告之前就去世了。皮尔逊在一份初步数据分析报告中描述了实验及其结果，但最终的分析却从未进行。为实验提供资金的英国政府要求提交最终报告，但最终报告杳无音信。韦尔登去世了，实验也随之终止。

Just as Darwin had predicted, the crabs that survived in the silted jars showed a change in distribution parameters! Did this prove the theories of evolution? Unfortunately, Weldon died before he could write up the results of his experiment. Pearson described the experiment and its results in a preliminary analysis of the data, but a final analysis was never run. The British government, which had supplied the funds for the experiment, demanded a final report. It never came. Weldon was dead, and the experiment was ended.

最终，达尔文的理论被证实适用于细菌和果蝇等短寿命物种。利用这些物种，科学家可以在短时间内进行数千代的实验。现代对DNA（遗传的基本组成单元）的研究，为物种间的关系提供了更有力的证据。如果我们假设过去一千万年甚至更长时间里突变率保持不变，那么DNA研究就可以用来估算灵长类动物和其他哺乳动物物种出现的时间。至少，这个时间跨度可达数十万年。现在大多数科学家都接受达尔文的进化机制。没有任何其他理论机制能够如此完美地契合所有已知数据。科学界对此感到满意，那种认为需要确定分布参数的变化才能证明短时间尺度上进化的观点已被摒弃。

Eventually, Darwin’s theories were shown to be true for short-lived species like bacteria and fruit flies. Using these species, the scientist could experiment with thousands of generations in a short interval of time. Modern investigations of DNA, the building blocks of heredity, have provided even stronger evidence of the relationships among species. If we assume that the rate of mutation has been constant over the past ten million or more years, studies of DNA can be used to estimate the time frame of species emergence for primates and other mammals. At a minimum, it runs into the hundreds of thousands of years. Most scientists now accept Darwin’s mechanism of evolution as correct. No other theoretical mechanism has been proposed that matches all known data so well. Science is satisfied, and the idea that one needs to determine the shift in distribution parameters to show evolution on a short time scale has been dropped.

皮尔逊革命的遗存在于这样一种理念：科学的“对象”并非可观测的现象，而是描述与观测结果相关的概率的数学分布函数。如今，医学研究运用精妙的数学分布模型来确定治疗对长期生存的潜在影响。社会学家和经济学家运用数学分布来描述人类社会的行为。物理学家在量子力学中运用数学分布来描述亚原子粒子。科学的各个方面都受到了这场革命的影响。一些科学家声称，概率分布的使用只是权宜之计，最终我们将找到回归十九世纪科学决定论的方法。爱因斯坦那句著名的“我不相信全能的上帝会掷骰子决定宇宙的运行”便是这种观点的例证。另一些人则认为，自然本质上是随机的，唯一的现实存在于分布函数之中。无论一个人的基本哲学是什么，事实仍然是，皮尔逊关于分布函数和参数的思想主导了二十世纪的科学，并在二十一世纪的门槛上取得了胜利。

What remains of the Pearsonian revolution is the idea that the “things” of science are not the observables but the mathematical distribution functions that describe the probabilities associated with observations. Today, medical investigations use subtle mathematical models of distributions to determine the possible effects of treatments on long-term survival. Sociologists and economists use mathematical distributions to describe the behavior of human society. In the form of quantum mechanics, physicists use mathematical distributions to describe subatomic particles. No aspect of science has escaped the revolution. Some scientists claim that the use of probability distributions is a temporary stopgap and that, eventually, we will be able to find a way to return to the determinism of nineteenth-century science. Einstein’s famous dictum that he did not believe that the Almighty plays dice with the universe is an example of that view. Others believe that nature is fundamentally random and that the only reality lies in distribution functions. Regardless of one’s underlying philosophy, the fact remains that Pearson’s ideas about distribution functions and parameters came to dominate twentieth-century science and stand triumphant on the threshold of the twenty-first century.

C章3

CHAPTER 3

那位亲爱的戈塞特先生

THAT DEAR MR. GOSSET

T位于爱尔兰都柏林的吉尼斯啤酒公司，这家历史悠久、声名显赫的企业，在二十世纪伊始便对科学进行了投资。年轻的吉尼斯勋爵刚刚继承了这家企业，他决定从牛津大学和剑桥大学聘请顶尖的化学毕业生，将现代科学技术引入公司。1899年，他招募了威廉·西利·戈塞特，戈塞特当时年仅23岁，刚刚从牛津大学毕业，获得了化学和数学的双学位。戈塞特的数学背景在当时属于传统范畴，包括微积分、微分方程、天文学以及其他一些关于宇宙运行规律的科学理论。卡尔·皮尔逊的创新以及量子力学的雏形尚未被纳入大学课程。戈塞特被聘用是因为他的化学专长。一家啤酒厂需要一位数学家做什么呢？

That ancient and honorable firm, the Guinness Brewing Company of Dublin, Ireland, began the twentieth century with an investment in science. Young Lord Guinness had recently inherited the enterprise, and he decided to introduce modern scientific techniques into the business by hiring the leading graduates in chemistry from Oxford and Cambridge Universities. In 1899, he recruited William Sealy Gosset, who had just graduated from Oxford at age twenty-three with a combined degree in chemistry and mathematics. Gosset’s mathematical background was a traditional one of the time, including calculus, differential equations, astronomy, and other aspects of the clockwork universe view of science. The innovations of Karl Pearson and the first glimmerings of what was to become quantum mechanics had not yet made their way into the university curriculum. Gosset had been hired tor his expertise in chemistry. What use would a brewery have for a mathematician?

事实证明，戈塞特对吉尼斯来说是一笔不错的投资。他展现出了卓越的管理才能，最终在公司步步高升，掌管了整个大伦敦地区的业务。实际上，他最初对啤酒酿造艺术做出重大贡献，正是因为他是一位数学家。几年前，丹麦电话公司是最早聘请数学家的工业企业之一，但他们面临的显然是一个数学问题：电话交换机的交换机应该建多大。那么，在啤酒和麦芽酒的酿造过程中，又有哪件事需要解决数学问题呢？

Gosset turned out to be a good investment for Guinness. He showed himself to be a very able administrator, and he eventually rose in the company to be in charge of its entire Greater London operations. It was, in fact, as a mathematician that he made his first major contribution to the art of brewing beer. A few years before, the Danish telephone company was one of the first industrial companies to hire a mathematician, but they had a clearly mathematical problem: how big to make the switchboard of a telephone exchange. Where in the making of beer and ale would there be a mathematical problem to be solved?

戈塞特于1904年发表的第一篇论文就探讨了这个问题。在准备发酵醪时，需要使用精确计量的酵母。酵母是活的微生物，在加入醪之前，需要将酵母培养物放入装有液体的罐子中，使其保持活性并不断繁殖。为了确定所需的液体量，工人们必须测量特定罐子中酵母的含量。他们抽取液体样本，在显微镜下观察，并计数酵母细胞的数量。这种测量方法的准确性如何？了解这一点至关重要，因为醪中使用的酵母量必须严格控制。酵母量过少会导致发酵不完全，而过多则会导致啤酒发苦。

Gosset’s first published paper, in 1904, deals with such a problem. When the mash was prepared for fermentation, a carefully measured amount of yeast was used. Yeast are living organisms, and cultures of yeast were kept alive and multiplying in jars of fluid before being put into the mash. The workers had to measure how much yeast was in a given jar in order to determine how much fluid to use. They drew a sample of the fluid and examined it under a microscope, counting the number of yeast cells they saw. How accurate was that measure? It was important to know, because the amount of yeast used in the mash had to be carefully controlled. Too little led to incomplete fermentation. Too much led to bitter beer.

注意这与皮尔逊的科学方法多么契合。测量的是样本中酵母细胞的数量，但真正想要了解的“东西”是整个容器中酵母细胞的浓度。由于酵母是活的，细胞不断增殖分裂，这个“东西”实际上并不存在。在某种意义上，真正存在的是单位体积内酵母细胞的概率分布。戈塞特分析了数据，并确定酵母细胞的数量可以用一种称为“泊松分布”的概率分布来建模。[3]这并非皮尔逊提出的偏态分布族中的一员。事实上，它是一种特殊的分布，只有一个参数（而不是四个）。

Note how this matches Pearson’s approach to science. The measurement was the count of yeast cells in the sample, but the real “thing” that was sought was the concentration of yeast cells in the entire jar. Since the yeast was alive, and cells were constantly multiplying and dividing, this “thing” did not really exist. What did exist in some sense was the probability distribution of yeast cells per unit volume. Gosset examined the data and determined that the counts of yeast cells could be modeled with a probability distribution known as the “Poisson distribution.”[3] This was not one of Pearson’s family of skew distributions. In fact, it is a peculiar distribution that has only one parameter (instead of four).

戈塞特确定样品中活酵母细胞的数量服从泊松分布后，制定了一系列规则和测量方法，从而能够更准确地评估酵母细胞的浓度。吉尼斯啤酒厂运用戈塞特的方法，生产出了品质更加稳定的产品。

Having determined that the number of live yeast cells in a sample follows a Poisson distribution, Gosset was able to devise rules and methods of measurement that led to much more accurate assessments of the concentration of yeast cells. Using Gosset’s methods, Guinness was able to produce a much more consistent product.

“学生”一词的诞生

THE BIRTH OF “STUDENT”

戈塞特想把这个结果发表在合适的期刊上。泊松分布（或其公式）已经为人所知一百多年了，过去也曾有人尝试在现实生活中寻找它的例证。其中一项尝试是统计普鲁士军队中死于马蹄踢伤的士兵人数。戈塞特的酵母细胞计数实验就提供了一个清晰的例证，同时也展现了统计分布这一新概念的重要应用。然而，公司规定不允许员工发表论文。几年前，吉尼斯的一位酿酒大师曾撰写文章，泄露了他们酿造工艺的秘密成分。为了避免公司再次遭受如此宝贵的财产损失，吉尼斯禁止员工发表任何论文。

Gosset wanted to publish this result in an appropriate journal. The Poisson distribution (or the formula for it) had been known for over 100 years, and attempts had been made in the past to find examples of it in real life. One such attempt involved counting the number of soldiers in the Prussian Army who died from horse kicks. In his yeast cell counts, Gosset had a clear example, along with an important application of the new idea of statistical distributions. However, it was against company policy to allow publications by its employees. A few years before, a master brewer from Guinness had written an article in which he revealed the secret components of one of their brewing processes. To avoid the further loss of such valuable company property, Guinness had forbidden its employees from publishing.

戈塞特与当时《生物统计学》（Biometrika）杂志的编辑之一卡尔·皮尔逊结下了友谊，皮尔逊对戈塞特卓越的数学才能印象深刻。1906年，戈塞特说服了他的雇主，这些新的数学思想对一家啤酒公司很有用，于是他请了一年假，前往高尔顿生物统计实验室跟随皮尔逊学习。在此两年前，当戈塞特描述他关于酵母的研究成果时，皮尔逊就迫不及待地想将其发表在自己的期刊上。他们决定用笔名发表这篇文章。戈塞特的这项最初发现，正是以一位只署名为“学生”的作者发表的。

Gosset had become friendly with Karl Pearson, one of the editors of Biometrika at the time, and Pearson was impressed with Gosset’s great mathematical ability. In 1906, Gosset convinced his employers that the new mathematical ideas were useful for a beer company and took a one-year leave of absence to study under Pearson at the Galton Biometrical Laboratory. Two years before this, when Gosset described his results dealing with yeast, Pearson was eager to print it in his journal. They decided to publish the article using a pseudonym. This first discovery of Gosset’s was published by an author identified only as “Student.”

在接下来的三十年里，“学生”撰写了一系列极其重要的论文，几乎全部发表在《生物统计学》（Biometrika）杂志上。后来，吉尼斯家族发现他们“亲爱的戈塞特先生”一直在秘密撰写和发表科学论文，这违反了公司规定。“学生”的大部分数学活动都是在正常工作时间之外的家中进行的，而他在公司里不断晋升到更重要的职位，也表明戈塞特的业余工作对吉尼斯公司并非毫无益处。坊间流传着这样一个故事：吉尼斯家族第一次听说这项工作，是在1937年戈塞特因心脏病突发去世之后，他的数学界朋友们找到吉尼斯公司，希望公司能资助将他的论文集结成册出版。无论此事是否属实，从美国统计学家哈罗德·霍特林的回忆录中可以清楚地看出，在20世纪30年代末，霍特林曾想与“学生”会面，并安排了秘密会面，整个过程颇具间谍活动的神秘色彩。这表明，吉尼斯公司当时对“学生”的真实身份仍然一无所知。“学生”发表在《生物统计学》杂志上的论文，正处于理论与应用的交汇点。戈塞特从高度实际的问题入手，构建复杂的数学模型，最终又将解决方案应用于实际，而这些解决方案后来被其他人所效仿。

Over the next thirty years, “Student” wrote a series of extremely important papers, almost all of them printed in Biometrika. At some point, the Guinness family discovered that their “dear Mr. Gosset” had been secretly writing and publishing scientific papers, contrary to company policy. Most of “Student”’s mathematical activity took place at home, after normal working hours, and his rise in the company to ever more responsible positions shows that the Guinness company was not ill-served by Gosset’s extracurricular work.
