Karl Pearson’s son, Egon Pearson, was named chairman of the diminished Biometric Department, was in charge of the Galton Biometrical Laboratory, and was editor of Biometrika. Fisher and young Pearson did not have a good personal relationship. This was entirely Fisher’s fault. He treated Egon Pearson with obvious hostility. This gentle man suffered from Fisher’s dislike of his father and from Fisher’s further dislike of Jerzy Neyman, whose collaboration with Egon Pearson will be described in chapter 10. However, young Pearson was most respectful of and had high regard for Fisher’s work. In later years, he wrote that he had long since gotten used to Fisher’s never having mentioned his name in print. In spite of these tensions and some jurisdictional disputes between the two departments, Fisher and Egon Pearson sent students to each other’s lectures and refrained from public disputes.

当时，学生们都称卡尔·皮尔逊为“老头子”。他被分配了一名研究生助教，并获准拥有一间办公室，但他的办公室位于一栋距离两个系和生物统计实验室所在建筑较远的楼里。丘吉尔·艾森哈特从美国来到伦敦，跟随费舍尔和埃贡·皮尔逊学习一年。他很想去拜访卡尔·皮尔逊，但他的同学和老师们都劝阻了他。他们问，谁会想见卡尔·皮尔逊呢？他能为费舍尔那才华横溢、灵感迸发的新思想和新方法带来什么呢？遗憾的是，艾森哈特在伦敦期间始终没能去拜访卡尔·皮尔逊。不到一年，皮尔逊就去世了。

Karl Pearson, at this time referred to by students as “the old man,” was given a single graduate assistant and allowed to maintain an office, but his office was situated in a building a distance from the one that housed the two departments and the Biometrical Laboratory. Churchill Eisenhart, who had come from America to study for a year with Fisher and Egon Pearson, wanted to go over to meet Karl Pearson, but his fellow students and members of the faculty discouraged him. Why, they asked, would anyone want to see Karl Pearson? What did he have to offer to the exciting new ideas and methods that were flowing from the prolific mind of R. A. Fisher? To his regret, Eisenhart never did visit Karl Pearson during his stay in London. Pearson would be dead within the year.

费舍尔统计学观点与皮尔逊统计学观点

THE FISHERIAN VERSUS THE PEARSONIAN VIEW OF STATISTICS

卡尔·皮尔逊和费舍尔在分布理论上的分歧在于哲学层面。皮尔逊认为统计分布描述的是他实际要分析的数据集合。而费舍尔则认为，真实的分布是一个抽象的数学公式，收集到的数据只能用于估计真实分布的参数。由于所有此类估计都存在误差，费舍尔提出了一些分析工具，旨在最大限度地减少误差，或者比其他任何工具更频繁地得出更接近真实值的答案。在20世纪30年代，费舍尔似乎赢得了这场争论。然而，到了20世纪70年代，皮尔逊的观点又重新兴起。时至今日，统计学界在这个问题上仍然存在分歧，尽管皮尔逊本人恐怕很难认同他那些学术继承者的论点。费舍尔清晰的数学思维扫除了皮尔逊思想中许多模糊不清的障碍，使他得以看清其观点的本质。因此，后来对皮尔逊理论的复兴都必须基于费舍尔的理论工作。本书中，我计划在多处探讨这些哲学问题，因为将统计模型应用于现实存在一些严重的问题。这里就是其中一处。

A philosophical difference separated Karl Pearson’s approach to distributions from that of Fisher’s. Karl Pearson viewed statistical distributions as describing the actual collections of data he would analyze. According to Fisher, the true distribution is an abstract mathematical formula, and the data collected can be used only to estimate the parameters of the true distribution. Since all such estimates will include error, Fisher proposed tools of analysis that would minimize the degree of such error or that would produce answers that are closer to the truth more often than any other tool. In the 1930s, Fisher appeared to have won the argument. In the 1970s, the Pearsonian view made a comeback. As of this writing, the statistical community is divided over this question, though Pearson would hardly recognize the arguments of his intellectual heirs. Fisher’s clear mathematical mind had swept away much of the debris of confusion that kept Pearson from seeing the underlying nature of his views, and later revivals of Pearson’s approach have to deal with Fisher’s theoretical work. At several places in this book, I plan to look at these philosophical questions, because there are some serious problems with the application of statistical models to reality. This is such a place.

皮尔逊将测量值的分布视为一个真实存在的事物。在他的方法中，针对特定情况存在一个庞大但有限的测量值集合。理想情况下，科学家会收集所有这些测量值并确定其分布参数。如果无法收集所有测量值，则应收集一个非常大且具有代表性的子集。从这个具有代表性的子集中计算出的参数将与整个测量值集合的参数相同。此外，用于计算整个测量值集合参数值的数学方法也可以应用于该代表性子集，从而在计算参数时不会出现严重的误差。

Pearson viewed the distribution of measurements as a real thing. In his approach, there was a large but finite collection of measurements for a given situation. Ideally, the scientist would collect all of these measurements and determine the parameters of their distribution. If all of them could not be collected, then one collected a very large and representative subset of them. The parameters computed from that large, representative subset will be the same as the parameters of the entire collection. Furthermore, the mathematical methods used to compute the values of the parameters for the entire collection can be applied to the representative subset to calculate the parameters without serious error.

在费舍尔看来，这些测量值是从所有可能的测量值集合中随机抽取的。因此，基于这种随机抽取对参数进行的任何估计本身也是随机的，并且具有概率分布。为了将这一概念与潜在参数的概念区分开来，费舍尔将这种估计称为“统计量”。现代术语通常称之为“估计量”。假设我们有两种方法来推导估计给定参数的统计量。例如，一位老师想要确定学生掌握了多少知识（参数），于是布置了一组测试（测量值），并计算平均值（统计量）。那么，是取中位数作为统计量“更好”？还是取测试中最高分和最低分的平均值“更好”？又或者，去掉最高分和最低分，只取剩余测试的平均值“更好”？

To Fisher, the measurements were a random selection from the set of all possible measurements. As a result, any estimate of a parameter based upon that random selection would, itself, be random and have a probability distribution. To keep this idea clear from the idea of an underlying parameter, Fisher called this estimate a “statistic.” Modern terminology often calls it an “estimator.” Suppose we have two methods of deriving a statistic that estimates a given parameter. For instance, the teacher who wishes to determine how much knowledge a pupil has (the parameter) gives a group of tests (the measurements) and takes the average (the statistic). Would it be “better” to take the median as the statistic, or would it be “better” to take the average of the highest and lowest marks in the group of tests, or would it be “better” still to leave off the highest and lowest marks and use the average of the remaining tests?

由于统计量是随机的，讨论其单个值的准确性毫无意义。这与讨论单个测量值并询问其准确性毫无意义的原因相同。我们需要的是一个取决于统计量概率分布的标准，正如皮尔逊所提出的，一组测​​量值必须根据其概率分布而非单个观测值来评估。费舍尔提出了几个衡量良好统计量的标准：

Since the statistic is random, it makes no sense to talk about how accurate a single value of it is. This is the same reason it makes no sense to talk about a single measurement and ask how accurate it is. What is needed is a criterion that depends upon the probability distribution of the statistic, just as Pearson proposed that measurements in a set have to be evaluated in terms of their probability distribution and not their individual observed values. Fisher proposed several criteria of a good statistic:

一致性：数据越多，计算出的统计量越接近参数的真实值。

无偏性：如果您在不同的数据集上多次使用某个统计量，则该统计量的平均值应该接近参数的真实值。

统计量的效率值不会完全等于参数的真实值，但估计参数的大量统计量的大部分应该不会与真实值相差太远。

Consistency The more data you have, the greater the probability that the statistic you calculate is close to the true value of the parameter.

Unbiasedness If you use a particular statistic many times on different sets of data, the average of those values of the statistic should come close to the true value of the parameter.

Efficiency Values of the statistic will not be exactly equal to the true value of the parameter, but the bulk of a large number of statistics that estimate a parameter should not be too far from the true value.

这些描述可能有些含糊，因为我试图将具体的数学公式翻译成通俗易懂的语言。实际上，可以通过适当运用数学方法来评估费舍尔准则。

These descriptions are a little vague, because I have tried to translate the specific mathematical formulations into plain English. In practice, Fisher’s criteria can be evaluated with an appropriate use of mathematics.

费舍尔之后的统计学家提出了其他准则。费舍尔本人也在后来的著作中提出了一些次要准则。抛开所有这些准则的繁杂，关键在于要将统计量本身视为随机的，并且好的统计量应具有良好的概率性质。我们永远无法确切知道某个统计量对于特定数据集的值是否正确。我们只能说，我们使用的统计方法能够产生符合这些准则的统计量。

Statisticians after Fisher have proposed other criteria. Fisher, himself, proposed some secondary criteria in later work. Cutting away the confusion of all these criteria, the important element is that one considers the statistic to be itself random and that good statistics have good probabilistic properties. Never will we know if the value of a statistic for a particular set of data is correct. We can only say we used a procedure that produces a statistic that meets these criteria.

在费舍尔提出的三个基本标准中，无偏性标准最受公众关注。这可能是因为“偏倚”一词带有某些令人难以接受的意味。似乎没有人愿意接受有偏的统计数据。美国食品药品监督管理局的官方指南告诫人们应该使用“避免偏倚”的方法。一种非常奇特的分析方法（将在第27章详细讨论），即“意向性治疗分析”，已经在许多医学试验中占据主导地位，因为这种方法可以保证结果的无偏性，即使它忽略了疗效标准。

Of the three fundamental criteria Fisher proposed, the criterion of unbiasedness has caught the public imagination. This is probably because the word bias has certain unacceptable overtones. A statistic that is biased seems to be something no one wants. Official guidelines from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration admonish that methods should be used that “avoid bias.” A very strange method of analysis (which will be discussed in detail in chapter 27), called “intent to treat,” has come to dominate many medical trials because this method guarantees the result will be unbiased, even though it ignores the criterion of efficiency.

事实上，带有偏见的统计数据往往被有效地利用。例如，根据费舍尔的一些研究成果，目前用于确定市政供水净化所需氯浓度的标准方法就依赖于一种带有偏见（但却稳定有效）的统计数据。这一切都揭示了科学社会学的一个层面：一个原本旨在清晰定义概念的词语，是如何将自身的情感包袱带入科学领域，并影响人们的行为的。

In fact, biased statistics are often used with great effectiveness. Following some of Fisher’s work, the standard method for determining the concentration of chlorine needed to purify a municipal water supply depends upon a biased (but consistent and efficient) statistic. All of this is some sort of lesson in the sociology of science, how a word created to clearly define a concept has brought its emotional baggage into the science and influenced what people do.

费舍尔最大似然法

FISHER’S MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD METHODS

在推导数学公式的过程中，费舍尔意识到卡尔·皮尔逊用来计算其分布参数的方法所产生的统计量未必一致，而且常常存在偏差，还有更有效的统计方法。为了得到一致且有效（但不一定是无偏的）的统计量，费舍尔提出了他称之为“最大似然估计量”（MLE）的方法。

As he worked out the mathematics, Fisher realized that the methods Karl Pearson had been using to compute the parameters of his distributions produced statistics that were not necessarily consistent and were often biased, and that there were much more efficient statistics available. To produce consistent and efficient (but not necessarily unbiased) statistics, Fisher proposed something he called the “maximum likelihood estimator” (MLE).

费舍尔随后证明，最大似然估计始终是一致的，并且（如果允许一些被称为“正则性条件”的假设）它是所有统计量中最有效的。此外，他还证明，如果最大似然估计存在偏差，则可以计算出偏差并从最大似然估计中减去该偏差，从而得到一个修正后的统计量，该统计量是一致的、有效的且无偏的。[9]

Fisher then proved that the MLE was always consistent and that (if one allows a few assumptions known as “regularity conditions”) it was the most efficient of all statistics. Furthermore, he proved that, if the MLE is biased, the bias can be calculated and subtracted from the MLE, making a modified statistic that is consistent, efficient, and unbiased.[9]

费舍尔似然函数迅速风靡数理统计界，并很快成为参数估计的主要方法。然而，最大似然估计存在一个问题：求解最大似然估计值所面临的数学难题极其艰巨。费舍尔的论文中充斥着一行又一行复杂的代数运算，展示了不同分布下最大似然估计值的推导过程。他提出的方差分析和协方差分析算法堪称卓越的数学成就，他巧妙地运用多维空间中的代换和变换，推导出能够提供所需最大似然估计值的公式。

Fisher’s likelihood function swept through the mathematical statistics community and rapidly became the major method for estimating parameters. There was only one problem with maximum likelihood estimation. The mathematical problems posed in attempting to solve for the MLEs were formidable. Fisher’s papers are filled with line after line of complicated algebra showing the derivation of the MLE for different distributions. His algorithms for analysis of variance and analysis of covariance are magnificent mathematical accomplishments, where he managed to make use of clever substitutions and transformations in multidimensional space to produce formulas that gave the user the MLEs that were needed.

尽管费舍尔的理论非常巧妙，但大多数情况下，最大似然估计（MLE）的计算对潜在用户而言仍然面临着难以处理的数学难题。二十世纪后半叶的统计文献中有很多非常精妙的文章，它们利用数学的简化方法，在某些情况下获得了MLE的良好近似值。在我自己的博士论文（大约在1966年）中，我不得不接受一个仅在数据量非常大的情况下才有效的解决方案。假设我拥有大量数据，我才能将似然函数简化到足以计算MLE近似值的程度。

In spite of Fisher’s ingenuity, the majority of situations presented intractable mathematics to the potential user of the MLE. The statistical literature of the last half of the twentieth century contains many very clever articles that make use of simplifications of the mathematics to get good approximations of the MLE for certain cases. In my own doctoral thesis (circa 1966), I had to settle for a solution to my problem that was good only if there was a very large amount of data. Assuming that I had a large amount of data enabled me to simplify the likelihood function to a point where I could compute an approximate MLE.

然后计算机出现了。计算机并非人脑的竞争对手。计算机只是一个庞大而耐心的数字运算器。它不会感到厌倦，也不会犯困或出错。它会一遍又一遍地重复同样的繁重计算——重复数百万次——它可以通过被称为“迭代算法”的方法来找到最大似然估计值。

Then came the computer. The computer is not a competitor for the human brain. The computer is just a big, patient number cruncher. It doesn’t get bored. It doesn’t get sleepy and make a mistake. It will do the same taxing calculation over and over and over and over—for several million “overs”—again. It can find MLEs by methods known as “iterative algorithms.”

迭代算法

ITERATIVE ALGORITHMS

最早的迭代数学方法之一似乎出现在文艺复兴时期（尽管大卫·史密斯在其1923年出版的《数学史》中声称在早期的埃及和中国文献中发现了这种方法的例子）。在资本主义萌芽时期，意大利北部正在组建的银行或会计机构面临着一个基本问题。每个小城邦或国家都有自己的货币。会计机构必须能够计算出，例如，一批木材以127威尼斯杜卡特的价格购入，如果汇率是1杜卡特兑换14德拉克马，那么它应该值多少钱。如今，我们拥有代数符号来解决这个问题。还记得高中代数吗？如果x等于以德拉克马为单位的价值，那么……

One of the earliest iterative mathematical methods seems to have emerged during the Renaissance (although David Smith, in his 1923 History of Mathematics, claims to have found examples of this method in early Egyptian and Chinese records). The banking companies or counting houses being organized in northern Italy during the first glimmerings of capitalism had a basic problem. Each little city-state or country had its own currency. The counting house had to be able to figure out how to convert the value of, say, a load of lumber, which had been bought for 127 Venetian ducats, to what it should be worth in Athenian drachma, if the exchange rate was 14 drachma to the ducat. Nowadays, we have the power of algebraic notation to get a solution. Remember high school algebra? If x equals the value in drachmas, then …

尽管当时数学家们已经开始发展代数，但这种便捷的计算方式对大多数人来说却遥不可及。银行家们使用一种名为“假位法则”的计算方法。每家银行​​都有自己版本的假位法则，并秘密传授给职员，因为每家银行都认为自己的版本才是“最佳”的。十六世纪英国数学家罗伯特·雷科德在推广新的代数符号方面发挥了重要作用。为了对比代数的强大功能和假位法则，他在1542年出版的《艺术之根源》（The Grovnd of Artes）一书中提供了以下版本的假位法则：

Though mathematicians were beginning to develop algebra at that time, this ease of computation was not available to most people. The bankers used a calculation method called the “rule of false position.” Each counting house had its own version of the rule, which was taught to its clerks under a veil of secrecy, because each counting house believed that its version of the rule was “best.” Robert Recorde, a sixteenth-century English mathematician, was prominent in popularizing the new algebraic notation. To contrast the power of algebra to the rule of false position, he provides the following version of the rule of false position in The Grovnd of Artes, a book he wrote in 1542:

盖西（Gesse）以幸福的方式领导了这项工作。

通过机会证明真相，你可以继续前进。

首先，请根据问题进行思考。

虽然其中没有任何真相。

Suche falsehode is so good a grounde,

真理很快就会被揭示。

从许多鱼饵到更多，

从少数到少数也采取少数。

太多的离子对太少，

少者添加到多数人。

在十字路口乘以相反的种类，

所有真相都由虚假的寻找。

Gesse at this woorke as happe doth leade.

By chaunce to truthe you may procede.

And firste woorke by the question,

Although no truthe therein be don.

Suche falsehode is so good a grounde,

That truthe by it will soone be founde.

From many bate to many more,

From to fewe take to fewe also.

With to much ioyne to fewe againe,

To to fewe adde to manye plaine.

In crossewaies multiplye contrary kinde,

All truthe by falsehode for to fynde.

罗伯特·雷科德（Robert Recorde）十六世纪的英语著作指出，首先要猜测一个答案，然后将其应用于问题。使用这个猜测得到的结果与你想要的结果之间会存在差异。利用这个差异，你可以做出一个更准确的猜测。应用这个猜测，你会得到一个新的差异，从而产生另一个猜测。如果你能够巧妙地计算出这个差异，那么经过一系列猜测，最终就能得到正确答案。对于错误位置规则，只需要一次迭代。第二次猜测总是正确的。而对于费舍尔最大似然法，可能需要成千上万次甚至数百万次的迭代才能得到一个好的答案。

What Robert Recorde’s sixteenth-century English says is that you first guess the answer and apply it to the problem. There will be a discrepancy between the result of using this guess and the result you want. You take that discrepancy and use it to produce a better guess. You apply this guess and get a new discrepancy, producing another guess. If you are clever in the way you compute the discrepancy, the sequence of guesses will eventually come to the correct answer. For the rule of false position, it takes only one iteration. The second guess is always correct. For Fisher’s maximum likelihood, it might take thousands or even millions of iterations before you get a good answer.

对一台耐心的电脑来说，区区一百万次迭代又算得了什么呢？在当今世界，这不过是眨眼之间。不久之前，电脑的性能远不如现在强大，运行速度也慢得多。上世纪六十年代末，我有一台可编程的桌面计算器。这是一种原始的电子仪器，可以进行加、减、乘、除运算。它还有一个小小的内存，你可以把程序存进去，让它执行一系列算术运算。其中一次运算还可以修改程序中的某些行。因此，用这台可编程计算器进行迭代计算就成为可能。只是速度非常慢。一天下午，我编写好程序，检查了前几步，确保没有出错，然后关掉办公室的灯，回家了。与此同时，这台可编程计算器还在默默地进行着加减乘除运算，在它内部的电子元件中发出低沉的嗡嗡声。它偶尔会被编程打印出一个结果。机器上的打印机是一个噪音很大的冲击式装置，会发出像“BRRRAAAK”一样的巨响。

What are a mere million iterations to a patient computer? In today’s world, it is no more than the blinking of an eye. Not so long ago, computers were less powerful and much slower. In the late 1960s, I had a programmable desk calculator. This was a primitive electronic instrument that would add, subtract, multiply, and divide. But it also had a small memory, where you could put a program that told it to engage in a sequence of arithmetic operations. One of those operations could also change lines of your program. So, it became possible to run an iterative calculation on this programmable calculator. It just took a long time. One afternoon, I programmed the machine, checked the first few steps to make sure I had not made an error in my program, turned off the light in my office, and left for home. Meanwhile, the programmable calculator was adding and subtracting, multiplying and dividing, silently, mumbling away in its electronic innards. Every once in a while it was programmed to print out a result. The printer on the machine was a noisy impact device that made a loud sound like “BRRRAAAK.”

夜间清洁人员来到大楼，其中一个人拿着扫帚和废纸篓进了我的办公室。在黑暗中，他听到一阵嗡嗡声。他看到计算器的蓝光忽明忽暗，一遍又一遍地进行着加减运算。突然，机器启动了。“嘶——”它发出声音，然后是“嘶—— ...

The nighttime cleaning crew came into the building and one of the men took his broom and wastepaper collector into my office. There in the darkness, he could hear a humming. He could see the blue light of the calculator’s one eye waxing and waning as it added and subtracted over and over again. Suddenly, the machine awoke. “BRRAAK,” it said, and then, “BRRAAK, BRRAAK, BRRAAK, BRRRRAAAAK!” He told me later that it was a terrifying experience and asked that I leave some sort of sign up the next time, warning that the computer was at work.

如今的计算机运行速度飞快，能够分析的似然函数也越来越复杂。哈佛大学的南·莱尔德教授和詹姆斯·韦尔教授发明了一种极其灵活且强大的迭代算法，称为“EM算法”。我的每一期统计学期刊都会报道有人如何将EM算法应用于曾经被认为无法解决的问题。文献中还出现了其他算法，它们有着诸如“模拟退火”和“克里金法”之类的奇特名称。还有Metropolis算法和Marquardt算法，以及其他一些以其发现者命名的算法。一些包含成百上千行代码的复杂软件包使得这类迭代计算变得“用户友好”。

Today’s computers work much faster, and ever more complicated likelihoods are being analyzed. Professors Nan Laird and James Ware of Harvard University have invented a remarkably flexible and powerful iterative procedure known as the “EM algorithm.” Every new issue of my statistical journals describes how someone has adapted his or her EM algorithm to what had once been considered an unsolvable problem. Other algorithms have been appearing in the literature, going by fanciful names like “simulated annealing” and “kriging.” There is the Metropolis algorithm and the Marquardt algorithm, and others named after their discoverers. There are complicated software packages with hundreds and thousands of lines of code that have made such iterative calculations “user friendly.”

费舍尔的统计估计方法堪称辉煌。极大似然法统治着世界，而皮尔逊的方法则被尘封在历史的尘埃中。然而，在20世纪30年代——费舍尔的数学统计理论贡献最终得到认可，他正值壮年，精力充沛——就在那时，一位名叫耶日·内曼的年轻波兰数学家，正在就一些费舍尔避而不谈的问题提出疑问。

Fisher’s approach to statistical estimation is triumphant. Maximum likelihood rules the world, and Pearson’s methods lie in the dust of discarded history. But at that time, in the 1930s—when Fisher was finally recognized for his contributions to the theory of mathematical statistics, when Fisher was in his forties and in the full bloom of his strength—at that very moment, there was a young Polish mathematician named Jerzy Neyman, who was asking questions about some problems that Fisher had swept under the rug.

C章第八章

CHAPTER 8

致命剂量

THE DOSE THAT KILLS

E每年三月，生物识别学会都会在美国南部城市举办春季会议。我们这些在北方生活和工作的人有机会前往路易斯维尔、孟菲斯、亚特兰大或新奥尔良，呼吸清新的春日空气，欣赏花果树在家乡盛开的美景，比我们提前几周就能欣赏到它们的风采。与其他科学会议一样，会议也设有专题研讨会，每场研讨会由三到五位演讲者进行论文报告，讨论者和听众则通过质疑论文的推导过程或提出其他方法来对这些论文进行评析。通常上午会安排两组平行研讨会，午餐时间稍作休息，下午再进行两组研讨会。最后一场研讨会通常在下午五点左右结束。与会者返回酒店房间，但大约一个半小时后会再次聚在一起。这些小组随后会前往餐厅享用晚餐，品尝当地美食。

Every March, the Biometric Society holds a spring meeting in a southern U.S. city. Those of us who live and work in the North have a chance to come down to Louisville, or Memphis, or Atlanta, or New Orleans, breathe in the new spring air, and watch the flowers and fruit trees bloom a few weeks before it will happen back home. As at other scientific meetings, sessions are held where three to five speakers present papers, and discussants and the audience criticize those papers by questioning the derivations or noting alternative approaches. There are usually two groups of parallel sessions in the morning, a brief break for lunch, followed by two groups of sessions in the afternoon. The last of the sessions is usually over around 5 P.M. The participants return to their rooms in the hotels but gather in groups an hour to an hour and a half later. These small groups then go off to dinner, sampling the restaurants of the city.

通常情况下，你会在研讨会上结识朋友，并约好当天一起吃晚饭。有一天，我却忘了这么做。我和下午一位演讲者进行了长时间的愉快交谈。他来自附近地区，正准备回家，所以我没有和他约好一起吃晚饭。我们结束谈话时，会场空无一人，我找不到其他人可以联系。于是我回到房间，给妻子打了电话，和孩子们聊了一会儿，然后下楼去了酒店大堂。或许我能遇到一群认识的人，可以加入他们。

You usually meet friends at the sessions and make arrangements for dinner during the day. One day, I had failed to do so. I had gotten into a long, interesting discussion with one of the afternoon speakers. He came from the area and was heading home, so I did not arrange to have dinner with him. When we left off our conversation, the hall was empty, and there was no one else for me to contact. I went back to my room, telephoned my wife, talked to our children, and descended to the hotel lobby. Perhaps I would find a group of people I knew and could join them.

大厅里几乎空无一人，只有一位身材高大、头发花白的老人独自坐在其中一张软垫椅上。我认出他是切斯特·布利斯。我知道他是药物和毒物剂量反应关系测定中常用统计模型的发明者。那天上午我参加了一个研讨会，他在会上做了报告。我走上前去，做了自我介绍，并称赞了他的演讲。他邀请我坐下，我们便坐了一会儿，聊起了统计学和数学。是的，这些话题确实可以聊，甚至可以开开玩笑。我们俩显然都没有晚餐的安排，于是决定一起吃。他是一位风趣幽默的餐桌伙伴，有很多他亲身经历的故事可以分享。后来几年，我们有时会在会议上再次共进晚餐，而且我经常会在耶鲁大学统计系举办的讲座上见到他，他当时在那里任教。

The lobby was almost empty, except for a tall, white-haired man sitting by himself in one of the upholstered chairs. I recognized him as Chester Bliss. I knew him as the inventor of the basic statistical models that were used in determining dose response relationships for drugs and poisons. I had attended a session that morning where he had presented a paper. I went over to him, introduced myself, and complimented him on his talk. He invited me to sit down, and we sat there for a while talking about statistics and mathematics. Yes, it is possible to talk about such things and even make jokes about them. It became obvious that neither of us had made plans for dinner, so we agreed to dine together. He was an entertaining dinner companion, with a wealth of stories from his experience. At meetings in later years, we would sometimes dine together again, and I would often see him when I attended talks sponsored by the Yale University statistics department, where he taught.

布利斯出身于美国中西部一个典型的美国中产阶级家庭。他的父亲是一位医生，母亲是一位家庭主妇。他有几个兄弟姐妹。他最初对生物学感兴趣，大学时主修昆虫学。20世纪20年代末，大学毕业后，他进入美国农业部担任昆虫学家，很快便参与到杀虫剂的研发工作中。他很快意识到，杀虫剂的田间试验涉及许多无法控制的变量，难以解释结果。于是，他将昆虫带回室内，开展了一系列实验室实验。有人向他推荐了R.A.费舍尔的著作《科研人员的统计方法》。以此为起点，他开始阅读费舍尔的数学论文，试图理解书中方法背后的原理。

Bliss came from a solid middle-American Midwestern home. His father was a doctor. His mother was a homemaker. He had several sisters and brothers. His first interests were in biology, and he studied entomology in college. In the late 1920s, when he graduated, he took a position with the U.S. Department of Agriculture as an entomologist and was soon involved in the development of insecticides. He quickly realized that field experiments with insecticides involved many uncontrolled variables and were difficult to interpret. He took his insects indoors and set up a series of laboratory experiments. Someone introduced him to R. A. Fisher’s book, Statistical Methods for Research Workers. With this as a start, he found himself reading Fisher’s more mathematical papers as he tried to understand what lay behind the methods Fisher showed in the book.

概率分析

PROBIT ANALYSIS

不久，布利斯效仿费舍尔的做法，开始进行实验室实验。他将昆虫分组放入玻璃罐中，并分别施用不同组合和剂量的杀虫剂。在实验过程中，他注意到一个有趣的现象：无论杀虫剂的浓度有多高，总会有一两只昆虫在接触后存活下来。而且，无论杀虫剂的浓度有多低，即使只使用液体载体，也会有一些昆虫因接触而死亡。

Soon, following Fisher’s lead, Bliss was setting up laboratory experiments in which groups of insects were placed under glass jars and subjected to different combinations and doses of insecticides. As he ran these experiments, he began to notice an interesting phenomenon. No matter how concentrated he made the insecticide, there would always be one or two specimens still alive after exposure. And no matter how weak the pesticide, even if he had used only the liquid carrier, there would be a few insects killed by their exposure.

鉴于这种明显的变异性，用皮尔逊统计分布来模拟杀虫剂的影响会很有帮助，但该如何做呢？读者或许还记得高中代数课上那些可怕的应用题。A先生和B先生可能在静水中划船，或者逆流而上，也可能在混合油水，或者来回弹球。不管是什么，应用题都会给出一些数字，然后提出一个问题，可怜的学生必须把这些数字代入公式，解出x的值。读者可能还记得自己翻遍课本，拼命寻找类似的例题，然后试图把新的数字套用到例题的公式里。

With this obvious variability, it would be useful to model the effects of insecticides in terms of Pearson’s statistical distributions, but how? The reader may recall those terrible moments in high school algebra when the book shifted into word problems. Mr. A and Mr. B were set rowing in still water or against a steady current, or maybe they were mixing water with oil, or bouncing a ball back and forth. Whatever it was, the word problem would propose some numbers and then ask a question, and the poor student had to put those words into a formula and solve for x. The reader may recall going back through the pages of the textbook, desperately seeking a similar problem that was worked out as an example and trying to stuff the new numbers into the formulas that were used in that example.

在高中代数课上，公式都已经有人推导出来了。老师要么知道这些公式，要么可以从教材的教师手册里找到。想象一下，一道应用题，没人知道怎么把它转换成公式，有些信息是多余的，不应该用到，关键信息常常缺失，而且教材里也没有类似的例子。这就是试图将统计模型应用于实际问题时会遇到的情况。切斯特·布利斯试图将概率分布的新数学概念应用于他的杀虫剂实验时，就遇到了这种情况。

In high school algebra, someone had already worked out the formulas. The teacher knew them or could find them in the teacher’s manual for the textbook. Imagine a word problem where nobody knows how to turn it into a formula, where some of the information is redundant and should not be used, where crucial information is often missing, and where there is no similar example worked out earlier in the textbook. This is what happens when one tries to apply statistical models to real-life problems. This was the situation when Chester Bliss tried to adapt the new mathematical ideas of probability distributions to his experiments with insecticides.

布利斯发明了一种他称之为“概率单位分析”的方法。他的发明需要非凡的原创性思维。费舍尔、“学生”或其他任何人的著作中都没有任何内容能启发他进行这项研究。他之所以使用“概率单位”这个词，是因为他的模型将剂量与昆虫在该剂量下死亡的概率联系起来。他的模型生成的最重要的参数是“半数致死剂量”，通常简称为“LD50”。这是有50%概率杀死昆虫的杀虫剂剂量。如果将杀虫剂用于大量昆虫，那么50%的昆虫将被LD50杀死。布利斯模型的另一个结果是，无法确定杀死特定样本的剂量。

Bliss invented a procedure he called “probit analysis.” His invention required remarkable leaps of original thought. There was nothing in the works of Fisher, of “Student,” or of anyone else that even suggested how he might proceed. He used the word probit because his model related the dose to the probability that an insect would die at that dose. The most important parameter his model generated is called the “50 percent lethal dose,” usually referred to as the “LD-50.” This is the dose of insecticide that has a 50 percent probability of killing. If the insecticide is applied to large numbers of insects, then 50 percent will be killed by the LD-50. Another consequence of Bliss’s model is that it is impossible to determine the dose that will kill a specific specimen.

布利斯的概率单位分析法已成功应用于毒理学问题。从某种意义上说，概率单位分析法所获得的洞见构成了毒理学诸多科学理论的基础。概率单位分析法为十六世纪医生帕拉塞尔苏斯提出的“剂量决定毒性”理论提供了数学基础。根据帕拉塞尔苏斯的理论，任何物质如果剂量足够高，都可能成为毒物；而如果剂量足够低，则无毒。布利斯在此基础上，进一步考虑了个体实验结果的不确定性。

Bliss’s probit analysis has been applied successfully to problems in toxicology. In some sense, the insights gained from probit analysis form the foundation of much of the science of toxicology. Probit analysis provides a mathematical foundation for the doctrine first established by the sixteenth-century physician Paracelsus: “Only the dose makes a thing not a poison.” Under the Paracelsus doctrine, all things are potential poisons if given in a high enough dose, and all things are nonpoisonous if given in a low enough dose. To this doctrine, Bliss added the uncertainty associated with individual results.

许多愚蠢的街头毒品使用者因吸食可卡因、海洛因或安非他明而死亡或身患重病的原因之一是，他们看到其他人吸食毒品后并未丧命。他们就像布利斯的昆虫一样，环顾四周，看到一些同伴仍然活着。然而，知道有些个体仍然活着并不能保证某个个体一定能够幸存。我们无法预测单个个体的反应。就像皮尔逊统计模型中的个体观察值一样，这些并非科学感兴趣的“事物”。我们只能估计抽象的概率分布及其参数（例如半数致死量）。

One reason why many foolish users of street drugs die or become very sick on cocaine or heroin or speed is that they see others using the drugs without being killed. They are like Bliss’s insects. They look around and see some of their fellow insects still alive. However, knowing that some individuals are still living provides no assurance that a given individual will survive. There is no way of predicting the response of a single individual. Like the individual observations in Pearson’s statistical model, these are not the “things” with which science is interested. Only the abstract probability distribution and its parameters (like the LD-50) can be estimated.

布利斯提出概率单位分析法后[10] ，其他研究人员提出了不同的数学分布模型。现代计算机程序通常提供多种模型供用户选择，这些模型都是对布利斯方法的改进。使用实际数据的研究表明，所有这些替代方法都能得出非常相似的LD50估计值，尽管它们在对概率低得多的剂量（例如LD10）的估计值上存在差异。

Once Bliss had proposed probit analysis,[10] other workers proposed different mathematical distributions. Modern computer programs for computing the LD-50 usually offer the user a selection from several different models that have been proposed as refinements on Bliss’s work. Studies using actual data indicate that all these alternatives produce very similar estimates of the LD-50, although they differ in their estimates of doses associated with much lower probabilities, like the LD-10.

利用概率单位分析或其他替代模型，可以估算不同的致死剂量，例如LD-25或LD-80，分别代表能杀死25%或80%小鼠的剂量。距离50%致死率点越远，需要的实验规模就越大才能获得准确的估算值。我曾参与一项研究，旨在确定一种能使小鼠患癌的化合物的LD-01。该研究使用了65,000只小鼠，我们对最终结果的分析表明，我们仍然无法准确估算出1%小鼠患癌所需的剂量。基于该研究数据的计算表明，我们需要数亿只小鼠才能获得可接受的LD-01估算值。

It is possible, using probit analysis or any of the alternative models, to estimate a different lethal dose, such as the LD-25 or the LD-80, the doses that will kill 25 percent or 80 percent, respectively. The further you get from the 50 percent point, the more massive the experiment that is needed to get a good estimate. I was once involved in a study to determine the LD-01 of a compound that causes cancer in mice. The study used 65,000 mice, and our analysis of the final results indicated that we still did not have a good estimate of the dose that would produce cancer in 1 percent of the mice. Calculations based on the data from that study showed that we would need several hundred million mice to get an acceptable estimate of the LD-01.

斯大林恐怖统治时期苏联列宁格勒的幸福

BLISS IN SOVIET LENINGRAD DURING THE STALIN TERRORS

切斯特·布利斯最初对概率分析的研究在1933年戛然而止。富兰克林·罗斯福当选美国总统。在竞选期间，罗斯福明确指出，联邦赤字是造成经济大萧条的罪魁祸首，并承诺削减国家赤字，精简政府规模。虽然新政最终并未兑现这一承诺，但这却是他竞选时的承诺。新总统上任后，他的一些任命官员开始解雇不必要的政府工作人员，以落实这一竞选承诺。负责研发新型杀虫剂的农业部助理副部长助理审查了该部门的工作，发现有人愚蠢地在实验室里进行杀虫剂试验，而不是在昆虫出没的田野里进行试验。布利斯的实验室被关闭，布利斯本人也被解雇。在大萧条的深渊中，他失去了工作。切斯特·布利斯发明了概率分析，而失业的昆虫学家却找不到工作，尤其是那些在室内而不是在昆虫生活的室外研究昆虫的昆虫学家，这些都无关紧要。

Chester Bliss’s initial work on probit analysis was interrupted in 1933. Franklin D. Roosevelt had been elected president of the United States. In his campaign for the presidency, Roosevelt had made it clear that it was the federal deficit that was causing the depression, and he promised to cut the national deficit and reduce the size of government. That is not what the New Deal eventually did, but that was the campaign promise. When the new president came into office, some of his appointees started firing unnecessary government workers to follow up on this campaign promise. The assistant to the assistant undersecretary of agriculture, in charge of developing new insecticides, looked at what the department had been doing and discovered that someone was foolishly trying to experiment with insecticides inside a laboratory, instead of out in the fields where the insects were. Bliss’s laboratory was closed and Bliss was fired. He found himself without a job in the depths of the Great Depression. It did not matter that Chester Bliss had invented probit analysis, and that there were no jobs for an out-of-work entomologist, especially one who worked with insects indoors instead of outdoors where they live.

布利斯联系了刚刚在伦敦找到新工作的R.A.费舍尔。费舍尔提出为他提供住宿和一些实验室设施，但他没有合适的职位，也无法支付这位美国昆虫学家的薪水。尽管如此，布利斯还是去了英国。他在费舍尔一家住了几个月。他和费舍尔一起改进了概率单位分析法。费舍尔发现布利斯的数学计算中存在一些错误，并提出了修改建议，使最终的统计数据更加高效。布利斯发表了一篇新论文，运用了费舍尔的建议。费舍尔也将必要的表格纳入了他与弗兰克·耶茨合著的统计表格新版中。

Bliss contacted R. A. Fisher, who had just taken a new position in London. Fisher offered to put him up and give him some laboratory facilities, but he did not have a job for him and could not pay the American entomologist. Bliss went to England anyway. He lived with Fisher and his family for a few months. Together, he and Fisher refined the methodology of probit analysis. Fisher found some errors in his mathematics and suggested modifications that made the resulting statistics more efficient. Bliss published a new paper, making use of Fisher’s suggestions, and Fisher incorporated the necessary tables in a new edition of the book of statistical tables he had written in conjunction with Frank Yates.

在英国待了不到一年，费舍尔就帮布利斯找到了一份工作。那是在苏联列宁格勒工厂研究所。想象一下，身材高挑、瘦削，典型的美国中西部人，对政治漠不关心的切斯特·布利斯，始终没能学会第二外语，带着一个装着仅有衣物的小行李箱，乘火车穿越欧洲，抵达列宁格勒火车站时，残暴的苏联独裁者斯大林正开始对政府各级官员进行血腥清洗。

After less than a year in England, Fisher found Bliss a job. It was at the Leningrad Plant Institute in the Soviet Union. Imagine tall, thin, Midwestern, middle-American, apolitical Chester Bliss, who was never able to learn a second language, crossing Europe by train with a small suitcase containing his only clothes, and arriving at the station in Leningrad just as the ruthless Soviet dictator, Stalin, was beginning his bloody purges of both major and minor government officials.

布利斯抵达不久，雇佣他的老板就被召往莫斯科——从此杳无音讯。一个月后，雇佣布利斯的那个人也被召往莫斯科——在返回途中“自杀身亡”。布利斯隔壁实验室的负责人有一天匆匆离开，偷偷越过拉脱维亚边境逃离了俄罗斯。

Soon after Bliss arrived, the boss of the man who had hired him was called to Moscow—and was never seen again. A month later, the man who had hired Bliss was called to Moscow—and “committed suicide” on the way back. The man in charge of the laboratory next to Bliss’s left hastily one day and escaped Russia by sneaking across the Latvian border.

与此同时，布利斯开始着手工作，他用不同的杀虫剂组合处理选定的俄罗斯害虫群体，并计算概率单位和半数致死量。他在研究所附近的一栋房子里租了一间房。他的俄罗斯女房东只会说俄语，而布利斯只会说英语，但他告诉我，他们靠着手势和爽朗的笑声相处得相当融洽。布利斯结识了一位来自美国的年轻女子。她辍学来到俄罗斯，参与伟大的共产主义实验，带着年轻人的满腔理想和真正的马克思列宁主义者的教条盲目而来。她与贫穷的、只会说俄语的切斯特·布利斯成了朋友，帮助他购物，带他游览城市。她也是当地共产党的党员。党对布利斯的情况了如指掌。他们知道他何时被聘用，何时抵达俄罗斯，住在哪里，以及他在实验室里做什么。

In the meantime, Bliss set to work, treating selected groups of Russian pests with different combinations of insecticides and working out the probits and LD-50s. He rented a room in a house near the institute. His Russian landlady could speak only Russian, and Bliss could speak only English, but he told me that they managed to get along quite well with combinations of gestures and hearty laughs. Bliss met a young woman from America. She had left college to participate in the great Communist experiment in Russia and arrived with the full idealism of youth and the dogmatic blindness of a true Marxist-Leninist. She befriended poor monolingual Chester Bliss and helped him shop and get around the city. She was also a member of the local Communist party. The party knew all about Bliss. They knew when he had been hired, when he arrived in Russia, where he lived, and what he was doing in his lab.

有一天，她告诉他，党内有好几个人都认定他是美国间谍。她为他辩护，试图解释说他只是个单纯、天真的科学家，一心只想做实验。然而，莫斯科方面已经得知了这些怀疑，并派了一个委员会前往列宁格勒进行调查。

One day, she told him that several members of the party had come to the conclusion that he was an American spy. She had defended him and tried to explain that he was a simple, naive scientist who was only interested in his experiments. However, Moscow had been notified of these suspicions, and they were sending a committee to Leningrad to investigate.

委员会在列宁格勒植物研究所召开会议，传唤布利斯到场接受质询。布利斯走进房间时，已经知道委员会成员是谁——他的女友告诉过他。他们刚问完几个问题，他就开口说道：“我看到某某教授也在你们中间（布利斯跟我讲这个故事时，记不起他的名字了）。我一直在读他的论文。请问，他提出的这种农业实验方法，难道就是马克思和列宁的教诲吗？”翻译犹豫着要不要翻译这个问题，翻译出来后，委员会成员们有些骚动。他们要求布利斯详细解释。

The committee convened at the Leningrad Plant Institute and called Bliss to appear before them to be interrogated. When he walked into the room, he knew who the members of the committee were—having been told by his girlfriend. They had barely gotten through the first few questions, when he said to them, “I see Professor so-and-so is among you [Bliss could not remember his name when he told me the story]. I have been reading his papers. Tell me, this method of agricultural experimentation he proposes, is it the gospel according to Saint Marx and Saint Lenin?” The interpreter was hesitant to translate this question, and when he did there was some stirring among the committee members. They asked him to elaborate.

布利斯问道：“某某教授的方法，是官方的路线吗？这是党要求进行农业实验的方式吗？”

“Professor so-and-so’s method,” Bliss asked, “is this the official party line? Is this the way in which the party requires that agricultural experimentation be done?”

最终的答复是，是的，这是正确的做法。

The eventual response was, yes, this was the correct way to do things.

“好吧，既然如此，”布利斯说，“我就违背了你的宗教信仰。”布利斯接着解释说，这个人提出的农业研究方法要求对大片土地进行相同的处理。布利斯说他认为这样的实验毫无意义，并指出他一直提倡使用相邻的小块地，并在每个地块内的每行作物上采用不同的处理方法。

“Well, in that case,” Bliss said, “I am in violation of your religion.” Bliss went on to explain that the methods of agricultural research proposed by this man required that vast tracts of land all be given the same treatment. Bliss said that he considered such experiments useless and pointed out that he had been advocating the use of small neighboring plots with different treatments assigned to rows within those plots.

审讯并未继续进行下去。当晚，布利斯的朋友告诉他，委员会已经认定他不是间谍。他太过坦率，太过明显。他很可能就像她所说的那样，是一个头脑简单的科学家，只是在做他的实验。

The interrogation did not proceed much further. That evening, Bliss’s friend told him the committee had decided he was not a spy. He was too open and obvious. He was probably what she said he was, a simpleminded scientist going about his experiments.

接下来的几个月，布利斯继续在列宁格勒工厂研究所工作。他不再受老板管束，只能凭自己的判断行事。他必须加入实验室工人的共产党工会。在苏联，所有有工作的人都被迫加入政府运营的工会。除此之外，他们没有干涉他。到了20世纪50年代，美国国务院因为他曾经加入过共产党组织而拒绝给他发放护照。

Bliss continued working at the Leningrad Plant Institute for the next few months. He had no boss anymore. He just did what he thought best. He had to join the Communist labor union of laboratory workers. Everyone who had a job in Russia was forced to belong to a government-run labor union. Other than that, they left him alone. In the 1950s, the U.S. State Department would deny him his passport because he had once belonged to a Communist organization.

一天下午，他的女友突然闯进他的实验室。“你必须立刻离开，”她对他说。他抗议说他的实验还没完成，笔记还没整理好。她一把推开他，让他放下笔记本，然后开始给他拿外套。他必须马上离开。她告诉他，他必须放弃一切。她看着他收拾好小行李箱，向房东太太道别。他的朋友送他到火车站，她坚持让他安全抵达里加后给她打电话。

One afternoon, his girlfriend burst into his lab. “You have to leave immediately,” she told him. He protested that his experiments had not been finished, that he had not yet written up his notes. She pushed him away from his notebooks and started getting him his coat. He had to leave without delay. He had to abandon everything, she told him. She watched him as he packed his small suitcase and said goodbye to his landlady. His friend saw him to the train station, and she insisted that he telephone her when he was safely in Riga.

20世纪60年代初，苏联的冷酷镇压稍稍放松。苏联科学家重新融入国际科学界，国际统计学会（切斯特·布利斯是该学会的成员）在列宁格勒组织了一次会议。会议间隙，布利斯去寻找他20世纪30年代的老朋友。他们都已去世，要么在斯大林的清洗运动中遇害，要么在二战中丧生。只有他的房东太太还健在。两人互相问候，点头致意，他用英语低声说着问候，她则用俄语回应。

In the early 1960s, the cold hand of repression was lifted slightly in the Soviet Union. Soviet scientists rejoined the international scientific community, and the International Statistical Institute (of which Chester Bliss was a fellow) arranged a meeting in Leningrad. Between sessions, Bliss set out to look up his old friends from the 1930s. They were all dead, having been murdered during Stalin’s purges or killed during World War II. Only his landlady remained alive. The two of them greeted each other, bobbing and nodding, he muttering his good wishes in English and she responding in Russian.

C第九章

CHAPTER 9

钟形曲线

THE BELL-SHAPED CURVE

T读过本书前八章的读者或许会认为，统计学革命仅仅发生在英国。这在某种程度上是正确的，因为最早将统计模型应用于生物学和农业研究的尝试确实发生在英国和丹麦。在R.A.费舍尔的影响下，统计方法很快传播到美国、印度、澳大利亚和加拿大。当统计模型的直接应用在英语世界蓬勃发展时，欧洲大陆拥有悠久的数学传统，欧洲数学家们也在致力于研究与统计建模相关的理论问题。

The reader of the first eight chapters of this book has probably come to believe that the statistical revolution occurred solely in Great Britain. To some extent this is true, in that the first attempts to apply statistical models to biological and agricultural studies were in Great Britain and also in Denmark. Under R. A. Fisher’s influence, statistical methods soon spread to the United States, India, Australia, and Canada. While the immediate applications of statistical models were being pushed in the English-speaking world, continental Europe had a long mathematical tradition, and European mathematicians were working on the theoretical problems related to statistical modeling.

其中最重要的是中心极限定理。直到20世纪30年代初，这还是一个未经证明的定理，许多人相信它是正确的，但却无人能够证明。费舍尔关于似然函数值的理论工作正是基于中心极限定理成立的假设。19世纪初，皮埃尔·西蒙·拉普拉斯也曾以此假设来论证他的最小二乘法。新兴的心理学发展出了基于中心极限定理的智力测量技术和精神疾病量表。

Foremost among these was the central limit theorem. Until the early 1930s, this was an unproven theorem, a conjecture that many believed was true but which no one had been able to prove. Fisher’s theoretical work on the value of the likelihood function assumed this theorem to be true. Pierre Simon Laplace, in the early nineteenth century, had justified his method of least squares with that assumption. The new science of psychology developed techniques of measuring intelligence and scales of mental illness that rested on the central limit theorem.

中心极限定理是什么？

WHAT IS THE CENTRAL LIMIT THEOREM?

大量数字的平均值服从某种统计分布。中心极限定理指出，无论初始数据来自何处，这种分布都可以用正态概率分布来近似。正态概率分布与拉普拉斯误差函数相同，有时也被称为“高斯分布”。在一些科普作品中，它也被通俗地描述为“钟形曲线”。18世纪末，亚伯拉罕·棣莫弗证明了中心极限定理适用于来自机会游戏的简单数字集合。此后150年间，在证明这一猜想方面没有取得任何进展。

The averages of large collections of numbers have a statistical distribution. The central limit theorem states that this distribution can be approximated by the normal probability distribution regardless of where the initial data came from. The normal probability distribution is the same as Laplace’s error function. It is sometimes called the “Gaussian distribution.” It has been described loosely in popular works as the “bell-shaped curve.” In the late eighteenth century, Abraham de Moivre proved that the central limit theorem holds for simple collections of numbers from games of chance. For the next 150 years, no further progress was made on proving this conjecture.

该猜想被广泛认为是正确的，因为它证明了用正态分布来描述大多数数据的合理性。一旦假设数据服从正态分布，数学问题就变得容易处理得多。正态分布具有一些非常好的性质。如果两个随机变量服从正态分布，那么它们的和也服从正态分布。一般来说，所有正态变量的和与差都服从正态分布。因此，许多由正态变量导出的统计量本身也服从正态分布。

The conjecture was widely assumed true because it justified using the normal distribution to describe most data. Once one assumes that the normal distribution is involved, the mathematics become much more tractable. The normal distribution has some very nice properties. If two random variables have a normal distribution, their sum has a normal distribution. In general, all kinds of sums and differences of normal variables have normal distributions. So, many statistics derived from normal variates are themselves normally distributed.

正态分布只有卡尔·皮尔逊提出的四个参数中的两个：均值和标准差。对称性和峰度均为零。一旦这两个数值已知，其他所有信息也就都已知了。费舍尔证明，从一组数据中估计出的均值和标准差是他所谓的“充分估计”。它们包含了数据中的所有信息。由于这两个数值包含了从这些测量中可以发现的所有信息，因此无需保留任何原始测量数据。如果测量数据足以对均值和标准差进行相当精确的估计，则无需进行更多测量，收集更多测量数据是浪费时间。例如，如果您只希望将正态分布的两个参数精确到两位有效数字，则只需收集大约五十个测量数据即可。

The normal distribution has only two of Karl Pearson’s four parameters, the mean and the standard deviation. The symmetry and kurtosis are equal to zero. Once these two numbers are known, everything else is known. Fisher showed that estimates of the mean and the standard deviation taken from a set of data are what he called sufficient. They contain all the information in the data. There is no need to keep any of the original measurements, since these two numbers contain all that can be discovered from those measurements. If there are enough measurements to allow for reasonably precise estimates of the mean and the standard deviation, no more measurements are needed, and the effort to collect them is a waste of time. For instance, if you are willing to settle for knowing the two parameters of a normal distribution within two significant figures, you need collect only about fifty measurements.

正态分布的数学可处理性意味着科学家可以构建复杂的关系模型。只要基础分布是正态分布，费舍尔似然函数通常可以用简单的代数运算进行处理。即使对于需要迭代求解的复杂模型，如果分布是正态分布，南·莱尔德和詹姆斯·韦尔的EM算法也会变得特别容易使用。在建模问题中，统计学家常常假设所有数据都服从正态分布，因为数学处理起来非常方便。然而，要做到这一点，他们必须用到中心极限定理。

The mathematical tractability of the normal distribution means that the scientist can pose a complicated model of relationships. As long as the underlying distribution is normal, Fisher’s likelihood function often has a form that can be manipulated with simple algebra. Even for models that are so complicated they need iterative solutions, Nan Laird and James Ware’s EM algorithm becomes especially easy to use if the distributions are normal. In modeling problems, statisticians often act as if all the data are normally distributed because the mathematics are so tractable. To do so, however, they have to invoke the central limit theorem.

但是，中心极限定理是正确的吗？更准确地说，它在什么条件下成立？

But, was the central limit theorem true? To be more exact, under what conditions was it true?

在20世纪20年代和30年代，一群来自斯堪的纳维亚、德国、法国和苏联的数学家，利用20世纪初发现的一系列新的数学工具，研究这些问题。而这一切，都发生在人类文明即将面临灾难——邪恶的极权国家崛起——的背景下。

In the 1920s and 1930s, a group of mathematicians in Scandinavia, Germany, France, and the Soviet Union was pursuing these questions with a set of new mathematical tools that had been discovered in the early years of the twentieth century. This, in the face of an impending disaster for all of civilization—the rise of evil totalitarian states.

数学家并不需要配备昂贵设备的实验室。在20世纪20年代和30年代，数学家的典型工具是黑板和粉笔。在黑板上做数学题比在纸上做题更好，因为粉笔更容易擦掉，而且数学研究总是充满错误。很少有数学家能够独自工作。如果你是一名数学家，你需要与人交流你的研究成果。你需要将你的新想法公之于众，接受他人的批评。犯错或引入你看不见但对其他人来说显而易见的隐性假设是如此容易。国际数学家群体通过书信往来、参加会议、审阅彼此的论文，不断地进行批评、质疑和探讨各种可能性。 20 世纪 30 年代初，德国的威廉·费勒和理查德·冯·米塞斯、法国的保罗·列维、俄罗斯的安德烈·科尔莫戈罗夫、斯堪的纳维亚的雅尔·瓦尔德马·林德伯格和哈拉尔德·克拉默、奥地利的亚伯拉罕·瓦尔德和赫尔曼·哈特利、意大利的圭多·卡斯特尔努沃以及其他许多学者都进行了交流，其中许多人利用这些新工具研究了中心极限定理。

A mathematician does not need a laboratory with expensive equipment. In the 1920s and 1930s, the typical equipment of a mathematician was a blackboard and chalk. It is better to do mathematics on a chalkboard than on a piece of paper because chalk is easier to erase, and mathematical research is always filled with mistakes. Very few mathematicians can work alone. If you are a mathematician, you need to talk about what you are doing. You need to expose your new ideas to the criticism of others. It is so easy to make mistakes or to include hidden assumptions that you do not see, but that are obvious to others. There is an international community of mathematicians who exchange letters, go to meetings, and examine each other’s papers, constantly criticizing, questioning, exploring ramifications. In the early 1930s, William Feller and Richard von Mises in Germany, Paul Levy in France, Andrei Kolmogorov in Russia, Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg and Harald Cramér in Scandinavia, Abraham Wald and Herman Hartley in Austria, Guido Castelnuovo in Italy, and many others were all in communication, many of them examining the central limit conjecture with these new tools.

然而，这种自由自在的交流很快就结束了。斯大林的恐怖统治、纳粹的种族理论以及墨索里尼的帝国野心，都如同阴云般笼罩着一切，即将将其摧毁。斯大林精心策划了一系列操纵性的审判和夜间突袭，杀害并恐吓任何被他怀疑的人。希特勒和他的犯罪爪牙将犹太教授（主要是）从大学里拖出来，送往残酷的劳改营。墨索里尼则将人们划分成一个个预先设定的阶层，他称之为“社团国家”。

This free and easy interaction, however, was soon to cease. The dark shadows of Stalin’s terror, Nazi racial theories, and Mussolini’s dreams of empire were about to destroy it. Stalin was perfecting a combination of rigged show trials and arrests in the middle of the night, killing and intimidating anyone who fell under his paranoid suspicion. Hitler and his criminal henchmen were dragging Jewish professors (primarily) out of the universities and into brutal work camps. Mussolini was locking people into preordained castes that he called the “corporate state.”

死亡万岁！

VIVA LA MUERTE!

这种猖獗的反智主义在西班牙内战期间达到了一个极端例子。当时，法西斯主义和斯大林主义这两大邪恶势力以勇敢的西班牙青年的生命为代价，展开了一场残酷的代理人战争。长枪党（即西班牙法西斯分子）占领了古老的萨拉曼卡大学。该大学的校长是享誉世界的西班牙哲学家米格尔·德·乌纳穆诺，当时他已年过七旬。长枪党将军米兰·阿斯特雷曾在之前的战争中失去了一条腿、一只胳膊和一只眼睛，他是这支新占领军的宣传部长。他的口号是“死亡万岁！”（Viva la muerte!）。如同莎士比亚笔下的理查三世国王一样，米兰·阿斯特雷的残疾象征着他扭曲邪恶的思想。长枪党在萨拉曼卡大学的礼堂举行了一场盛大的庆祝活动。主席台上坐着新任命的省长塞尼奥拉·弗朗西斯科·弗朗哥、萨拉曼卡主教米兰·阿斯特雷，以及年迈的米格尔·德·乌纳穆诺，他们被当作战利品拖了出来。

An extreme example of this rampant anti-intellectualism occurred during the Spanish Civil War, where the twin evils of fascism and Stalinism were fighting a vicious proxy war with the lives of brave young Spaniards. The Falangists (as the Spanish fascists were known) had conquered the ancient University of Salamanca. The rector of the university was the world-renowned Spanish philosopher Miguel de Unamuno, then in his early seventies. The Falangist general Millan Astray, who had lost a leg, an arm, and an eye in a previous war, was the propaganda chief of the newly conquering forces. His motto was Viva la muerte! (Long live death!). Like Shakespeare’s King Richard III, Millan Astray’s crippled body was a metaphor for his twisted and evil mind. The Falangists decreed a great celebration in the ceremonial hall of the University of Salamanca. The dais held the newly appointed governor of the province, Señora Francisco Franco, Millan Astray, the bishop of Salamanca, and an elderly Miguel de Unamuno, dragged forth as a trophy of their conquests.

“死亡万岁！”米兰·阿斯特雷高喊道，拥挤的大厅里回荡着他的呼喊。“西班牙！”有人喊道，大厅里响起一片回应：“西班牙！死亡万岁！”身着蓝色制服的长枪党成员齐声起立，向讲台上佛朗哥的画像行法西斯礼。在这些呼喊声中，乌纳穆诺站起身，缓缓走向讲台。他平静地开始讲话：

Viva la muerte! Millan Astray shouted, and the crowded hall echoed his cry. España! someone shouted, and the hall responded, España! Viva la muerte! The Falangists in their blue uniforms stood up in unison and gave a fascist salute to the portrait of Franco above the dais. In the midst of these cries, Unamuno stood up and slowly moved to the podium. He began quietly:

你们都在关注我的话。你们都了解我，也知道我无法保持沉默。有时候，沉默就是撒谎。因为沉默会被解读为默许。我想评论一下米兰·阿斯特雷将军的演讲——姑且称之为演讲吧……。刚才我听到一声令人作呕、毫无意义的叫喊：“死亡万岁！”而我，一个毕生致力于构建悖论的人……我必须以专家的身份告诉你们，这种荒谬的悖论令我反感。米兰·阿斯特雷将军是个残疾人……他是个战争残疾人……。不幸的是，现在西班牙的残疾人太多了。如果上帝不来救我们，很快残疾人还会更多……

All of you are hanging on my words. You all know me and are aware that I am unable to remain silent. At times to be silent is to lie. For silence can be interpreted as acquiescence. I want to comment on the speech—to give it a name—of General Millan Astray … . Just now I heard a necrophilous and senseless cry: “Long live death.” And I, who have spent my life shaping paradoxes … I must tell you, as an expert authority, that this outlandish paradox is repellent to me. General Millan Astray is a cripple … . He is a war cripple … . Unfortunately, there are too many cripples in Spain just now. And soon there will be even more of them if God does not come to our aid … .

米兰·阿斯特雷一把推开乌纳穆诺，高喊道：“智商低下！死亡万岁！”长枪党人响应他的喊声，冲上前去抓住乌纳穆诺，但这位老校长继续说道：

Millan Astray pushed Unamuno aside and cried out, Abajo la inteligencia! Viva la muerte! Echoing his cries, the Falangists pushed forward to seize Unamuno, but the old rector continued:

这是智慧的殿堂，而我是它的大祭司。是你亵渎了它的圣地。你会赢，因为你拥有足够的蛮力。但你无法说服我。因为要说服我，你需要的是劝服。而要劝服我，你需要你所缺乏的：理性和正义……

This is the temple of the intellect. And I am its high priest. It is you who profane its sacred precincts. You will win, because you have more than enough brute force. But you will not convince. For to convince you need to persuade. And in order to persuade you would need what you lack: reason and right … .

乌纳穆诺被迫软禁在家，并在一个月内被宣布“死于自然原因”。

Unamuno was forced into house arrest and was declared “dead of natural causes” within the month.

斯大林的恐怖统治开始切断俄罗斯数学家与欧洲其他地区的联系。希特勒的种族政策摧毁了德国的大学，因为许多伟大的欧洲数学家是犹太人或与犹太人结婚，而大多数非犹太人则反对纳粹的计划。威廉·费勒去了普林斯顿大学，亚伯拉罕·瓦尔德去了哥伦比亚大学。赫尔曼·哈特利和理查德·冯·米塞斯去了伦敦。埃米尔·J·冈贝尔逃往法国。埃米·诺特在宾夕法尼亚州的布林莫尔学院获得了一份临时教职。

The Stalin terror began to cut off communication between the Russian mathematicians and the rest of Europe. Hitler’s racial policies decimated German universities, since many of the great European mathematicians were Jewish or were married to Jews, and most of those who were not Jewish were opposed to the Nazi plans. William Feller came to Princeton University, Abraham Wald to Columbia University. Herman Hartley and Richard von Mises went to London. Emil J. Gumbel escaped to France. Emmy Noether was given a temporary position on the faculty of Bryn Mawr College in Pennsylvania.

但并非所有人都逃脱了。美国对任何无法证明自己在美国有工作的人关闭了移民大门。拉丁美洲国家的边境开放和关闭完全取决于一些小官僚的意愿。纳粹军队占领华沙后，搜捕了所有能找到的华沙大学教职员工，残忍地杀害了他们，并将他们埋葬在万人坑中。在纳粹的种族世界里，波兰人和其他斯拉夫人注定要成为雅利安主人的无知奴隶。欧洲许多古老大学里才华横溢的年轻学子惨遭杀害。在苏联，杰出的数学家们只能在纯数学领域寻求庇护，不敢尝试应用数学，因为正是应用数学让科学家们落入了斯大林的冷酷怀疑之中。

But not everyone escaped. American immigration gates were closed to anyone who could not show that he or she had employment waiting in the United States. Latin American nations opened and closed their doors on the whims of petty bureaucrats. When the Nazi forces conquered Warsaw, they hunted down all the members of the faculty of the University of Warsaw they could find, brutally murdered them, and buried them in a mass grave. In the Nazi racial world, Poles and other Slavs were to be the uneducated slaves of their Aryan masters. Many of the young and promising students of the ancient universities of Europe perished. In the Soviet Union, the major mathematicians sought the refuge of pure mathematics, with no attempt at applications, since it was in the applications that scientists fell under Stalin’s cold suspicions.

然而，在这些设想完全成为现实之前，欧洲数学家们已经解决了中心极限定理的问题。芬兰的雅尔·瓦尔德马·林德伯格和法国的保罗·列维分别独立地发现了一组相互重叠的条件，这些条件是中心极限定理成立的必要条件。事实证明，解决这个问题至少有三种不同的方法，而且中心极限定理并非单一定理，而是一组定理，每个定理都基于略有不同的条件推导而来。到1934年，中心极限定理不再是猜想。人们只需要证明林德伯格-列维条件成立即可。这样，中心极限定理就成立了，科学家们就可以自由地假设正态分布是一个合适的模型。

Before these shadows had all become reality, however, European mathematicians solved the problem of the central limit theorem. Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg from Finland and Paul Levy from France independently discovered a set of overlapping conditions that were required for the conjecture to be true. It turned out that there were at least three different approaches to the problem and that there was not a single theorem but a group of central limit theorems, each derived from a slightly different set of conditions. By 1934, the central limit theorem(s) was no longer a conjecture. All one had to do was prove that the Lindeberg-Levy conditions held. Then the central limit theorem holds, and the scientist is free to assume the normal distribution as an appropriate model.

从林德伯格-莱维到U统计

FROM LINDEBERG-LÉVY TO U-STATISTICS

然而，要证明林德伯格-莱维条件在特定情况下成立却十分困难。了解林德伯格-莱维条件固然令人欣慰，因为它们描述的条件看似合理，并且在大多数情况下可能成立。但证明这些条件却截然不同。正因如此，战后在北卡罗来纳大学辛勤耕耘的瓦西里·霍夫丁才显得如此重要。1948年，霍夫丁在《数理统计年鉴》上发表了一篇题为《一类渐近正态分布的统计量》的论文。

However, it is difficult to prove that the Lindeberg-Lévy conditions hold for a particular situation. There is a certain comfort in knowing about the Lindeberg-Lévy conditions, because they describe conditions that seem reasonable and are probably true in most situations. But proving them is a different matter. This is why Wassily Hoeffding, toiling away at the University of North Carolina after the war, is so important to this story. In 1948, Hoeffding published a paper, “A Class of Statistics with Asymptotically Normal Distribution,” in the Annals of Mathematical Statistics.

回顾一下，R.A. Fisher 将统计量定义为从观测测量值中导出的、用于估计分布参数的数值。Fisher 建立了一些统计量应具备的实用标准，并在此过程中指出，Karl Pearson 的许多方法得到的统计量并不符合这些标准。统计量的计算方法有很多种，其中许多都符合 Fisher 的标准。一旦计算出统计量，我们就需要知道它的分布才能使用它。如果它服从正态分布，那么使用起来就容易得多。Hoeffding 证明，属于他称之为“U 统计量”的一类统计量满足 Lindeberg-Lévy 条件。既然如此，我们只需要证明一个新的统计量满足 Hoeffding 的定义，而无需进行复杂的数学推导来证明 Lindeberg-Lévy 条件成立。Hoeffding 所做的只是用另一组数学要求替换了一组。然而，Hoeffding 的条件实际上很容易验证。自霍夫丁的论文发表以来，几乎所有证明新统计量具有正态分布的文章都是通过证明新统计量是 U 统计量来实现的。

Recall that R. A. Fisher defined a statistic as a number that is derived from the observed measurements and that estimates a parameter of the distribution. Fisher established some criteria that a statistic should have in order to be useful, showing in the process that many of Karl Pearson’s methods led to statistics that did not meet these criteria. There are different ways of computing statistics, many of which meet Fisher’s criteria. Once the statistic is computed, we have to know its distribution to use it. If it has a normal distribution, it is much easier to use. Hoeffding showed that a statistic that is part of a class he called “U-statistics” meets the Lindeberg-Lévy conditions. Since this is so, one need only show that a new statistic meets Hoeffding’s definition and not have to work out the difficult mathematics to show Lindeberg-Lévy holds true. All Hoeffding did was to replace one set of mathematical requirements with another. However, Hoeffding’s conditions are, in fact, very easy to check. Since the publication of Hoeffding’s paper, almost all articles that show that a new statistic has a normal distribution do so by showing that the new statistic is a U-statistic.

霍夫丁在柏林

HOEFFDING IN BERLIN

瓦西里·霍夫丁在二战期间处境复杂。他于1914年出生于芬兰，父亲是丹麦人，母亲是芬兰人。当时芬兰是俄罗斯帝国的一部分。一战后，霍夫丁随家人移居丹麦，后又迁往柏林。因此，他拥有两个斯堪的纳维亚国家的双重国籍。1933年高中毕业后，他开始在柏林学习数学，而此时纳粹德国刚刚掌权。预见到即将发生的事情，他所在大学数学系主任理查德·冯·米塞斯提前离开了德国。霍夫丁的许多其他教授也很快逃离，或被撤职。在混乱之中，年轻的霍夫丁只能跟随一些资历较浅的教师学习，而这些教师大多未能完成他们所教授的课程，因为纳粹持续不断地“清洗”高校中的犹太人和犹太同情者。

Wassily Hoeffding had an ambiguous situation during World War II. Born in Finland in 1914, to a Danish father and Finnish mother, at a time when Finland was part of the Russian Empire, Hoeffding moved with his family to Denmark and then Berlin after World War I. He thus had dual citizenship in two Scandinavian countries. He finished high school in 1933 and began studying mathematics in Berlin, just as the Nazis came to power in Germany. Anticipating what would happen, Richard von Mises, the head of the mathematics department at his university, left Germany early. Many of Hoeffding’s other professors fled soon after or were removed from their positions. In the confusion, young Hoeffding took courses with lower-level instructors, many of whom did not last to complete the courses they were teaching, as the Nazis continued to “cleanse” the faculties of Jews and Jewish sympathizers.

他和数学系的其他学生一样，被迫去听路德维希·比伯巴赫的讲座。比伯巴赫此前只是系里一个不起眼的成员，但他对纳粹党的热情拥护使他成为了新的系主任。比伯巴赫的讲座探讨了“雅利安”数学和“非雅利安”数学之间的区别。他发现，那些颓废的“非雅利安”（实为犹太裔）数学家依赖于复杂的代数符号，而“雅利安”数学家则在更高尚、更纯粹的几何直觉领域进行研究。讲座结束时，他征求学生提问，后排的一位学生问他，为什么理查德·库朗（20世纪初德国伟大的犹太裔数学家之一）要运用几何洞察力来发展他的实分析理论。比伯巴赫此后再也没有就此主题发表过公开演讲。然而，他创办了《德国数学》（Deutsche Mathematik）杂志，该杂志很快便成为当局眼中最重要的数学期刊。

Along with the other math students, he was forced to attend a lecture given by Ludwig Bieberbach, a hitherto minor member of the faculty whose enthusiastic espousal of the Nazi party made him the new department chairman. Bieberbach’s lecture dealt with the difference between “Aryan” and “non-Aryan” mathematics. He found that the decadent “non-Aryan” (read Jewish) mathematicians depended upon complicated algebraic notation, whereas “Aryan” mathematicians worked in the nobler and more pure realm of geometric intuition. At the end of the lecture, he called for questions, and a student in the back row asked him why it was that Richard Courant (one of the great Jewish mathematicians in early-twentieth-century Germany) used geometric insights to develop his theories of real analysis. Bieberbach never gave another public lecture on the subject. However, he founded the journal Deutsche Mathematik, which soon became the primary mathematics journal in the eyes of the authorities.

霍夫丁于1940年从大学毕业，当时其他年轻人正值应征入伍的年纪。然而，由于他身份不明，加上芬兰是德国的盟国，他得以免役。他找到了一份在大学间精算科学研究所担任研究助理的工作。他还兼职在一家历史悠久的德国数学期刊的办公室工作，这家期刊与比伯巴赫的期刊不同，出版困难，发行频率也很低。霍夫丁甚至没有去找教职，因为他需要申请正式的德国国籍才能获得教职资格。

Hoeffding finished his studies at the university in 1940, at an age when other young men were being conscripted into the army. However, his ambiguous citizenship and the fact that Finland was an ally of Germany meant that he was exempted. He took a job as a research assistant with an interuniversity institute for actuarial science. He also worked part-time in the offices of one of the older German mathematics journals, a journal that, unlike Bieberbach’s, had difficulty getting paper and was published infrequently. Hoeffding did not even seek a teaching job, since he would need to apply for formal German citizenship to be eligible.

1944年，非德国籍的“具有德国血统或相关血统”公民被规定必须服兵役。然而，霍夫丁在体检中被诊断出患有糖尿病，因此免除了兵役。他现在可以参加劳动服务。他兼职工作的期刊编辑哈拉尔德·盖珀特建议他从事一些与军事应用相关的数学研究。盖珀特是在另一位编辑赫尔曼·施密德也在场的情况下提出这个建议的。霍夫丁犹豫了一下，但相信盖珀特会谨慎行事，于是告诉盖珀特，任何与战争相关的工作都违背他的良心。施密德出身于普鲁士贵族家庭，霍夫丁希望他的荣誉感能够让这次谈话保密。

In 1944, non-German citizens “of German or related blood” were declared subject to military service. However, on his physical examination, Hoeffding was found to have diabetes and was excused from the army. He was now eligible for labor service. Harald Geppert, the editor of the journal where he worked part-time, suggested that he do some sort of mathematical work with military applications. He made the suggestion while another editor, Hermann Schmid, was in the room. Hoeffding hesitated and then, trusting of Geppert’s discretion, told Geppert that any kind of war work would be contrary to his conscience. Schmid was a member of a noble Prussian family, and Hoeffding hoped that his sense of honor would keep this conversation private.

接下来的几天，瓦西里·霍夫丁（Wassily Hoeffding）焦急万分，但幸运的是他安然无恙，被允许继续工作。随着俄军逼近，一天早晨，盖珀特（Geppert）给年幼的儿子喂了毒药当早餐，然后他和妻子也服毒自尽。1945年2月，霍夫丁和母亲逃往汉诺威的一个小镇，在那里，他们亲眼目睹了该镇被划入英国占领区。他的父亲则留在柏林，被苏联秘密警察逮捕，因为他曾为美国驻丹麦商务专员工作，被认定为间谍。霍夫丁一家对他的命运一无所知，直到几年后，他的父亲设法越狱，辗转来到西方。与此同时，年轻的霍夫丁于1946年秋天来到纽约继续学业，后来受邀加入北卡罗来纳大学的教职队伍。

Wassily Hoeffding sweated out the next few days, but nothing happened to him, and he was allowed to continue his work. As the Russian army approached, Geppert fed poison to his young son for breakfast one morning and then he and his wife took poison. In February 1945, Hoeffding fled with his mother to a small town in Hannover, and were there when it became part of the British zone of occupation. His father remained behind in Berlin, where he was captured by the Russian secret police who considered him a spy, because he had once worked for the American commercial attaché in Denmark. The family remained ignorant of his fate for several years, until his father managed to escape from prison and make his way to the West. In the meantime, young Hoeffding came to New York in the fall of 1946 to continue his studies and was later invited to join the faculty at the University of North Carolina.

运筹学

OPERATIONS RESEARCH

纳粹的反智主义和反犹主义导致二战盟军得以招募到大量杰出的科学家和数学家，为战争贡献力量。英国生物学家彼得·布莱克特向海军部提议，军队可以利用科学家来解决战略和战术问题。科学家，无论其专业领域如何，都接受过运用逻辑和数学模型解决问题的训练。他建议组建科学家团队，专门研究与战争相关的问题。由此，运筹学（在美国被称为作战研究）这门学科应运而生。来自不同领域的科学家团队合作，共同研究如何最佳利用远程轰炸机打击潜艇、如何为防空炮提供射击表、如何确定前线后方弹药库的最佳位置，甚至如何解决部队的粮食供应问题。

One consequence of the Nazi’s anti-intellectualism and antiSemitism was that the World War II Allies reaped a harvest of brilliant scientists and mathematicians to help in their war effort. The British biologist Peter Blackett proposed to the Admiralty that the armed forces could use scientists to solve their strategic and tactical problems. Scientists, regardless of their field of expertise, are trained to apply logic and mathematical models to problems. He proposed that teams of scientists be put to work on war-related problems. Thus was born the discipline of operational research (called operations research in the United States). Teams of scientists from different fields combined to determine the best use of long-range bombers against submarines, to provide firing tables for antiaircraft guns, to determine the best placement of ammunition depots behind front lines, and even to solve questions involving food supplies for troops.

二战结束后，运筹学从战场走向了商业领域。参战的科学家们展示了如何运用数学模型和科学思维来解决战争中的战术问题。同样的方法和许多相同的技巧也适用于工厂车间的作业组织、仓库与销售区域之间的最佳布局，以及解决许多其他涉及平衡有限资源或提高生产效率和产量的商业问题。自那时起，大多数大型企业都设立了运筹学部门。这些部门的大部分工作都涉及统计模型。在辉瑞公司工作期间，我参与了多个旨在改进药物研发管理和新产品上市测试流程的项目。所有这些工作的一个重要工具就是在必要时运用正态分布。

Operations research moved from the battlefield to the business world at the end of the war. The scientists who enlisted in the war showed how mathematical models and scientific thinking could be used to solve tactical problems in warfare. The same approach and many of the same methods could be used in organizing work on a factory floor, finding optimum relationships between warehouses and salesrooms, and in solving many other business problems that involved balancing limited resources or improving production and output. Since that time, operations research departments have been established in most large corporations. Much of the work done by these departments involves statistical models. While at Pfizer, Inc., I worked on several projects aimed at improving the way drug research was managed and new products were brought forward for testing. An important tool in all this work is the ability to call forth the normal distribution when conditions warrant it.

第十章​

CHAPTER 10

检验拟合优度

TESTING THE GOODNESS OF FIT

D20世纪80年代，一种新型数学模型出现并引起了公众的广泛关注，这主要是因为它的名称：“混沌理论”。[11]这个名称暗示着某种统计建模，但其中却包含着一种极其狂野的随机性。创造这个名称的人特意避开了“随机”一词。事实上，混沌理论试图通过在更精细的层面上复兴决定论来逆转统计革命。

During the 1980s a new type of mathematical model emerged and caught the public imagination, primarily because of its name: “chaos theory.”[11] The name suggests some form of statistical modeling with a particularly wild type of randomness. The people who coined the name purposely stayed away from using the word random. Chaos theory is actually an attempt to undo the statistical revolution by reviving determinism at a more sophisticated level.

回想一下，在统计革命之前，科学研究的“对象”要么是测量结果，要么是产生这些测量结果的物理事件。而随着统计革命的到来，科学研究的对象变成了支配测量结果分布的参数。

Recall that, before the statistical revolution, the “things” with which science dealt were either the measurements made or the physical events that generated those measurements. With the statistical revolution, the things of science became the parameters that governed the distribution of the measurements.

在早期的确定性方法中，人们始终认为更精确的测量能够更好地定义所研究的物理现实。而在统计方法中，分布的参数有时并不需要具有物理现实性，无论测量系统多么精确，它们都只能被估计，且存在误差。例如，在确定性方法中，存在一个固定的数值——万有引力常数——来描述物体落向地球的规律。而在统计方法中，我们对万有引力常数的测量结果总是会存在差异，我们希望确定的是这些测量结果分布的离散程度，以此来“理解”物体的下落过程。

In the earlier deterministic approach, there was always the belief that more refined measurements would lead to a better definition of the physical reality being examined. In the statistical approach, the parameters of the distribution are sometimes not required to have a physical reality and can only be estimated with error, regardless of how precise the measuring system. For example, in the deterministic approach, there is a fixed number, the gravitational constant, that describes how things fall to the Earth. In the statistical approach, our measurements of the gravitational constant will always differ from one another, and the scatter of their distribution is what we wish to establish in order to “understand” falling bodies.

1963年，混沌理论家爱德华·洛伦兹发表了一篇题为《巴西蝴蝶扇动翅膀会在德克萨斯州引发龙卷风吗？》的著名演讲，该演讲至今仍被广泛引用。洛伦兹的主要观点是，混沌数学函数对初始条件非常敏感。初始条件的微小差异，经过多次迭代后，会导致截然不同的结果。洛伦兹认为，正是这种对初始微小差异的敏感性，使得他提出的问题无法得到解答。洛伦兹演讲的理论基础是决定论，即每个初始条件在理论上都可以被追溯为最终结果的原因。这一被称为“蝴蝶效应”的观点，被混沌理论的普及者奉为至理名言。

In 1963, the chaos theorist Edward Lorenz presented an often-referenced lecture entitled “Does the Flap of a Butterfly’s Wings in Brazil Set Off a Tornado in Texas?” Lorenz’s main point was that chaotic mathematical functions are very sensitive to initial conditions. Slight differences in initial conditions can lead to dramatically different results after many iterations. Lorenz believed that this sensitivity to slight differences in the beginning made it impossible to determine an answer to his question. Underlying Lorenz’s lecture was the assumption of determinism, that each initial condition can theoretically be traced as a cause of a final effect. This idea, called the “Butterfly Effect,” has been taken by the popularizers of chaos theory as a deep and wise truth.

然而，并没有科学证据证明这种因果关系的存在。也没有任何成熟的数学模型能够解释这种现象。这是一种信仰，其科学有效性与关于魔鬼或上帝的论断无异。定义科学探索的统计模型，即以分布参数来描述科学，同样也是基于对现实本质的信仰。我的科研经验使我相信，这种基于统计学的信仰论断比决定论的论断更有可能是正确的。

However, there is no scientific proof that such a cause and effect exists. There are no well-established mathematical models of reality that suggest such an effect. It is a statement of faith. It has as much scientific validity as statements about demons or God. The statistical model that defines the quest of science in terms of parameters of distributions is also based on a statement of faith about the nature of reality. My experience in scientific research has led me to believe that the statistical statement of faith is more likely to be true than the deterministic one.

混沌理论与拟合优度

CHAOS THEORY AND GOODNESS OF FIT

混沌理论源于这样的观察：由固定的确定性公式生成的数字可能呈现出随机模式。一群数学家在绘制一些相对简单的迭代公式的输出图时发现了这一点。在第九章中，我将迭代公式描述为：它先生成一个数字，然后用这个数字代入方程生成另一个数字。第二个数字又被用来生成第三个数字，以此类推。二十世纪初，法国数学家亨利·庞加莱试图通过绘制连续的微分方程组数字对来理解复杂的微分方程组。庞加莱在这些图中发现了一些有趣的模式，但他没有找到利用这些模式的方法，于是放弃了这个想法。混沌理论正是从这些庞加莱图开始的。绘制庞加莱图时，图纸上的点最初看起来似乎没有任何结构。它们以看似随意的方式出现在不同的位置。然而，随着图中点数的增加，一些模式开始显现。它们有时是一组组平行的直线，也可能是一组相交的直线、圆，或者是由直线穿过的圆。

Chaos theory results from the observation that numbers generated by a fixed deterministic formula can appear to have a random pattern. This was seen when a group of mathematicians took some relatively simple iterative formulas and plotted the output. In chapter 9, I described an iterative formula as one that produces a number, then uses that number in its equations to produce another number. The second number is used to produce a third number, and so on. In the early years of the twentieth century, the French mathematician Henri Poincaré tried to understand complicated families of differential equations by plotting successive pairs of such numbers on a graph. Poincaré found some interesting patterns in those plots, but he did not see how to exploit those patterns and dropped the idea. Chaos theory starts with these Poincaré plots. What happens as you build a Poincaré plot is that the points on the graph paper appear, at first, as if they have no structure to them. They appear in various places in a seemingly haphazard fashion. As the number of points in the plot increases, however, patterns begin to emerge. They are sometimes groups of parallel straight lines. They might also be a set of intersecting lines, or circles, or circles with straight lines across them.

混沌理论的支持者认为，现实生活中看似纯粹随机的测量结果，实际上是由一组确定性的方程生成的，而这些方程可以从庞加莱图中的模式推导出来。例如，一些混沌理论的支持者将人类心跳的时间间隔绘制成庞加莱图。他们声称在这些图中发现了某种模式，并且找到了能够产生相同模式的确定性生成方程。

The proponents of chaos theory suggest that what in real life appear to be purely random measurements are, in fact, generated by some deterministic set of equations, and that these equations can be deduced from the patterns that appear in a Poincaré plot. For instance, some proponents of chaos theory have taken the times between human heartbeats and put them into Poincaré plots. They claim to see patterns in these plots, and they have found deterministic generating equations that appear to produce the same type of pattern.

截至本文撰写之时，以这种方式应用混沌理论存在一个重大缺陷。它无法衡量基于数据绘制的图表与由特定方程组生成的图表之间的拟合程度。证明所提出的生成器正确性的方法，仅仅是让读者观察两个相似的图表。然而，这种目测法在统计分析中已被证明是不可靠的。那些看起来相似或非常接近的事物，在使用专门为此目的开发的统计工具进行仔细分析时，往往会发现它们之间存在显著差异。

As of this writing, there is one major weakness to chaos theory applied in this fashion. There is no measure of how good the fit is between the plot based on data and the plot generated by a specific set of equations. The proof that the proposed generator is correct is based on asking the reader to look at two similar graphs. This eyeball test has proved to be a fallible one in statistical analysis. Those things that seem to the eye to be similar or very close to the same are often drastically different when examined carefully with statistical tools developed for this purpose.

皮尔逊拟合优度检验

PEARSON’S GOODNESS OF FIT TEST

卡尔·皮尔逊在其职业生涯早期就意识到了这个问题。皮尔逊的一项伟大成就便是创建了第一个“拟合优度检验”。通过比较观测值和预测值，皮尔逊得到了一个用于检验拟合优度的统计量。他将这个检验统计量称为“卡方拟合优度检验”。他使用了希腊字母χ（χ），因为该检验统计量的分布属于他所定义的偏态分布族——卡方族。实际上，该检验统计量的行为类似于卡方的平方，因此得名“卡方”。由于这是一个费舍尔意义上的统计量，它具有概率分布。皮尔逊证明，无论使用何种类型的数据，卡方拟合优度检验的分布都相同。也就是说，他可以列出该统计量的概率分布表，并将同一组表格用于所有检验。卡方拟合优度检验只有一个参数，费舍尔称之为“自由度”。在1922年他首次批评皮尔逊工作的论文中，费舍尔指出，在比较两个比例的情况下，皮尔逊对该参数的值判断错误。

This problem was one that Karl Pearson recognized early in his career. One of Pearson’s great achievements was the creation of the first “goodness of fit test.” By comparing the observed to the predicted values, Pearson was able to produce a statistic that tested the goodness of the fit. He called his test statistic a “chi square goodness of fit test.” He used the Greek letter chi (X), since the distribution of this test statistic belonged to a group of his skew distributions that he had designated the chi family. Actually, the test statistic behaved like the square of a chi, thus the name “chi squared.” Since this is a statistic in Fisher’s sense, it has a probability distribution. Pearson proved that the chi square goodness of fit test has a distribution that is the same, regardless of the type of data used. That is, he could tabulate the probability distribution of this statistic and use that same set of tables for every test. The chi square goodness of fit test has a single parameter, which Fisher was to call the “degrees of freedom.” In the 1922 paper in which he first criticized Pearson’s work, Fisher showed that, for the case of comparing two proportions, Pearson had gotten the value of that parameter wrong.

但仅仅因为皮尔逊在其理论的某个小方面犯了错误，并不意味着就应该贬低他的伟大成就。皮尔逊的拟合优度检验是现代统计分析的一个重要组成部分的先驱。这个组成部分被称为“假设检验”或“显著性检验”。它允许分析人员提出两个或多个相互竞争的数学模型来解释现实，并利用数据来否定其中一个模型。假设检验的应用如此广泛，以至于许多科学家认为它是他们唯一可用的统计方法。正如我们将在后面的章节中看到的，假设检验的使用涉及一些严重的哲学问题。

But just because he made a mistake in one small aspect of his theory is no reason to denigrate Pearson’s great achievement. Pearson’s goodness of fit test was the forerunner of a major component of modern statistical analysis. This component is called “hypothesis testing,” or “significance testing.” It allows the analyst to propose two or more competing mathematical models for reality and use the data to reject one of them. Hypothesis testing is so widely used that many scientists think of it as the only statistical procedure available to them. The use of hypothesis testing, as we shall see in later chapters, involves some serious philosophical problems.

测试这位女士能否尝出茶的味道有何不同

TESTING WHETHER THE LADY CAN TASTE A DIFFERENCE IN THE TEA

假设我们想测试这位女士能否分辨出先将牛奶倒入茶中的茶和先将茶倒入牛奶中的茶之间的区别。我们给她两个杯子，告诉她其中一个是先将牛奶倒入茶中的茶，另一个是先将牛奶倒入茶中的茶。她品尝后正确地辨别了两个杯子。她完全有可能是靠猜的；她猜对的概率是50%。我们又给她两个同样的杯子。她再次正确辨别。如果她只是靠猜，那么连续两次都正确辨别的概率是1/4。我们给她第三个杯子，她再次正确辨别。这次完全靠猜的概率是1/8。我们继续给她更多杯子，她仍然能正确辨别。在某个时刻，我们需要确信她能够分辨出这两种情况。假设她有一对杯子辨别错误。假设这是第二十四对，而她之前都答对了。我们还能得出她能够分辨差异的结论吗？假设她在二十四对中答错了四对呢？五对呢？

Suppose we wish to test whether the lady can detect the difference between a cup of tea into which the milk has been poured into the tea versus a cup of tea wherein the tea has been poured into the milk. We present her with two cups, telling her that one of the cups is tea into milk and the other is milk into tea. She tastes and identifies the cups correctly. She could have done this by guessing; she had a 50:50 chance of guessing correctly. We present her with another pair of the same type. Again, she identifies them correctly. If she were just guessing, the chance of this happening twice in a row is ¼. We present her with a third pair of cups, and again she identifies them correctly. The chance that this has happened as a result of pure guesswork is 1/8. We present her with more pairs, and she keeps identifying the cups correctly. At some point, we have to be convinced that she can tell the difference. Suppose she was wrong with one pair. Suppose further that this was the twenty-fourth pair and she was correct on all the others. Can we still conclude that she is able to detect a difference? Suppose she was wrong in four out of the twenty-four? Five of the twenty-four?

假设检验（或显著性检验）是一种正式的统计程序，它计算在假设待检验的假设为真的情况下，我们观察到的现象发生的概率。当观察到的概率非常低时，我们得出结论：该假设不成立。一个重要的点是，假设检验提供了一种拒绝假设的工具。在上述案例中，该假设是这位女士只是在猜测。即使与该假设相关的概率非常高，假设检验也不允许我们接受该假设。

Hypothesis, or significance, testing is a formal statistical procedure that calculates the probability of what we have observed, assuming that the hypothesis to be tested is true. When the observed probability is very low, we conclude that the hypothesis is not true. One important point is that hypothesis testing provides a tool for rejecting a hypothesis. In the case above, this is the hypothesis that the lady is only guessing. It does not allow us to accept a hypothesis, even if the probability associated with that hypothesis is very high.

在这一总体概念发展的早期阶段， “显著性”（significant）一词开始被用来表示概率低到足以拒绝原假设。如果数据能够被用来拒绝一个既定的分布，那么这些数据就具有显著性。该词最初使用其19世纪末英语中的含义，即计算结果具有意义或表明某种事物。随着英语进入20世纪，“显著性”一词开始衍生出其他含义，最终发展成现在的含义，暗示着非常重要的事物。统计分析仍然使用“显著性”一词来表示在所检验的假设下计算出的极低概率。在这种语境下，该词具有精确的数学含义。然而，遗憾的是，统计分析的使用者常常将显著性检验统计量理解为更接近该词现代含义的含义。

Somewhere early in the development of this general idea, the word significant came to be used to indicate that the probability was low enough for rejection. Data became significant if they could be used to reject a proposed distribution. The word was used in its late-nineteenth-century English meaning, which is simply that the computation signified or showed something. As the English language entered the twentieth century, the word significant began to take on other meanings, until it developed its current meaning, implying something very important. Statistical analysis still uses the word significant to indicate a very low probability computed under the hypothesis being tested. In that context, the word has an exact mathematical meaning. Unfortunately, those who use statistical analysis often treat a significant test statistic as implying something much closer to the modern meaning of the word.

费舍尔对p值的运用

FISHER’S USE OF P-VALUES

R.A. Fisher 开发了目前普遍使用的绝大多数显著性检验方法。他将能够判定结果是否显著的概率称为“p 值”。他对 p 值的含义和实用性毫不怀疑。《科研人员统计方法》一书的大部分篇幅都致力于讲解如何计算 p 值。正如我之前提到的，这本书是为那些希望使用统计方法的非数学专业人士编写的。在书中，Fisher 并没有描述这些检验是如何推导出来的，也从未明确指出什么样的 p 值才算显著。相反，他展示了一些计算示例，并指出结果是否显著。在一个例子中，他展示了 p 值小于 0.01，并指出：“一百个值中只有一个会偶然超过[计算出的检验统计量]，因此结果之间的差异显然是显著的。”

R. A. Fisher developed most of the significance testing methods now in general use. He referred to the probability that allows one to declare significance as the “p-value.” He had no doubts about its meaning or usefulness. Much of Statistical Methods for Research Workers is devoted to showing how to calculate p-values. As I noted earlier, this was a book designed for nonmathematicians who want to use statistical methods. In it, Fisher does not describe how these tests were derived, and he never indicates exactly what p-value one might call significant. Instead, he displays examples of calculations and notes whether the result is significant or not. In one example, he shows that the p-value is less than .01 and states: “Only one value in a hundred will exceed [the calculated test statistic] by chance, so that the difference between the results is clearly significant.”

他最接近于定义一个在所有情况下都具有显著性的特定p值，是在1929年发表于《心灵研究学会会刊》的一篇文章中。心灵研究指的是试图通过科学方法来证明透视能力的存在。心灵研究者广泛运用统计显著性检验来证明，他们的结果不太可能是受试者纯粹随机猜测的结果。在这篇文章中，费舍尔谴责一些作者未能正确使用显著性检验。然后他指出：

The closest he came to defining a specific p-value that would be significant in all circumstances occurred in an article printed in the Proceedings of the Society for Psychical Research in 1929.
