关于个人概率存在不同的观点。一种极端观点是萨维奇-德·菲内蒂的理论，该理论认为每个人都有自己独特的概率集合。另一种极端观点是凯恩斯的理论，该理论认为概率是指特定文化背景下受过教育的人可能持有的信念程度。在凯恩斯看来，特定文化背景下的所有人（萨维奇所说的“科学家”或“中国农民”）都可以就某个特定陈述的普遍概率水平达成共识。由于这种概率水平取决于文化和时代，因此，从某种绝对意义上讲，这种适当的概率水平很可能是错误的。

There are different versions of personal probability. At one extreme is the Savage-de Finetti approach, which says that each person has his or her own unique set of probabilities. At the other extreme is Keynes’s view that probability is the degree of belief that an educated person in a given culture can be expected to hold. In Keynes’s view, all people in a given culture (Savage’s “scientists” or “Chinese peasants”) can agree on a general level of probability that holds for a given statement. Because this level of probability depends upon the culture and time, it is very possible that the appropriate level of probability is wrong in some absolute sense.

萨维奇和德·菲内蒂提出，每个人都有一套特定的个人概率，他们描述了如何通过一种被称为“标准赌博”的技术来获得这些概率。为了使整个文化共享一套给定的概率，凯恩斯不得不弱化概率的数学定义，不再将其视为一个精确的数字（例如 67%），而是将其视为一种排序观念的方法（明天下雨的概率大于下雪的概率）。

Savage and de Finetti proposed that each individual has a specific set of personal probabilities, and they described how such probabilities might be elicited via a technique known as the “standard gamble.” In order for an entire culture to share a given set of probabilities, Keynes had to weaken the mathematical definition and refer to probability not so much as a precise number (like 67 percent) but more as a method of ordering ideas (the probability that it will rain tomorrow is greater than the probability that it will snow).

无论个人概率的概念如何精确定义，贝叶斯定理在个人概率中的应用方式似乎与大多数人的思维方式相符。贝叶斯方法首先假设某人心中存在一组先验概率。然后，此人进行观察或实验并产生数据。之后，这些数据被用来修正先验概率，从而产生一组后验概率：

Regardless of how the concept of personal probability is defined exactly, the way in which Bayes’s theorem is used in personal probability seems to match the way in which most people think. The Bayesian approach is to start with a prior set of probabilities in the mind of a given person. Next, that person observes or experiments and produces data. The data are then used to modify the prior probabilities, producing a posterior set of probabilities:

先验概率 → 数据 → 后验概率

prior probability → data → posterior probability

假设某人想要确定所有乌鸦是否都是黑色的。她首先对这个结论的概率有所了解。例如，她可能一开始对乌鸦一无所知，并假设所有乌鸦都是黑色的概率均为 50%。数据由她对乌鸦的观察结果构成。假设她看到一只乌鸦，并观察到它是黑色的，那么她的后验概率就会增加。下次她观察乌鸦时，她的新先验概率（即之前的后验概率）将大于 50%，并且由于观察到了另一组乌鸦（它们都是黑色的），这个概率还会进一步增加。

Suppose the person wishes to determine whether all ravens are black. She starts with some prior knowledge about the probability that this is true. For instance, she may know nothing about ravens at first and starts with equipose, 50:50, that all ravens are black. The data consist of her observations of ravens. Suppose she sees a raven and observes that it is black, her posterior probability is increased. The next time she observes ravens, her new prior (the old posterior) is greater than 50 percent and is further increased by observing a new set of ravens, all of which are black.

另一方面，一个人可能带着非常强烈的先验观念进入决策过程，这种观念如此根深蒂固，以至于需要海量数据才能克服。在20世纪80年代宾夕法尼亚州三里岛核电站险些酿成灾难的事件中，反应堆操作员使用一块巨大的仪表盘来监控反应堆的运行状况。其中包括一些警示灯，其中一些警示灯过去曾出现故障并发出过误报。操作员的先验观念是，任何新的警示灯都会被视为误报。即使警示灯和相关仪表盘的显示模式始终表明反应堆水位过低，他们仍然置之不理。他们的先验观念如此根深蒂固，以至于数据并没有显著改变他们的后验观念。

On the other hand, a person can enter the process with a very strong prior, so strong that it may take massive amounts of data to overcome it. In the near disaster of American nuclear power at the Three Mile Island power plant in Pennsylvania in the 1980s, the operators of the reactor had a large board of dials and indicators to follow the progress of the reactor. Among these were warning lights, some of which had been faulty and presented false alarms in the past. The prior beliefs of the operators were such that any new warning light would be viewed as a false alarm. Even as the pattern of warning lights and associated dials produced a consistent picture of low water in the reactor, they continued to dismiss the evidence. Their prior was so strong that the data did not change the posterior very much.

假设只有两种可能性，就像一篇有争议的《联邦党人文集》文章那样：它要么是麦迪逊写的，要么是汉密尔顿写的。那么，应用贝叶斯定理，就能得出先验概率和后验概率之间的简单关系，并将数据概括为一个称为“贝叶斯因子”的量。这是一个数学计算，它完全不参考先验概率来描述数据。有了贝叶斯因子，分析人员就可以告诉读者，他们可以代入任何他们想要的先验概率，将其乘以计算出的贝叶斯因子，然后计算后验概率。莫斯特勒和华莱士对十二篇有争议的《联邦党人文集》文章都进行了这样的计算。

Suppose there are only two possibilities, as was the case for a disputed Federalist paper: It was written either by Madison or by Hamilton. Then, the application of Bayes’s theorem leads to a simple relationship between the prior odds and the posterior odds where the data can be summarized into something called the “Bayes factor.” This is a mathematical calculation that characterizes the data without reference to the prior odds at all. With this in hand, the analyst can tell the readers to insert whatever prior odds they wish, multiply it by the computed Bayes factor, and compute the posterior odds. Mosteller and Wallace did this for each of the twelve disputed Federalist papers.

他们还对无内容词的出现频率进行了两项非贝叶斯分析。这使他们获得了四种确定争议论文作者身份的方法：层级贝叶斯模型、计算贝叶斯因子以及这两项非贝叶斯分析。结果如何？所有十二篇论文都以压倒性优势判给了麦迪逊。事实上，使用计算贝叶斯因子，对于某些论文，读者必须先验概率超过十万比一，才能得出汉密尔顿的后验概率为50:50。

They also ran two non-Bayesian analyses of the frequency of contentless words. This gave them four methods for determining the authorship of the disputed papers: the hierarchal Bayes model, the computed Bayes factor, and the two non-Bayesian analyses. How did the results come out? All twelve were overwhelmingly awarded to Madison. In fact, using the computed Bayes factors, for some of the papers the reader would have to have had prior odds greater than 100,000 to 1 in favor of Hamilton in order to produce posterior odds of 50:50.

C第十四章

CHAPTER 14

数学界的莫扎特

THE MOZART OF MATHEMATICS

拉在二十世纪统计方法的发展中，A. Fisher 并不是唯一的领军人物。比 Fisher 小十三岁的 Andrei Nikolaevich Kolmogorov 于 1987 年去世，享年 85 岁，他在数理统计和概率论领域留下了深刻的印记，他的成就建立在 Fisher 的一些工作之上，但在数学的深度和细节方面超越了 Fisher。

RA. Fisher was not the only presiding genius when it came to the development of statistical methods in the twentieth . century. Andrei Nikolaevich Kolmogorov, who was thirteen years younger than Fisher and died at age eighty-five in 1987, left an imprint on mathematical statistics and probability theory that built on some of Fisher’s work but that exceeds Fisher’s in the depth and detail of the mathematics.

但或许与他对科学的贡献同样重要的是，这位杰出人物对认识他的人产生了深远的影响。他的学生阿尔伯特·N·希里亚耶夫在1991年写道：

But perhaps as important as his contributions to science was the impact this remarkable man had on those who knew him. His student Albert N. Shiryaev wrote in 1991:

安德烈·尼古拉耶维奇·科尔莫戈罗夫属于少数几位让人感觉仿佛接触到了非凡之人、伟大而卓越的人物，仿佛邂逅了一位奇迹。科尔莫戈罗夫的一切都非同寻常：他的一生，他的求学经历，他在数学、气象学、流体动力学、历史学、语言学和教育学等领域的开创性发现。他的兴趣爱好极其广泛，涵盖音乐、建筑、诗歌和旅行。他的博学也非同寻常；似乎他对万事万物都有着独到的见解……与科尔莫戈罗夫见面，哪怕只是简单的交谈，都会给人一种非同寻常的感觉。你会感觉到他始终保持着高度活跃的思维状态。

Andrei Nikolaevich Kolmogorov belonged to a select group of people who left one with the feeling of having touched someone unusual, someone great and extraordinary, the feeling of having met a wonder. Everything about Kolmogorov was unusual: his entire life, his school and college years, his pioneering discoveries in … mathematics … meteorology, hydrodynamics, history, linguistics and pedagogy. His interests were unusually diverse, including music, architecture, poetry, and travel. His erudition was unusual; it seemed as if he had an educated opinion about everything … . One’s feeling after meeting Kolmogorov, after a simple conversation with him, [was] unusual. One sensed that he had a continuously intensive brain activity.

科尔莫戈罗夫出生于1903年，当时他的母亲正从克里米亚返回位于俄罗斯南部图诺什纳村的家乡，却不幸难产去世。他的一位传记作者委婉地写道：“他是父母未正式结婚的儿子。”玛丽娅·雅科夫列夫娜·科尔莫戈罗娃在怀孕晚期被男友抛弃后，正返回家乡。在阵痛难忍之际，她在坦波夫镇被人从火车上抱了下来。她在那里生下孩子，然后独自一人死在了这座陌生的城市。只有她年幼的儿子回到了图诺什纳。他由母亲的未婚姐妹抚养长大。其中一位，薇拉·雅科夫列夫娜，成为了他的代孕母亲。他的姑姑们在村里为小安德烈和他的朋友们开办了一所小型学校。她们还出版了一份名为《春燕》的家庭杂志，刊登了他的第一篇文学作品。五岁时，他做出了他的第一个数学发现（也发表在《春燕》杂志上）。他发现前k个奇数的和等于k的平方。随着年龄的增长，他会向同学们出题，这些题目和答案都被刊登在《春燕》杂志上。其中一个例子是：用多少种方法缝一个有四个孔的纽扣？

Kolmogorov was born in 1903 while his mother was on a journey from the Crimea to her home in the village of Tunoshna in southern Russia; she died in childbirth. One of his biographers delicately states: “He was the son of parents not formally married.” Mariya Yakovlevna Kolmogorova was returning home in the final stages of pregnancy, having been abandoned by her boyfriend. In the throes of birth pangs, she was taken off the train at the town of Tambov. There she gave birth and then died alone in a strange town. Only her infant son came back to Tunoshna. His mother’s maiden sisters raised him. One of them, Vera Yakovlevna, became his surrogate mother. His aunts ran a small school for young Andrei and his friends in their village. They printed a house magazine, Spring Swallows, in which they published the first of his literary efforts. At age five, he made his first mathematical discovery (which appeared in Spring Swallows). He found that the sum of the first k odd numbers was equal to the square of k. As he grew older, he would pose problems to his classmates, and the problems and their solutions were printed in Spring Swallows. One example of these problems was: In how many ways is it possible to sew on a button with four holes?

十四岁时，柯尔莫戈罗夫通过百科全书自学高等数学，并填补了其中缺失的证明。中学时，他为一系列永动机设计图纸，令年轻的物理老师十分头疼。这些图纸设计得如此巧妙，以至于老师根本无法发现其中的错误（而柯尔莫戈罗夫早已将其巧妙地隐藏起来）。他决定提前一年参加中学毕业考试。他告知了老师，被要求午饭后返回，于是便出去散步。回来后，考试委员会竟然没有给他考试，就直接把证书发给了他。他后来告诉希里亚耶夫，这是他一生中最失望的事情之一；他原本期待的是智力上的挑战。

At fourteen, Kolmogorov learned higher mathematics from an encyclopedia, filling in the missing proofs. In secondary school, he frustrated his young physics teacher by creating plans for a series of perpetual motion machines. The plans were so clever that the teacher was unable to discover the errors (which Kolmogorov had carefully concealed). He decided to take the final examinations for his secondary school a year early. He informed his teachers, was asked to return after lunch, and went for a walk. When he returned, the examination board gave him his certificate without testing him. He recalled to Shiryaev that this was one of the greatest disappointments of his life; he had looked forward to the intellectual challenge.

1920年，17岁的科尔莫戈罗夫来到莫斯科，进入大学学习。他最初是数学专业的学生，​​但后来也旁听了冶金等其他领域的课程，并参加了一个俄罗斯历史研讨会。在研讨会上，他发表了自己的第一篇研究论文，探讨了15至16世纪诺夫哥罗德的土地所有权制度。他的教授批评了这篇论文，认为科尔莫戈罗夫没有提供足够的证据来支持他的论点。多年后，一次对该地区进行的考古探险证实了科尔莫戈罗夫的推测。

Kolmogorov arrived in Moscow in 1920, at age seventeen, to attend the university. He entered as a student in mathematics but went to lectures in other fields like metallurgy, and took part in a seminar in Russian history. As a part of that seminar, he presented his first piece of research to be published, an examination of landholding in Novgorod in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. His professor criticized the paper because he did not think Kolmogorov had provided enough proof of his thesis. An archeological expedition to the region years later confirmed Kolmogorov’s conjectures.

在莫斯科国立大学读本科时，他曾在中学兼职教书，并积极参与各种课外活动。之后，他继续在莫斯科攻读数学研究生。数学系要求学生修读十四门基础课程。学生可以选择参加课程的期末考试，或者提交一篇原创论文。很少有学生会提交多篇论文。科尔莫戈罗夫从未参加过考试。他准备了十四篇论文，每篇都包含精彩绝伦的原创成果。“其中一篇论文后来被证明是错误的，”他回忆道，“但我后来才意识到这一点。”

As an undergraduate at Moscow State University, he worked part-time teaching in a secondary school and joined in a large number of extracurricular activities. He went on to graduate studies in mathematics at Moscow. There was a basic set of fourteen courses required by the department. The students had the choice of taking a final exam in a given course or presenting an original paper. Few students attempted more than one paper. Kolmogorov never took an exam. He prepared fourteen papers with brilliant, original results in each one. “One of these results turned out to be false,” he recalled, “but I realized it only later.”

杰出的数学家柯尔莫戈罗夫凭借在德国期刊上发表的一系列杰出论文和著作，为西方科学界所熟知。20世纪30年代，他甚至获准参加在德国和斯堪的纳维亚半岛举行的一些数学会议。然而，在二战期间及战后，柯尔莫戈罗夫本人却消失在了斯大林的铁幕之后。1938年，他发表了一篇论文，建立了平滑和预测平稳随机过程的基本定理。（这项工作将在本章后面详细介绍。）诺伯特·维纳在麻省理工学院从事相关研究，致力于将这些方法应用于军事问题，他对战争的保密性发表了一番有趣的评论。这些成果对美国的冷战至关重要，以至于维纳的工作被列为绝密。但维纳坚称，所有这些结论都可以从柯尔莫戈罗夫早期的论文中推导出来。二战期间，科尔莫戈罗夫忙于将他的理论应用于苏联的战争事业。他一贯谦逊低调，将基本思想归功于R.A.费舍尔，后者曾在遗传学研究中使用过类似的方法。

Kolmogorov, the brilliant mathematician, was known to the scientists of the West through a series of remarkable papers and books published in German journals. He was even allowed to attend a few mathematics conferences in Germany and Scandinavia during the 1930s. Kolmogorov, the man, disappeared behind Stalin’s iron curtain during and after World War II. In 1938, he had published a paper that established the basic theorems for smoothing and predicting stationary stochastic processes. (This work will be described later in the chapter.) An interesting comment on the secrecy of war efforts comes from Norbert Wiener who, at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, worked on applications of these methods to military problems during and after the war. These results were considered so important to America’s Cold War efforts that Wiener’s work was declared top secret. But all of it, Wiener insisted, could have been deduced from Kolmogorov’s early paper. During World War II, Kolmogorov was busy developing applications of the theory for the Soviet war effort. With the simple modesty that marked so much of his accomplishment, he attributed the basic ideas to R. A. Fisher, who had used similar methods in his genetics work.

科尔莫戈罗夫和他的学生们

A. N. Kolmogorov with students

科尔莫戈罗夫，这个人

KOLMOGOROV, THE MAN

1953年斯大林去世后，笼罩在猜忌之上的铁环开始松动。科尔莫戈罗夫其人开始出现在公众视野，参与国际会议，并在俄罗斯组织此类会议。数学界的其他人士也开始认识他。他热情友好、开朗幽默，兴趣广泛，热爱教学。他思维敏捷，总是对所听到的内容进行深入思考。我面前有一张照片，照片拍摄于1963年，地点是第比利斯，照片中的科尔莫戈罗夫正在聆听英国统计学家大卫·肯德尔的讲座。他的眼镜架在鼻梁上，身体前倾，全神贯注地听着讨论。你可以感受到他那充满活力的个性在周围的人群中熠熠生辉。

When Stalin died in 1953, the iron ring of suspicion began to open. Kolmogorov, the man, emerged to participate in international meetings and to organize such meetings in Russia. The rest of the mathematical world now came to know him. He was an eager, friendly, open, and humorous man, with a wide range of interests and a love of teaching. His keen mind was always playing with what he was hearing. I have in front of me a photograph of Kolmogorov in the audience at a lecture being given by the British statistician David Kendall in Tbilisi in 1963. Kolmogorov’s glasses are perched on the end of his nose. He is leaning forward, eagerly following the discussion. You can feel the vibrancy of his personality glowing in the midst of the others sitting around him.

科尔莫戈罗夫最喜欢的活动之一是在莫斯科一所天才儿童学校教书和组织课程。他喜欢向孩子们介绍文学和音乐，并带他们远足和探险。正如大卫·肯德尔所写，他认为每个孩子都应该“全面而自然地发展健全的人格”。“即使他们最终没有成为数学家，他也不会感到担忧。无论他们最终从事什么职业，只要他们的视野开阔、求知欲旺盛，他就感到欣慰。”

Some of Kolmogorov’s favorite activities were teaching and organizing classes at a school for gifted children in Moscow. He enjoyed introducing the children to literature and music. He took them on hikes and expeditions. He felt that each child should have a “broad and natural development of the whole personality,” David Kendall wrote. “It did not worry him if they did not become mathematicians. Whatever profession they ultimately followed, he was content if their outlook remained broad and their curiosity unstifled.”

科尔莫戈罗夫于1942年与安娜·德米特里耶夫娜·叶戈罗娃结婚。他和妻子恩爱相守，直至八十多岁。他酷爱徒步旅行和滑雪，七十多岁时，他经常带领一群年轻人徒步攀登他最爱的山脉，与他们探讨数学、文学、音乐以及人生百态。1971年，他参加了“德米特里·门捷列夫”号科考船的海洋科学考察。他的同代人常常惊叹于他的兴趣爱好和渊博的知识。在与教皇约翰·保罗二世会面时，他与这位体格健壮的教皇聊起了滑雪，并指出十九世纪教皇的体型时而肥胖时而苗条，而约翰·保罗二世是第264任教皇。看来，他对罗马天主教的历史也颇感兴趣。他曾就俄罗斯诗歌的统计文本分析发表演讲，并且能够背诵普希金的长篇诗句。

Kolmogorov was married in 1942 to Anna Dmitrievna Egorova. He and his wife lived in a loving marriage into their eighties. He was an avid hiker and skier, and in his seventies he would take groups of young people hiking the trails of his favorite mountains, discussing mathematics, literature, music, and life in general. In 1971, he joined a scientific expedition exploring the oceans on the research vessel the Dmitri Mendeleev. His contemporaries were constantly amazed at the things that interested him and the knowledge he had. Upon meeting Pope John Paul II, he discussed skiing with this athletic pope, and then pointed out that during the nineteenth century, fat popes had alternated with thin popes and that Pope John Paul II was the 264th pope. It seems that one of his interests was the history of the Roman Catholic Church. He gave lectures on the statistical textual analysis of Russian poetry and could quote long selections from Pushkin by heart.

1953年，莫斯科国立大学组织了一次会议，庆祝科尔莫戈罗夫五十岁生日。名誉教授帕维尔·亚历山德罗夫是发言者之一，他说道：

In 1953, a session was organized at Moscow State University to celebrate Kolmogorov’s fiftieth birthday. Pavel Aleksandrov, professor emeritus, one of the speakers, said:

科尔莫戈罗夫属于这样一群数学家：他们在任何领域的每一项工作都会导致对该领域的彻底重新评估。近年来，很难找到一位数学家，不仅兴趣如此广泛，而且对数学界的影响如此之大……哈代（一位杰出的英国数学家）认为他是三角级数专家，冯·卡门（一位二战后的德国物理学家）认为他是力学专家。哥德尔（一位数学哲学理论家）曾说过，人类天才的精髓在于青春的持久。青春有许多特质，其中之一就是激情。对数学的激情正是科尔莫戈罗夫天才的特征之一。科尔莫戈罗夫的激情体现在他的创造性工作中，体现在他为《苏联大百科全书》撰写的文章中，体现在他发展博士课程的过程中。而这仅仅是他的一面。他的另一面是孜孜不倦的奉献。

Kolmogorov belongs to a group of mathematicians whose every single work in any area leads to a complete reevaluation. It is difficult to find a mathematician in recent years with not only such broad interests, but also such an influence on mathematics … . Hardy [a prominent British mathematician] took him for a specialist in trigonometric series, and von Karman [a post–World War II German physicist] took him for a specialist in mechanics. Gödel [a mathematical-philosophical theoretician] once said that the gist of human genius is the longevity of one’s youth. Youth has several traits, one of which is excitement. Excitement with mathematics is one of the features of Kolmogorov’s genius. Kolmogorov’s excitement is in his creative work, in his articles in the Large Soviet Encyclopedia, in his development of the Ph.D. program. And this is just one side of him. The other is his dedicated labor.

那么，他毕生致力于此，最终取得了哪些成果呢？与其列举科尔莫戈罗夫影响深远的领域，不如列举他在数学、物理、生物学和哲学领域中未曾产生重大影响的领域来得容易。1941年，他创立了现代湍流数学方法。1954年，他研究了行星间的引力相互作用，并找到了一种模拟“不可积”现象的方法，而这些现象一百多年来一直困扰着数学分析。

And what were the results of this dedicated labor? It would be easier to list the fields of mathematics, physics, biology, and philosophy in which Kolmogorov did not have a major influence than it would be to list the areas in which his imprint was felt. In 1941, he founded the modern mathematical approach to turbulent fluid-flow. In 1954, he examined the gravitational interaction among the planets and found a way to model the “non-integrable” aspects that had defied mathematical analysis for over one hundred years.

柯尔莫戈罗夫在数理统计学中的工作

KOLMOGOROV’S WORK IN MATHEMATICAL STATISTICS

在统计学革命中，柯尔莫戈罗夫解决了两个最紧迫的理论问题。在他去世前，他已接近解决一个深刻的数学哲学问题，这个问题直指统计方法的核心。这两个紧迫的问题是：

Regarding the statistical revolution, Kolmogorov solved two of the most pressing theoretical problems. Before he died, he was close to the solution of a deep mathematical-philosophical problem that eats at the heart of statistical methods. The two pressing problems were

  1. 概率论的真正数学基础是什么？
  2. What are the true mathematical foundations of probability?
  3. 我们可以利用随着时间推移收集的数据做什么，例如地震（或地下核爆炸）后地球的震动？
  4. What can be done with data that are collected over time, like the vibrations of the earth following an earthquake (or an underground nuclear explosion)?

当柯尔莫戈罗夫开始研究第一个问题时，概率论在理论数学家中名声不太好。这是因为计算概率的数学技巧是在十八世纪发展起来的，当时人们将其视为巧妙的计数方法（例如，从一副标准扑克牌中抽取三组五张牌，使得只有一组牌是赢家，共有多少种方法？）。这些巧妙的计数方法似乎缺乏统一的理论基础，几乎都是临时性的，为了满足特定需求而创造的。

When Kolmogorov began examining the first question, probability was somewhat in ill repute among theoretical mathematicians. This was because the mathematical techniques of calculating probabilities had been developed during the eighteenth century as clever counting methods (viz., In how many ways can three sets of five cards be drawn from a standard deck so that only one hand is a winner?). These clever counting methods seemed to have no single underlying theoretical structure. They were almost all ad hoc, created to meet specific needs.

对大多数人来说，拥有解决问题的方法就足够了，但对于十九世纪末和二十世纪的数学家而言，坚实严谨的基础理论是确保解题过程无误的必要条件。十八世纪数学家的临时方法虽然有效，但若应用不当，也会导致棘手的悖论。二十世纪初数学的主要工作就是将这些临时方法建立在坚实严谨的数学基础之上。亨利·勒贝格的工作之所以重要（这位勒贝格曾以其数学才华令耶日·内曼印象深刻，但内曼见到他时却发现他粗鲁无礼），主要原因在于他为积分学的临时方法奠定了坚实的基础。由于概率论在十七、十八世纪仍是一项不完善的发明，二十世纪的数学家们对其价值不高（统计方法也包含在这一判断之中）。

For most people, having a method for solving a problem would be adequate, but to late-nineteenth- and twentieth-century mathematicians a solid, rigorous underlying theory was necessary to make sure there would be no errors in those solutions. The ad hoc methods of the eighteenth-century mathematicians had worked but had also led to difficult paradoxes when applied incorrectly. The major work of early-twentieth-century mathematics involved putting those ad hoc methods on solid, rigorous mathematical foundations. The major reason why Henri Lebesgue’s work was important (this was the Lebesgue who so impressed Jerzy Neyman with his mathematics but who turned out to be a discourteous boor when Neyman met him) was that it put the ad hoc methods of integral calculus on a solid foundation. As long as probability theory remained an incomplete seventeenth- and eighteenth-century invention, mathematicians of the twentieth century treated it as something of lesser value (and included statistical methods in that judgment).

柯尔莫戈罗夫思考了概率计算的本质，最终意识到，求事件发生的概率与求不规则图形的面积完全相同。他将新兴的测度论数学应用于概率计算。凭借这些工具，柯尔莫戈罗夫提炼出一组精简的公理，并以此构建了整个概率论体系。这就是柯尔莫戈罗夫的“概率论公理化”。如今，它被视为理解概率的唯一途径，并彻底解决了所有关于概率计算有效性的问题。

Kolmogorov thought about the nature of probability calculations and finally realized that finding the probability of an event was exactly like finding the area of an irregular shape. He adapted the newly emerging mathematics of measure theory to the calculations of probabilities. With these tools, Kolmogorov was able to identify a small set of axioms upon which he could construct the entire body of probability theory. This is Kolmogorov’s “axiomization of probability theory.” It is taught today as the only way to view probability. It settles forever all questions about the validity of those calculations.

在解决了概率论问题之后，柯尔莫戈罗夫着手解决统计方法的下一个重大问题（同时他还忙于教导天才儿童、组织研讨会、管理数学系、解决力学和天文学问题，以及尽情享受生活）。为了使统计计算成为可能，费舍尔和其他统计学家假设所有数据都是独立的。他们把一系列测量数据看作是掷骰子生成的。由于骰子不会记住之前的点数，所以每个新的点数都与之前的数字完全独立。

Having solved the problem of probability theory, Kolmogorov attacked the next major problem of statistical methods (in between teaching gifted children, organizing seminars, running a department of mathematics, solving problems in mechanics and astronomy, and living life to its fullest). To make statistical calculations possible, R. A. Fisher and other statisticians had assumed that all data are independent. They looked on a sequence of measurements as if it had been generated by tossing dice. Since the dice do not remember their previous configuration, each new number was completely independent of the previous ones.

大多数数据并非彼此独立。费舍尔在《科研人员统计方法》一书中举的第一个例子是他新生儿子的每周体重。显然，如果孩子某周体重增长异常，下一周的体重就会反映出来；同样，如果孩子某周生病体重没有增长，下一周的体重也会反映出来。在现实生活中，很难想象有什么数据序列能够保证连续的观测值彼此真正独立。

Most data are not independent of each other. The first example Fisher used in Statistical Methods for Research Workers was the weekly weights of his newborn son. Clearly, if the child gained an unusual amount of weight one week, the next week’s weight would reflect that, or if the child got sick and failed to gain one week, the next week’s weight would reflect that. It is difficult to think of any sequence of data collected over time in real-life situations where successive observations are truly independent.

在费舍尔的《作物变异研究》第三篇（H·费尔菲尔德·史密斯向我介绍的那篇巨著）中，他处理了一系列连续年份的小麦收成测量数据和连续日期的降雨量测量数据。为了解决这个问题，他创建了一组复杂的参数，以解释随时间收集的数据并非相互独立的事实。他找到了一系列有限的解决方案，这些方案依赖于一些可能并不成立的简化假设。费舍尔的研究无法继续深入，也没有人继续跟进他的工作。

In the third of his “Studies in Crop Variation” (the massive paper that H. Fairfield Smith introduced me to), Fisher dealt with a series of wheat harvest measurements taken in successive years and rainfall measurements taken on successive days. He attacked the problem by creating a set of complicated parameters to account for the fact that data collected over time are not independent. He found a limited range of solutions that depended upon simplifying assumptions that may not be true. Fisher was unable to proceed much further, and no one followed up on his work.

直到柯尔莫戈罗夫出现，才有人真正理解了随机过程。他将随着时间推移收集的一系列数字，以及每个后续值与先前值之间的关联性，称为“随机过程”。柯尔莫戈罗夫的开创性论文（发表于二战爆发前夕）为美国诺伯特·维纳、英国乔治·博克斯以及柯尔莫戈罗夫在俄罗斯的学生们的后续研究奠定了基础。正是由于柯尔莫戈罗夫的思想，如今人们才能分析长期积累的记录，并得出非常具体的结论。例如，人们利用加利福尼亚海滩的海浪来定位印度洋的风暴。射电望远镜可以区分不同的信号源（或许有一天，还能截获来自其他星球智慧生命的信息）。人们现在可以判断地震仪记录是由地下核爆炸还是自然地震造成的。工程期刊上充斥着大量运用柯尔莫戈罗夫随机过程研究成果所衍生方法的文章。

No one, that is, until Kolmogorov. He called a sequence of numbers collected over time with the successive values related to previous ones a “stochastic process.” Kolmogorov’s pioneering papers (published just before the beginning of World War II) laid the foundations for further work by Norbert Wiener in the United States, George Box in England, and Kolmogorov’s students in Russia. Because of Kolmogorov’s ideas, it is now possible to examine records taken over time and come to highly specific conclusions. The waves lapping against a beach in California have been used to locate a storm in the Indian Ocean. Radio telescopes can distinguish between different sources (and perhaps, some day, intercept a message from intelligent life on another planet). It is possible to know whether a seismographic record is the result of an underground nuclear explosion or a natural earthquake. Engineering journals are filled with articles that make use of methods that have evolved out of Kolmogorov’s work on stochastic processes.

现实生活中的概率是什么？

WHAT IS PROBABILITY IN REAL LIFE?

在生命的最后几年，柯尔莫戈罗夫着手解决一个更为棘手的问题，一个兼具哲学性和数学性的问题。可惜的是，他英年早逝，未能完成这项工作。一代又一代的数学家一直在思考如何继承他的洞见。截至本文撰写之时，这个问题仍未得到解决。正如我将在本书最后几章中阐述的那样，如果这个问题一直悬而未决，整个统计学方法论都可能因其自身的不一致性而崩溃瓦解。

In the final years of his life, Kolmogorov attacked a much more difficult problem, a problem as much philosophical as mathematical. He died before he could complete the work. A generation of mathematicians has been pondering over how to follow up on his insights. As of this writing, this problem is still unsolved, and, as I shall show in the final chapters of this book, if it remains unsolved, the whole of the statistical approach to science may come crashing down from the weight of its own inconsistencies.

柯尔莫戈罗夫的最后一个问题是：概率在现实生活中意味着什么？他​​已经建立了一套令人满意的概率数学理论。这意味着概率的定理和方法在内部都是自洽的。科学的统计模型超越了纯粹的数学领域，并将这些定理应用于现实问题。为此，必须将柯尔莫戈罗夫提出的概率论抽象数学模型与现实生活的某些方面联系起来。事实上，已经有数百次尝试这样做，每一次都赋予了概率在现实生活中不同的意义，也都受到了批评。这个问题非常重要。对统计分析的数学结论的解释取决于你如何将这些公理与现实情况联系起来。

Kolmogorov’s final problem was the question: What does probability mean in real life? He had produced a satisfactory mathematical theory of probability. This meant that the theorems and methods of probability were all internally self-consistent. The statistical model of science leaps out of the purely mathematical realm and applies these theorems to real-life problems. To do so, the abstract mathematical model that Kolmogorov proposed for probability theory has to be identified with some aspect of real life. There have been literally hundreds of attempts to do this, each one providing a different meaning to probability in real life and each of them subject to criticism. The problem is very important. The interpretation of the mathematical conclusions of statistical analysis depends upon how you identify these axioms with real-life situations.

在柯尔莫戈罗夫概率论的公理化中，我们假设存在一个称为“事件”的基本事物的抽象空间。这个空间中的事件集合可以像测量门廊的面积或冰箱的体积一样进行测量。如果对这个抽象事件空间的测量满足某些公理，那么它就是一个概率空间。要在现实生活中应用概率论，我们必须确定这个事件空间，并且要足够精确，以便能够在这个空间上实际计算概率。当实验科学家使用统计模型分析结果时，这个空间是什么？威廉·西利·戈塞特提出，这个空间是实验所有可能结果的集合，但他无法证明如何计算这个空间上的概率。除非我们能够确定柯尔莫戈罗夫的抽象空间，否则从统计分析中得出的概率陈述将具有许多不同甚至相互矛盾的含义。

In Kolomogorov’s axiomization of probability theory, we assume there is an abstract space of elementary things called “events.” Sets of events in this space can be measured the same way we measure the area of a porch deck or the volume of a refrigerator. If this measure on the abstract space of events fulfills certain axioms, then it is a probability space. To use probability theory in real life, we have to identify this space of events and do so with sufficient specificity to allow us to actually calculate probability measurements on that space. What is this space when an experimental scientist uses a statistical model to analyze the results? William Sealy Gosset proposed that the space was the set of all possible outcomes of the experiment, but he was unable to show how to calculate probabilities on it. Unless we can identify Kolmogorov’s abstract space, the probability statements that emerge from statistical analyses will have many different and sometimes contrary meanings.

例如，假设我们开展一项临床试验来检验一种新的艾滋病疗法的疗效。假设统计分析表明，新旧疗法之间的差异具有统计学意义。这是否意味着医学界可以确信这种新疗法对下一位艾滋病患者有效？这是否意味着它对一定比例的艾滋病患者有效？或者仅仅意味着，只有在研究中经过严格筛选的患者群体中，这种新疗法才表现出优势？

For instance, suppose we run a clinical trial to examine the efficacy of a new treatment for AIDS. Suppose the statistical analysis shows that the difference between the old and the new treatments is significant. Does this mean the medical community can be sure the new treatment will work on the next AIDS patient? Does this mean it will work for a certain percentage of AIDS patients? Does it merely mean that, only in the highly selected patient population of the study, there appears to be an advantage for the new treatment?

通常，人们试图通过为柯尔莫戈罗夫的抽象概率空间赋予现实意义来探寻概率的现实意义。柯尔莫戈罗夫另辟蹊径。他融合了热力学第二定律、卡尔·皮尔逊的早期工作、几位美国数学家对信息论数学理论的初步尝试以及保罗·列维关于大数定律的研究成果，从1965年开始发表了一系列论文，这些论文推翻了之前的公理和他对数学问题的解答，并将概率视为……

Finding the real-life meaning of probability has usually been approached by proposing real-life meaning for Kolmogorov’s abstract probability space. Kolmogorov took another tack. Combining ideas from the second law of thermodynamics, early work by Karl Pearson, tentative attempts by several American mathematicians to find a mathematical theory of information, and work by Paul Levy on the laws of large numbers, he produced a series of papers, starting in 1965, that wiped away the axioms and his solution to the mathematical problem and treated probability as …

1987 年 10 月 20 日，安德烈·尼古拉耶维奇·科尔莫戈罗夫去世了，他生前充满活力，直到生命的最后一刻仍在不断涌现出独创的想法——但没有人能够接手他留下的事业。

On October 20, 1987, Andrei Nikolaevich Kolmogorov died, vibrant with life and pouring out original ideas to his last days—and no one has been able to pick up the threads that he left.

关于苏联统计学失败的说明

AFTER NOTE ON THE FAILURES OF SOVIET STATISTICS

尽管柯尔莫戈罗夫及其学生对概率论和统计学的数学理论做出了重大贡献，但苏联却几乎没有从这场统计革命中获益。造成这种情况的原因恰恰说明了当政府掌握所有问题的“正确”答案时会发生什么。

Although Kolmogorov and his students made major contributions to the mathematical theories of probability and statistics, the Soviet Union gained little from the statistical revolution. Why this was the case provides an example of what happens when the government knows the “correct” answer to all questions.

在沙皇统治末期和俄国​​革命初期，俄国的统计活动十分活跃。俄国数学家们对英国和欧洲发表的研究成果了如指掌。俄国数学家和农学家的论文发表在《生物统计学》（Biometrika）杂志上。革命政府设立了中央统计局，各加盟共和国也设有类似的统计机构。中央统计局出版了统计活动期刊《统计学报》（Vestnik Statistiki），其中收录了发表在英文和德文期刊上的文章摘要。直到1924年，《统计学报》仍然发表了一篇关于统计设计在农业研究中应用的文章。

During the final days of the czar and in the early years of the Russian Revolution, there was a considerable amount of statistical activity in Russia. Russian mathematicians were fully aware of the work being published in Britain and Europe. Papers by Russian mathematicians and agronomists appeared in Biometrika. The revolutionary government set up a Central Statistical Administration, and there were similar statistical administrations in the individual Soviet republics. The Central Statistical Administration published a journal of statistical activity, Vestnik Statistiki, which contained summaries of articles appearing in English- and German-language journals. As late as 1924, Vestnik Statistiki published a description of the application of statistical designs to agricultural research.

随着20世纪30年代斯大林恐怖统治的到来，正统共产主义理论的冰冷之手落到了这些活动之上。党的理论家们（用切斯特·布利斯的话来说，他们是马克思和列宁这两位圣人的宗教神学家；参见第八章）将统计学视为社会科学的一个分支。在共产主义教条下，所有社会科学都服从于中央计划。随机变量的数学概念是统计方法的核心。俄语中“随机变量”一词的字面意思是“偶然量”。对于中央计划者和理论家来说，这是一种侮辱。苏联的一切工业和社会活动都是按照马克思和列宁的理论计划的。任何事情都不可能偶然发生。“偶然量”或许可以用来描述资本主义经济体中观察到的现象——但在苏联，它行不通。数理统计的应用很快就被扼杀了。S.S.扎尔科维奇在1956年发表于《数理统计年鉴》的一篇关于苏联统计学的历史回顾中，对此进行了较为委婉的描述：

With the coming of the Stalin terror in the 1930s, the cold hand of orthodox Communist theory fell on these activities. The theoreticians of the party (the theologians of their religion according to Saints Marx and Lenin, to quote Chester Bliss; see chapter 8) looked upon statistics as a branch of social science. Under Communist doctrine, all social science was subordinate to central planning. The mathematical concept of a random variable lies at the heart of statistical methods. The Russian word for random variable translates as “accidental magnitude.” To the central planners and theoreticians, this was an insult. All industrial and social activity in the Soviet Union was planned according to the theories of Marx and Lenin. Nothing could occur by accident. Accidental magnitudes might describe things observed in capitalist economies—not in Russia. The applications of mathematical statistics were quickly stifled. S. S. Zarkovic, in a historical review of Soviet statistics published in The Annals of Mathematical Statistics in 1956, described this with some delicacy:

在随后的几年里，政治因素在俄罗斯统计学的发展中扮演了日​​益重要的角色。这导致统计管理实践中理论应用的逐渐消失。20世纪30年代末，《统计公报》（Vestnik Statistiki）开始停止刊登以数学方法处理统计问题的文章。到了30年代末，这类文章彻底消失，此后再未出现。这一趋势导致统计学家放弃实践，转而留在大学和其他科研机构，以其他学科的名义从事统计学研究。官方说法是，柯尔莫戈罗夫、斯米尔诺夫、罗曼诺夫斯基等许多人都是脱离统计学的数学家。一个非常有趣的例子是斯卢茨基，他曾是计量经济学的先驱之一，享誉世界。但他放弃了统计学，转而投身天文学研究……。按照官方观点，统计学已成为国民经济规划的工具。因此，它代表了一门社会科学，或者换句话说，一门阶级科学。大数定律、随机偏差的概念，以及其他所有属于统计学数学理论的内容，都被当作虚假的统计科学普适理论的组成部分而摒弃了。

In subsequent years, political considerations became an increasingly pronounced factor in the development of Russia’s statistics. This brought about the gradual disappearance of the use of theory in the practical activity of the statistical administration. In the late thirties, Vestnik Statistiki began to close its pages to papers in which statistical problems were dealt with mathematically. At the end of the thirties, they disappeared completely and have not appeared since. The result of this trend was that statisticians abandoned practice to continue their work at universities and other scientific institutions where they pursued statistics under the name of some other subjects. Officially, A. N. Kolmogorov, N. V. Smirnov, V. I. Romanovsky and many others are mathematicians divorced from statistics. A very interesting example is E. Slutsky, who enjoyed worldwide renown as one of the forerunners of econometrics. He gave up statistics to embark on a new career in astronomy … . According to official views, statistics became an instrument for planning the national economy. Consequently it represents a social science or, in other words, a class science. The law of large numbers, the idea of random deviations, and everything else belonging to the mathematical theory of statistics were swept away as the constituent elements of the false universal theory of statistical science.

官方观点并不止步于统计数据。斯大林拥护一位名叫特罗菲姆·D·李森科的伪生物学家，他否定遗传学理论，声称动植物无需遗传性状，也能受环境影响而发生改变。试图研究R·A·费舍尔在数理遗传学领域工作的生物学家遭到打压，甚至被投入监狱。随着正统理论渗透到苏联的统计数据中，中央统计局及其后继机构所编制的数据越来越令人怀疑。在中央计划经济体制下，乌克兰和白俄罗斯肥沃的农田变成了泥泞的荒地。大量劣质机器和易碎的消费品从俄罗斯工厂源源不断地涌出。苏联甚至难以养活民众。唯一运转良好的经济活动是黑市。中央政府仍然炮制虚假乐观的统计数据，用各种变化率和变化率的指标掩盖了真实的经济活动水平。

Nor did the official views stop at statistics. Stalin embraced a quack biologist named Trofim D. Lysenko, who rejected the genetic theory of inheritance and claimed that plants and animals could be molded by their environment without having to inherit traits. Biologists who tried to follow R. A. Fisher’s work in mathematical genetics were discouraged or even sent to prison. As orthodox theory descended on Soviet statistics, the numbers generated by the Central Statistical Administration and its successors became more and more suspect. Under central planning, the rich farmlands of Ukraine and Belorussia were becoming muddy wastes. Vast amounts of poorly built machines that would not work and consumer items that fell apart poured out of Russian factories. The Soviet Union was having trouble feeding its populace. The only economic activity that worked was the black market. The central government still turned out false, optimistic statistics in which the exact level of economic activity was hidden behind reports dealing with rates of change and rates of rates of change.

当美国数学家诺伯特·维纳运用柯尔莫哥洛夫和亚历山大·雅·欣钦关于随机过程的定理来推进美国的战争努力，当美国标准局的沃尔特·休哈特等人向美国工业界展示如何运用统计方法进行质量控制，当美国、欧洲和一些亚洲农场的农业产量突飞猛进地增长时，苏联工厂仍在生产毫无价值的机器，苏联农场也无法养活全国人民。

While American mathematicians like Norbert Wiener were using the theorems of Kolmogorov and Alexander Ya. Khintchine on stochastic processes to further the American war effort, while Walter Shewhart and others at the U.S. Bureau of Standards were showing American industry how to use statistical methods of quality control, while the agricultural output of American, European, and some Asian farms was increasing by leaps and bounds, Soviet factories continued turning out worthless machines and Soviet farms were unable to feed the nation.

直到20世纪50年代尼基塔·赫鲁晓夫上台，官方理论的冰冷之手才开始松动，人们开始尝试将统计方法应用于工业和农业。然而，官方的“统计数据”依然充斥着谎言和精心设计的混淆，所有出版应用统计学期刊的努力最终也只是零星地发行了几期。现代统计模型在俄罗斯工业领域的应用，直到20世纪90年代末苏联及其中央计划体制彻底崩溃后才得以实现。

Only in the 1950s, with the coming to power of Nikita Khrushchev, did this cold hand of official theory begin to lift, and tentative attempts were made to apply statistical methods to industry and agriculture. The official “statistics” continued to be filled with lies and elaborate obfuscations, and all efforts to publish journals that dealt with applied statistics resulted in a few irregularly printed issues. The extension of modern statistical models to Russian industry had to wait for the complete collapse of the Soviet Union and its system of central planning in the late 1990s.

或许我们可以从中吸取一些教训。

Perhaps there is a lesson to be learned from this.

C第十五章

CHAPTER 15

虫眼视角

THE WORM’S-EYE VIEW

F弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔，这位维多利亚时代英国的传奇人物，令她面对的议员和英国陆军将领们闻风丧胆。人们往往只把她看作是护理专业的创始人，一位温柔无私、乐于助人的仁慈奉献者，但真实的弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔是一位肩负使命的女性。她还是一位自学成才的统计学家。

Florence Nightingale, a legendary English Victorian figure, was a terror to the members of Parliament and the British army generals whom she confronted. There is a tendency to think of her simply as the founder of the nursing profession, a gentle, self-sacrificing giver of mercy, but the real Florence Nightingale was a woman with missions. She was also a self-educated statistician.

南丁格尔的任务之一是迫使英国军队设立野战医院，为前线士兵提供护理和医疗服务。为了支持她的主张，她仔细查阅了大量军队档案资料。之后，她带着一系列引人注目的图表出席了皇家委员会的听证会。在这些图表中，她指出克里米亚战争期间英国军队的大部分死亡都是由于在战场外感染的疾病，或是战斗结束后因伤势未得到及时治疗而导致的并发症。她发明了饼图来清晰地呈现她的信息。

One of Nightingale’s missions was to force the British army to maintain field hospitals and supply nursing and medical care to soldiers in the field. To support her position, she plowed through piles of data from the army files. Then she appeared before a royal commission with a remarkable series of graphs. In them, she showed how most of the deaths in the British army during the Crimean War were due to illnesses contracted outside the field of battle, or that occurred long after action as a result of wounds suffered in battle but left unattended. She invented the pie chart as a means of displaying her message.

当她厌倦了与那些愚钝无知的军队将领们斗争时，她便会退隐到伊文顿村，在那里，她的朋友戴维一家总是热情地欢迎她。年轻的戴维夫妇生了一个女儿，给她取名为弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔·戴维。弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔的某些坚韧不拔和开拓精神似乎也遗传给了她的女儿。弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔·戴维（她用这个笔名出版了十本书和一百多篇科学期刊论文）出生于1909年，第一次世界大战爆发时，她五岁，原本正常的教育也因此中断。由于一家人住在偏远的小村庄，她最初的教育是在当地牧师的私人辅导下进行的。这位牧师对年轻的弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔·戴维有着一些独特的教育理念。他注意到她已经掌握了一些算术，于是开始教她代数。他认为她已经学会了英语，于是又开始教她拉丁语和希腊语。十岁时，她才开始接受正规教育。

When she tired of fighting the obtuse, seemingly ignorant army generals, she would retreat to the village of Ivington, where she would always be welcomed by her friends, the Davids. When the young David couple had a daughter, they named her Florence Nightingale David. Some of Florence Nightingale’s feistiness and pioneering spirit seem to have transferred to her namesake. F. N. David (the name under which she published ten books and more than 100 papers in scientific journals) was born in 1909 and was five years old when World War I interrupted what would have been the normal course of her education. Because the family lived in a small country village, her first schooling consisted of private lessons with the local parson. The parson had some peculiar ideas of education for the young Florence Nightingale David. He noted that she had already learned some arithmetic, so he started her on algebra. He felt that she had already learned English, so he started her on Latin and Greek. At age ten, she transferred to formal schooling.

弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔·戴维到了上大学的年纪，她的母亲对她想去伦敦大学学院（University College, London）的想法感到震惊。伦敦大学学院是由杰里米·边沁（Jeremy Bentham）创立的（他的木乃伊遗体至今仍身着正装安放在学院的回廊中）。这所学院是为“土耳其人、异教徒以及不信奉《三十九条信纲》的人”而设立的。在它创立之前，只有那些信奉英国国教《三十九条信纲》的人才被允许在英国的大学任教或学习。即使在戴维准备上大学的时候，伦敦大学学院仍然以异议人士的聚集地而闻名。“那时我母亲一想到我要去伦敦就大惊小怪……她觉得那是耻辱、罪恶之类的。”于是，她去了伦敦的贝德福德女子学院（Bedford College for Women）。

When it came time for Florence Nightingale David to go to college, her mother was appalled at her desire to attend University College, London. University College had been founded by Jeremy Bentham (whose mummified body sits in formal clothes in the cloisters of the college). The college was designed for “Turks, Infidels, and such as do not profess the Thirty-nine Articles.” Until its founding, only those who professed the Thirty-nine Articles of faith of the established Church of England were allowed to teach or study at the universities in England. Even when David was preparing for college, University College still had the reputation of being a hotbed of dissenters. “My mother was having fits by this time about my going to London … disgrace and iniquity and that sort of thing.” So she went to Bedford College for Women in London.

“我不太喜欢那段日子，”她后来在与哈佛大学公共卫生学院的南·莱尔德的录音对话中说道，“但我喜欢的是，我每天晚上都去看戏。如果你是学生，花六便士就能去老维克剧院……我玩得很开心。”她继续说道，在学校里，“我整整三年除了数学什么都没学，我不太喜欢数学。我不喜欢那里的人，我想我那时候算是叛逆吧。但我并不怀念那段时光。”

“I didn’t like it very much,” she said much later in a recorded conversation with Nan Laird of the Harvard School of Public Health. “But what I did like was I went to the theatre every night. If you were a student, you could go to the Old Vic for sixpence … . I had a great time.” At school, she continued, “I did nothing but mathematics for three years, and I didn’t like that very much. I didn’t like the people and I suppose I was a rebel in those days. But I don’t look back on it fondly.”

杰里米·边沁的木乃伊遗体保存在伦敦大学学院的回廊中。

The mummified body of Jeremy Bentham, preserved in the cloisters of University College, London

她毕业后能用学的那些数学知识做什么呢？她想成为一名精算师，但精算公司只招男性。有人建议她去大学学院找一个叫卡尔·皮尔逊的人，据她的线人说，他跟精算师之类的机构有关系。她径直去了大学学院，“硬闯进去见了卡尔·皮尔逊”。他很欣赏她，给她争取到了一份奖学金，让她继续做他的研究学生。

What could she do with all that mathematics when she graduated? She wanted to become an actuary, but the actuarial firms would take only men. Someone suggested that she see a fellow named Karl Pearson at University College who, her informant had heard, had something to do with actuaries or something like that. She walked over to University College and “I crashed my way in to see Karl Pearson.” He took a liking to her and got her a scholarship to continue her studies as his research student.

为KP工作

WORKING FOR K. P.

在卡尔·皮尔逊手下工作期间，F.N.戴维负责计算复杂且难度极高的多重积分的解，以及相关系数的分布。这项工作催生了她的第一本书—— 《相关系数表》，该书最终于1938年出版。在那几年里，她用一台名为布伦斯维加（Brunsviga）的手摇机械计算器完成了所有这些计算，以及更多其他计算。“我估计我转动那台手摇布伦斯维加大约200万次……在我学会用长长的织针疏通机器之前……我总是把这该死的机器弄卡住。卡住之后，你应该去告诉教授，然后他会告诉你他对你的看法；那真是太糟糕了。很多时候，我把机器弄卡住后就回家了，却没告诉他。”尽管她很敬佩皮尔逊，并在他生命的最后几年里与他相处了很长时间，但在20世纪30年代初，戴维对卡尔·皮尔逊却感到恐惧。

Working for Karl Pearson, F. N. David was set to computing the solutions of complicated difficult multiple integrals, calculating the distribution of the correlation coefficient. This work produced her first book, Tables of the Correlation Coefficient, which was finally published in 1938. She did all these calculations, and much more during those years, on a hand-cranked mechanical calculator known as a Brunsviga. “I estimated that I turned that hand Brunsviga roughly 2 million times … . Before I learned to manipulate long knitting needles [to unjam the machine] … I was always jamming the damn thing. When you jammed it, you were supposed to go tell the professor and then he would tell you what he thought of you; it was really rather awful. Many was the time I had jammed the machine and had gone home without telling him.” Although she admired him and was to spend a great deal of time with him in his final years, David was terrified of Karl Pearson in the early 1930s.

她也是个胆大妄为的人，​​过去常常骑摩托车参加越野赛。

She was also a daredevil and used to take a motorcycle on cross-country races.

有一天，我重重地撞到一堵16英尺高的玻璃墙上，摔了个狗啃泥，膝盖也受了伤。那天我在办公室里，心情糟透了，[威廉·S·]戈塞特来了，他说：“你最好去学学飞钓。”因为他是个狂热的飞钓爱好者。他邀请我去他家。当时他和戈塞特太太还有几个孩子都在亨登的家里。他教我怎么抛飞蝇，人特别好。

I had a hell of a crash one day into a 16-foot wall, which had glass on the top, and I pitched over and hurt my knee. I was in my office one day and I was miserable and [William S.] Gosset came and he said, “Well, you better take up flyfishing,” because he was an ardent flyfisher. He invited me to his house. There was him [sic] and Mrs. Gosset and various children in the house in Henden. He taught me to throw a fly and he was very kind.

当大卫在伦敦大学学院就读时，内曼和年轻的埃贡·皮尔逊开始研究费舍尔似然函数，这激怒了老卡尔·皮尔逊，他认为这一切都是无稽之谈。埃贡害怕进一步激怒父亲，所以他没有将这篇处女作投稿到父亲的期刊《生物统计学》（Biometrika），而是和内曼一起创办了一份新期刊《统计研究回忆录》（Statistical Research Memoirs），这份期刊发行了两年（大卫也在其中发表了几篇论文）。之后卡尔退休，埃贡接任《生物统计学》的编辑，并停刊了《统计研究回忆录》。大卫亲眼目睹了这位被人们称为“老家伙”的人被他的儿子和R.A.费舍尔取代。她也见证了年轻的耶日·内曼刚刚开始他的统计学研究。“我认为20世纪20年代到40年代是统计学发展史上一个真正具有开创性的时期，”她说，“而我则是以一个旁观者的视角见证了所有这些重要人物。”

David was at University College, London, when Neyman and young Egon Pearson began looking at Fisher’s likelihood function, angering old Karl Pearson, who thought it all nonsense. Egon was afraid of angering his father further, so rather than submit this first work to his father’s journal, Biometrika, he and Neyman began a new journal, Statistical Research Memoirs, which ran for two years (and in which F. N. David published several papers). Then Karl retired and Egon took over the editorship of Biometrika and ended the Memoirs. F. N. David was there when the “old man” (as he was called) was being usurped by his son and R. A. Fisher. She was there when young Jerzy Neyman was just starting his research in statistics. “I think the period between the 1920s and 1940 was really seminal in statistics,” she said. “And I saw all the protagonists from a worm’s-eye point of view.”

Jerzy Neyman 与他的两位“女士”合影，分别是 Evelyn Fix（左）和 FN David（右）。

Jerzy Neyman with two of his “ladies,” Evelyn Fix (left) and F. N. David (right)

戴维称卡尔·皮尔逊是一位很棒的讲师。“他讲课讲得太好了，你坐在那里就能完全吸收他讲的内容。”他也很宽容学生的打断，即使学生指出了他的错误，他也会很快纠正。而费舍尔的课则“糟透了。我什么都听不懂。我想问他问题，但如果我问了，他不会回答，因为我是女生。”于是她就坐在一个美国男学生旁边，推着他的胳膊说：“问他！问他！”“费舍尔的课结束后，我会去图书馆待上大约三个小时，试图弄明白他到底在讲什么。”

David called Karl Pearson a wonderful lecturer. “He lectured so well, you would sit there and let it all soak in.” He was also tolerant of interruptions from students, even if one of them spotted a mistake, which he would quickly correct. Fisher’s lectures, on the other hand, “were awful. I couldn’t understand anything. I wanted to ask him a question, but if I asked him a question, he wouldn’t answer it because I was a female.” So she’d sit next to one of the male students from America and push his arm, saying, “Ask him! Ask him!” “After Fisher’s lecture, I would go spend about three hours in the library trying to understand what he was up to.”

1933年卡尔·皮尔逊退休时，F·N·戴维作为他唯一的科研助理随他一同退休。正如她所写：

When Karl Pearson retired in 1933, F. N. David went with him as his only research assistant. As she wrote:

卡尔·皮尔逊是个非凡的人。他七十多岁了，我们常常一起工作一整天，直到下午六点才离开学院。有一次，他正要回家，我也正要回家，他对我说：“哦，你今晚或许可以看看椭圆积分。我们明天要用到它。” 我当时没勇气告诉他，我要和男朋友去参加切尔西艺术舞会。于是我去了舞会，凌晨四五点才回家，洗了个澡，然后去大学，等他九点来的时候，我已经把论文准备好了。年轻的时候真是傻。

Karl Pearson was an extraordinary person. He was in his seventies and we would have worked all day on something and go out of the college at 6:00. On one occasion he was going home and I was going home and he said to me, “Oh, you just might have a look at the elliptic integral tonight. We shall want it tomorrow.” And I hadn’t the nerve to tell him that I was going off with a boyfriend to the Chelsea Arts Ball. So I went to the Arts ball and came home at four to five in the morning, had a bath, went to the University, and then had it ready when he came in at nine. One’s silly when one’s young.

在卡尔·皮尔逊去世前几个月，FN·戴维回到生物统计实验室，与耶日·内曼共事。内曼惊讶于她竟然没有博士学位。在他的劝说下，她将最后发表的四篇论文整理成博士论文提交。后来有人问她，获得博士学位后，她的身份是否有所改变。“没有，没有，”她说，“我只是白白损失了二十英镑的入学费。”

A few months before Karl Pearson died, F. N. David came back to the biometrical laboratory and worked with Jerzy Neyman. Neyman was surprised she did not have her doctorate. At his urging, she took her last four published papers and submitted them as a dissertation. She was asked later if her status changed as a result of being granted a Ph.D. “No, no,” she said. “I was just out the twenty-pound entrance fee.”

回想起那些日子，她说道：“我倾向于认为，我被招进来是为了让内曼先生闭嘴。但那段时间真是动荡不安，因为费舍尔在楼上大吵大闹，一边是内曼，另一边是KP，戈塞特每隔一周就来一次。”​​她对那些年的回忆未免太过谦逊。她绝非“被招进来让内曼先生闭嘴的虫子”。她发表的论文（包括一篇与内曼合著的、对20世纪初俄罗斯数学家A.A.马尔科夫一项开创性定理进行推广的重要论文）在许多领域推动了统计学的实践和理论发展。我几乎可以从书架上翻出所有统计理论分支的书籍，都能找到对F.N.戴维论文的引用。

Looking back at those days, she noted, “I’m inclined to think that I was brought in to keep Mr. Neyman quiet. But it was a tumultuous time because Fisher was upstairs raising hell and there was Neyman on one side and K. P. on the other and Gosset coming in every other week.” Her reminiscences of those years are much too modest. She was far more than a worm “brought in to keep Mr. Neyman quiet.” Her published papers (including one very important one cowritten by Neyman on a generalization of a seminal theorem by A. A. Markov, a Russian mathematician of the early twentieth century) advanced the practice and theory of statistics in many fields. I can pull books off my shelf from almost every branch of statistical theory and find references to papers by F. N. David in all of them.

战争工作

WAR WORK

1939年战争爆发时，戴维在英国内政部工作，致力于预测炸弹可能对伦敦等人口中心造成的影响。她利用自己建立的统计模型，估算了伤亡人数、炸弹对电力、供水和污水处理系统的影响以及其他潜在问题。正因如此，英国才能在1940年和1941年德军对伦敦的闪电战中做好充分准备，在保障基本公共服务的同时，挽救了无数生命。

When war broke out in 1939, David worked at the Ministry of Home Security, trying to anticipate the effects of bombs that might be dropped on population centers like London. Estimates of the number of casualties, the effects of bombs on electricity, water, and sewage systems, and other potential problems were determined from statistical models she built. As a result, the British were prepared for the German blitz of London in 1940 and 1941 and were able to maintain essential services while saving lives.

战争末期，她写道：

Toward the end of the war, as she wrote:

我搭乘一架美国轰炸机飞往安德鲁斯空军基地。我去那里是为了看看他们建造的第一批大型数字计算机……那是一间尼森小屋（在美国被称为奎恩塞特小屋），大约100码长，中间铺着一排木板，木板之间可以跑来跑去。小屋两侧，大约每隔几英尺就有两个闪烁的巨型机器，天花板上全是引信。大约每隔30秒，一个美国大兵就会仰着头从木板上跑下来，按下一个引信……我回来后，跟别人讲了这件事……他们说：“你最好坐下来学学这门语言。”我说：“想都别想。如果我学了，我这辈子就只能做这个了，不，我不会学——让别人来学吧！”

I was flown over in one of the American bombers to Andrews Air Force Base. I came over to have a look at the first big digital computers that they built … . It was a Nissen hut [called a Quonset hut in the United States], about 100 yards long, and down the center were a whole lot of duckboards, on bits of wood you could run on. Either side, about every few feet, there were two winking monsters, and in the ceiling was [sic] nothing but fuses. Every thirty seconds or so, the GI would run down the duckboard with his face up to the sky and push in a fuse … . When I got back, I was telling somebody about it … and they said, “Well, you better sit down and learn the language.” And I said, “Not on your nelly. If I do, that’s all I will ever do for the rest of my life and no, I’m not going to—somebody else can!”

埃贡·皮尔逊不像他父亲那样专横​​跋扈，他推行了一项新政策，让生物统计学系主任一职在系内其他教员之间轮换。大约在FN·戴维轮任系主任的同时，她开始撰写《组合概率》一书，这本书是该领域的经典之作。它以极其清晰的方式阐述了被称为“组合数学”的复杂计数方法。当被问及这本书时，她回答说：这本书用一种简单易懂的方式呈现了极其复杂的概念。

Egon Pearson was not the domineering person his father was, and he initiated a new policy that had the chairmanship of the biometrics department rotate among the other members of the faculty. About the time F. N. David rotated to the chairmanship, she had begun working on Combinatorial Chance, a book that is one of the classics in the literature. It is a remarkably clear exposition of complicated methods of counting, known as “combinatorics.” When she was asked about this book, in which exceedingly complicated ideas are presented with a single underlying approach that makes them much easier to understand, she replied:

我这辈子一直有个毛病，就是做一件事总是做着做着就腻了。我构思了组合数学，也研究了很久，远在我认识巴顿（D.E. Barton，她那本书的合著者，后来成为伦敦大学学院的计算机科学教授）或者教过巴顿之前……但我还是请他来帮忙，因为是时候把这个项目完成了。于是我们开始合作，他负责所有复杂的计算，比如求极限之类的。他是个很棒的人。我们合作写了不少论文。

All my life, I have had this beastly business that I start something and then I get bored with it. I had the idea of combinatorics and I had worked on them for a long time, long before I knew Barton [D. E. Barton, the coauthor of her book, later to become professor of computer science at University College] or taught Barton … but I took him on because it was time that the thing was finished. So we got to work and he did all the fancy work, proceeding to limits and things like that. He was a good chap. We wrote quite a lot of papers together.

她最终来到美国，在加州大学伯克利分校任教，并接替内曼担任系主任。1970年，她离开伯克利，在加州大学河滨分校创立并担任统计系主任。1977年，68岁的她“退休”，成为伯克利生物统计学荣誉退休教授和研究员。本章许多引文均出自1988年的一次采访。她于1995年去世。

She eventually came to the United States, where she was on the faculty at the University of California, Berkeley, and succeeded Neyman as chair of the department. She left Berkeley to found and chair the statistics department at the University of California, Riverside, in 1970. She “retired” in 1977 at age sixty-eight and became an active professor emeritus and research associate in biostatistics at Berkeley. The interview that provided many of the quotations in this chapter took place in 1988. She died in 1995.

1962年，F.N. David出版了一本名为《游戏、神明与赌博》的书。以下是她对这本书创作缘起的描述：

In 1962, F. N. David published a book entitled Games, Gods, and Gambling. Here is her description of how it came about:

我小时候学过希腊语，后来……我对考古学产生了兴趣，是因为我的一位考古同事当时正在某个沙漠里忙着挖掘。总之，他来找我说：“我走遍了沙漠，把这些碎片的位置都标出来了。告诉我，厨房垃圾堆在哪里挖。”考古学家对金银财宝不感兴趣，他们只关心锅碗瓢盆。于是我拿了他的地图，仔细思考了一下，觉得这跟V型轰炸机的问题简直一模一样。伦敦被炸毁了，炸弹落在那里，你想知道炸弹是从哪里落下的，所以你可以假设一个正态二元曲面，然后预测它的主轴。我就是这么做的，处理了碎片分布图。你不觉得奇怪吗？问题之间似乎存在某种共通之处？真正截然不同的问题也就那么几个。

I had lessons in Greek when I was young and … I got interested in archaeology when I had a colleague in archaeology who was busy digging in one of the deserts, I think. Anyway, he came to me and said, “I have walked about the desert and I have plotted where these shards were. Tell me where to dig for the kitchen midden.” Archaeologists don’t care about gold and silver, all they care about are the pots and pans. So I took his map and I thought about it and I thought this is exactly like the problem of the V-bombers. Here you have London and here you have bombs landing and you want to know where they come from so you can assume a normal bivariate surface and predict the major axes. That’s what I did with the shard map. It’s curious there’s a sort of unity among problems, don’t you think? There’s only about a half dozen of them that are really different.

弗洛伦斯·南丁格尔·大卫对他们所有人的文学创作都做出了贡献。

And Florence Nightingale David contributed to the literature of all of them.

C第十六章

CHAPTER 16

取消参数

DOING AWAY WITH PARAMETERS

D20世纪40年代，美国氰胺公司的化学家弗兰克·威尔科克森（Frank Wilcoxon）被一个统计学问题困扰。他当时正在进行假设检验，比较不同处理方法的效果，使用的方法是“学生” t检验[17]和费舍尔方差分析。这在当时是分析实验数据的标准方法。统计学革命已经彻底改变了科学实验室，几乎每位科学家的书架上都摆放着用于解释这些假设检验结果的表格书籍。但威尔科克森担心这些方法经常出现失效的情况。

During the 1940s, Frank Wilcoxon, a chemist at American Cyanamid, was bothered by a statistical problem. He had been running hypothesis tests comparing the effects of different treatments, using “Student”’s[17] t-tests and Fisher’s analyses of variance. This was the standard way of analyzing experimental data at that time. The statistical revolution had completely taken over the scientific laboratory, and books of tables for interpreting these hypothesis tests were on every scientist’s shelves. But Wilcoxon was concerned about what often appeared to be a failure of these methods.

他可能会进行一系列实验，在这些实验中，他明显感觉到不同处理方法的效果存在差异。有时，t检验会得出显著性结果，有时则不会。在化学工程实验中，经常会出现这样的情况：反应发生的化学反应器在实验开始时没有充分预热。也可能出现某种酶的反应能力发生变化的情况。这会导致实验值出现偏差，通常是一个过大或过小的数值。有时，可以找到造成这种异常结果的原因。有时，这个结果本身就是一个异常值，与其他所有结果都截然不同，但却找不到明显的原因。

He might run a series of experiments where it was obvious to him that treatments differered in effect. Sometimes the t-tests would declare significance, and sometimes they would not. It often happens, when running an experiment in chemical engineering, that the chemical reactor, where the reaction takes place, is not sufficiently warmed up at the beginning of the sequence of experimental trials. It may happen that a particular enzyme begins to vary in its ability to react. The result is an experimental value that appears to be wrong. It is often a number that is much too large or much too small. Sometimes, it is possible to identify the cause of this outlying result. Sometimes, the result is an outlier, differing drastically from all the other results, but there is no obvious reason for this.

威尔科克森研究了t检验和方差分析的计算公式，发现这些异常值，即这些极端值和不寻常的值，对结果影响很大，导致t检验值比正常情况下要小。（通常，t检验值越大，p值越小。）人们很容易想到从观测值集中剔除异常值，然后用剩余的值重新计算t检验。但这会给假设检验的数学推导带来问题。化学家如何判断一个数字是否真的是异常值？需要剔除多少个异常值？如果剔除了异常值，化学家还能继续使用标准检验统计量的概率表吗？

Wilcoxon looked at the formulas for calculating t-tests and analyses of variance and realized that these outliers, these extreme and unusual values, greatly influenced the results, causing “Student”’ s t-test to be smaller than it would be otherwise. (In general, large values of the t-test lead to small p-values.) It was tempting to eliminate the outlier from the set of observations and calculate the t-test from the other values. This would introduce problems into the mathematical derivation of the hypothesis tests. How would the chemist know whether a number is really an outlier? How many outliers would have to be eliminated? Could the chemist continue to use the tables of probability for the standard test statistics if outliers have been eliminated?

弗兰克·威尔科克森开始查阅文献。那些创造统计方法的伟大数学大师们肯定早就遇到过这个问题！但他却找不到任何相关资料。威尔科克森觉得他找到了解决问题的办法。这涉及到基于观测数据的组合和排列的繁琐计算（F.N.戴维的组合数学在前一章中提到过）。他开始研究计算这些组合值的方法。

Frank Wilcoxon went on a literature search. Surely the great mathematical masters who had produced the statistical methods had seen this problem before! He could find no reference to it. Wilcoxon thought he saw a way out of the problem. It involved tedious calculations based on combinations and permutations of the observed numbers (F. N. David’s combinatorics are mentioned in the previous chapter). He began working out a method for calculating those combinatoric values.

哦，这真是愚蠢！像威尔科克森这样的化学家为什么要费力计算这些看似简单却又繁琐的题目呢？统计学家肯定早就做过了！他重新翻阅统计学文献，试图找到那篇相关的论文。结果一无所获。为了检验自己的计算，他向《生物统计学》 （ Biometrics，不要与皮尔逊的《生物统计学》（ Biometrika）混淆）杂志投稿。他仍然认为这不可能是原创作品，并寄希望于审稿人能发现之前发表过类似的内容，从而拒稿。他认为，如果拒稿，审稿人也会告知他其他相关的参考文献。然而，审稿人和编辑们最终认定，这确实是原创作品。此前从未有人想到过这一点，他的论文于1945年发表。

Oh, but this was foolish! Why should a chemist like Wilcoxon have to work out these simple but tedious calculations? Surely, somebody in statistics had done this already! Back he went to the statistical literature to find this previous paper. He found no such paper. Mainly to check on his own mathematics, he submitted a paper to the journal Biometrics (not to be confused with Pearson’s Biometrika). He still believed that this could not be original work and he depended upon the referees to know where this had been published before and reject his paper. By rejecting it, they would also notify him about these other references. However, as far as the referees and editors could determine, this was original work. No one had ever thought of this before, and his paper was published in 1945.

威尔科克森和《生物统计学》的编辑们都不知道，一位名叫亨利·B·曼的经济学家和俄亥俄州立大学统计学研究生D·兰索姆·惠特尼正在研究一个相关的问题。他们试图对统计分布进行排序，以便在某种意义上说，1940年的工资分布小于1944年的工资分布。他们提出了一种排序方法，该方法涉及一系列简单但繁琐的计数步骤。

What neither Wilcoxon nor the editors of Biometrics knew was that an economist named Henry B. Mann and a graduate student in statistics at Ohio State University named D. Ransom Whitney were working on a related problem. They were trying to order statistical distributions so that one might say, in some sense, that the distribution of wages in the year 1940 was less than the distribution of wages in 1944. They came up with a method of ordering that involved a sequence of simple but tedious counting methods.

这使得曼恩和惠特尼得到了一个检验统计量，其分布可以通过组合算术计算得出——与威尔科克森检验的计算方式相同。他们在威尔科克森的论文发表两年后的1947年发表了一篇论文，描述了他们的新技术。很快人们就发现，威尔科克森检验和曼恩-惠特尼检验密切相关，并且产生相同的p值。这两个检验统计量都涉及一些新的东西。在威尔科克森的论文发表之前，人们认为所有检验统计量都必须基于分布参数的估计。然而，这种检验并不估计任何参数。它将观测到的数据离散程度与纯粹随机离散程度的预期值进行比较。它是一种非参数检验。[18]

This led Mann and Whitney to a test statistic whose distribution could be computed from combinatoric arithmetic—the same type of computation as Wilcoxon’s. They published a paper describing their new technique in 1947, two years after Wilcoxon’s paper had appeared. It was quickly shown that the Wilcoxon test and the Mann-Whitney test were closely related and produced the same p-values. Both these test statistics involved something new. Until the Wilcoxon paper, it was thought that all test statistics would have to be based on estimates of parameters of distributions. This was a test, however, that did not estimate any parameter. It compared the observed scatter of data with what might have been expected from purely random scatter. It was a nonparametric test.[18]

如此一来，统计学革命超越了皮尔逊最初的设想。它现在无需使用参数即可处理测量分布。鲜为人知的是，在20世纪30年代末，苏联的安德烈·科尔莫戈罗夫和他的学生尼古拉·斯米尔诺夫研究了一种不使用参数的分布比较方法。威尔科克森和曼恩-惠特尼的工作通过关注有序秩的本质，开启了数学研究的新篇章，斯米尔诺夫-科尔莫戈罗夫的研究成果很快也被纳入其中。

In this way, the statistical revolution moved a step beyond Pearson’s original ideas. It could now deal with distributions of measurements without making use of parameters. Unknown to many in the West, in the late 1930s, Andrei Kolmogorov in the Soviet Union and a student of his, N. V. Smirnov, had investigated a different approach to the comparison of distributions that did not make use of parameters. The work by Wilcoxon and Mann and Whitney had opened a new window of mathematical investigation by directing attention to the underlying nature of ordered ranks, and the Smirnov-Kolmogorov work was soon included.

后续发展

FURTHER DEVELOPMENTS

一旦数学研究领域开启了一扇新的窗口，研究者们便会以不同的视角审视它。威尔科克森的这项开创性工作很快便催生了其他方法。赫尔曼·切尔诺夫和理查德·萨维奇发现，威尔科克森检验可以从有序统计量的期望均值角度来理解；他们成功地将非参数检验扩展为一系列涉及不同潜在分布的检验，而这些检验都不需要估计任何参数。到了20世纪60年代初，这类检验（现在被称为“无分布检验”）已成为研究热点。博士生们纷纷填补理论的空白，以此作为他们的论文主题。学术会议也专门讨论这一新理论。威尔科克森继续在该领域深耕，不断扩展检验的范围，并开发出极其巧妙的组合计算算法。

Once a new window has been opened in mathematical research, investigators begin to look through it in different ways. This original work by Wilcoxon was soon followed by alternative approaches. Herman Chernoff and I. Richard Savage discovered that the Wilcoxon test could be looked upon in terms of the expected average values of ordered statistics; and they were able to expand the nonparametric test into a set of tests involving different underlying distributions, none of them requiring the estimation of a parameter. By the early 1960s, this class of tests (now referred to as “distribution-free tests”) was the hot topic in research. Doctoral students filled in little corners of the theory to provide their theses. Meetings were devoted exclusively to this new theory. Wilcoxon continued to work in the area, expanding on the range of tests as he developed extremely clever algorithms for the combinatoric calculations.

1971年，捷克斯洛伐克的雅罗斯拉夫·哈耶克（Jaroslav Hájek）出版了一部权威教科书，为整个领域提供了一个统一的视角。哈耶克于1974年去世，年仅48岁。他发现了所有非参数检验的一个潜在推广，并将这一通用方法与中心极限定理的林德伯格-莱维条件联系起来。这往往是数学研究的常态。从某种意义上说，所有数学都是相互关联的，但这些关联的确切性质以及利用这些关联的洞见往往需要多年才能显现。

In 1971, Jaroslav Hájek from Czechoslovakia produced a definitive textbook that provided a unifying view for the entire field. Hájek, who died in 1974 at age forty-eight, discovered an underlying generalization for all nonparametric tests and linked this general approach to the Lindeberg-Lévy conditions of the central limit theorem. This is frequently the way of mathematical research. In some sense, all mathematics are linked, but the exact nature of those links and the insights to exploit them often take many years to emerge.

为了深入探究其统计学发现的意义，弗兰克·威尔科克森离开了化学领域，转而管理美国氰胺公司及其莱德勒实验室部门的统计服务团队。1960年，他加入佛罗里达州立大学统计系任教，在那里他展现出卓越的教学和研究才能，并指导了多位博士生。1965年去世时，他留下了众多杰出的学生和统计学创新成果，这些成果至今仍对该领域产生着深远的影响。

As he pursued the implications of his statistical discovery, Frank Wilcoxon left his original field of chemistry and managed the statistical services group at American Cyanamid and at its Lederle Labs division. In 1960, he joined the faculty of the Department of Statistics at Florida State University, where he proved himself to be a well-regarded teacher and researcher and oversaw several doctoral candidates. When he died in 1965, he left behind a legacy of students and statistical innovation that continue to have a remarkable effect on the field.

未解决的问题

UNSOLVED PROBLEMS

非参数方法的出现或许引发了这一新兴领域的蓬勃发展。然而，此前使用的参数方法与非参数方法之间并没有明显的联系。当时存在两个悬而未决的问题：

The development of nonparametric procedures may have led to a burst of activity in this new field. However, there was no obvious link between the parametric methods that were used before this and the nonparametric methods. There were two unsolved questions:

  1. 如果数据具有已知的参数分布，例如正态分布，那么如果我们使用非参数方法进行分析，分析结果会出错到什么程度？
  2. If the data have a known parametric distribution, like the normal distribution, how badly will the analysis go wrong if we use nonparametric methods?
  3. 如果数据与参数模型不太吻合，那么数据与该模型的偏差要达到多大程度，非参数方法才更适合使用？
  4. If the data do not quite fit a parametric model, how far off from that model must the data be before the nonparametric methods are the better ones to use?

1948年， 《数理统计年鉴》的编辑们收到了一篇来自塔斯马尼亚大学（位于澳大利亚南部海岸附近的一个岛屿上）一位名不见经传的数学教授的论文。这篇杰出的论文解决了这两个问题。埃德温·詹姆斯·乔治·皮特曼此前曾在《皇家统计学会杂志》上发表过三篇论文，并在《剑桥哲学学会会刊》上发表过一篇论文。事后看来，这些论文奠定了他后来研究的基础，但却被忽视或遗忘了。除了这四篇论文之外，皮特曼——他在向《年鉴》投稿时已52岁——此前从未发表过任何论文，也鲜为人知。

In 1948, the editors of the Annals of Mathematical Statistics received a paper from an unknown mathematics professor at the University of Tasmania, on the island off the southern coast of Australia. This remarkable paper solved both problems. Edwin James George Pitman had published three earlier papers in the Journal of the Royal Statistical Society and one in the Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society that, in retrospect, laid the foundations of his later work but that had been ignored or forgotten. Except for those four papers, Pitman, who was fifty-two years old when he submitted his article to the Annals, was unpublished and unknown.

E·J·G·皮特曼于1897年出生于澳大利亚墨尔本。他本科就读于墨尔本大学，但第一次世界大战中断了他的学业，他在军队服役两年。战后他重返校园，完成了学业。“那时候，”他后来写道，“澳大利亚的大学里没有数学研究生院。”一些大学会为优秀学生提供奖学金，资助他们前往英国攻读研究生，但墨尔本大学没有。“在墨尔本大学学习四年后离开时，我没有接受过任何研究方面的训练；但我认为我已经学会了如何学习和运用数学，并且我愿意解决任何出现的问题……”他面临的首要问题是如何谋生。

E. J. G. Pitman had been born in Melbourne, Australia, in 1897. He attended the University of Melbourne as an undergraduate, but his schooling was interrupted by the First World War, when he served two years in the army. He returned to complete his degree. “In those days,” he wrote later, “there were no graduate schools in mathematics in Australian universities.” Some of the universities provided scholarship money for their better students to go on to graduate study in England, but the University of Melbourne did not. “When I left the University of Melbourne after four years of study there, I had had no training in research; but I thought that I had learned to study and use mathematics, and that I would be willing to tackle any problem that arose … .” The first problem was how to earn a living.

塔斯马尼亚大学正在招聘数学教师。皮特曼应聘并被任命为数学教授。整个数学系只有这位新教授和一位兼职讲师。该系需要为其他所有院系的本科生开设数学课程，而这位新教授的教学任务繁重，几乎占据了他所有的时间。当校董会决定聘请一位全职数学教授时，一位校董听说数学中出现了一个名为统计学的新兴分支；于是，校董会询问这位新申请人是否愿意教授统计学课程（无论统计学究竟是什么）。

The University of Tasmania was looking for someone to teach mathematics. Pitman applied and was named professor of mathematics. The entire department consisted of the new professor and a part-time lecturer. The department was required to give courses in undergraduate mathematics to students in all other departments, and the new professor was busy with a course load that took almost all his time. When the board of trustees decided to hire a full-time mathematics professor, one of the board members had heard that there was a new branch of mathematics called statistics; so the new applicant was asked if he would be prepared to teach a course in statistics (whatever that was).

皮特曼回答说：“我不敢说自己对统计学理论有任何专门的知识；但是，如果被任命，我愿意在1927年讲授这门课程。”他既没有统计学理论方面的专业知识，也没有其他任何类型的知识。在墨尔本大学，他曾修读过一门高等逻辑课程，教授在那门课上用了两节课的时间来讲解统计学。正如皮特曼所说：“我当时就决定，统计学是我不感兴趣的东西，我永远也不需要为此费心。”

Pitman replied, “I cannot claim to have any special knowledge of the Theory of Statistics; but, if appointed, I would be prepared to lecture on this subject in 1927.” He had no special knowledge, nor any other type of knowledge, of the theory of statistics. At Melbourne, he had taken a course in advanced logic, during which the professor devoted a couple of lectures to statistics. As Pitman put it, “I decided then and there that statistics was the sort of thing that I was not interested in, and would never have to bother about.”

年轻的E·J·G·皮特曼于1926年秋抵达塔斯马尼亚的霍巴特，他仅有一个本科学位，却在一所偏远的小型地方学院担任教授。这所学院与伦敦和剑桥的学术氛围可谓相去甚远。“直到1936年，我才发表任何论文，”他写道，“这主要有两个原因：一是繁重的工作量，二是我的成长经历。”他所说的成长经历指的是他缺乏数学研究方法方面的训练。

Young E. J. G. Pitman arrived in the fall of 1926 in Hobart, Tasmania, with only an undergraduate degree to support his title of professor, at a small provincial school that was about as far away as one could get from the intellectual ferment in London and Cambridge. “I did not publish anything,” he wrote, “until 1936. There were two main reasons for the delay in publication; the work load I had to carry, and the nature of my upbringing,” by which he meant his lack of training in methods of mathematical research.

到1948年，当他将那篇杰出的论文投稿至《数理统计年鉴》时，塔斯马尼亚大学的数学系已经发展壮大。当时，该系拥有一位教授（皮特曼）、一位副教授、两位讲师和两位助教。他们教授的应用数学和理论数学课程范围广泛。皮特曼每周讲授十二节课，并在周六代课。他的研究也获得了一些支持。从1936年开始，联邦政府每年拨款3万英镑，用于促进澳大利亚各大学的科学研究。这笔资金根据各州的人口数量进行分配；由于塔斯马尼亚是人口较少的州之一，因此整个大学每年只能获得2400英镑。至于皮特曼获得了多少这笔资金，他没有透露。

By 1948, when he sent his remarkable paper to the Annals of Mathematical Statistics, the mathematics department at the University of Tasmania had grown. It now had one professor (Pitman), an associate professor, two lecturers, and two tutors. They taught a wide range of mathematics, both applied and theoretical. Pitman gave twelve lectures a week and covered classes on Saturdays. He now had some support for his research. Starting in 1936, the Commonwealth government began to provide 30,000 pounds a year for the promotion of scientific research in the universities of Australia. This was allocated to different states on a population basis; because Tasmania was one of the smaller states, this amounted to 2,400 pounds a year for the entire university. How much of this Pitman got, he did not say.

皮特曼逐渐涉猎不同类型的研究。他发表的第一篇论文探讨的是流体动力学问题。接下来的三篇论文则研究了假设检验理论中一些非常具体的问题。这些论文本身并不出彩，但却是皮特曼的学习成果。他当时正在探索如何发展思想，以及如何将不同的数学结构联系起来。

Gradually, Pitman engaged in different types of research. His first published paper dealt with a problem in hydrodynamics. His next three papers investigated highly specific aspects of hypothesis testing theory. These papers were not remarkable in themselves, but they were Pitman’s learning theses. He was exploring how to develop ideas and how to relate mathematical structures to each other.

皮特曼在开始撰写1948年的论文时，已经对统计假设检验的本质以及旧式（参数）检验和新式（非参数）检验之间的相互关系形成了一套清晰的推理体系。他运用这些新方法，着手解决两个悬而未决的问题。

By the time he began to work on his 1948 paper, Pitman had developed a clear line of reasoning about the nature of statistical hypothesis tests and the interrelationships between the older (parametric) and the newer (nonparametric) tests. With his new methods, he attacked the two outstanding problems.

他的发现令所有人大吃一惊。即使最初的假设成立，非参数检验的效果也几乎与参数检验一样好。皮特曼得以回答第一个问题：如果我们在已知参数模型且应该使用特定参数检验的情况下使用非参数检验，结果会有多差？皮特曼说，结果并不差。

What he found surprised everyone. Even when the original assumptions are true, the nonparametric tests were almost as good as the parametric tests. Pitman was able to answer the first question: How badly do we do if we use nonparametric tests in a situation where we know the parametric model and should be using a specific parametric test? Not badly at all, said Pitman.

第二个问题的答案更加令人惊讶。如果数据不符合参数模型，那么数据与参数模型的偏差要达到多大程度，非参数检验才会优于参数检验？皮特曼的计算表明，即使数据与参数模型的偏差很小，非参数检验的性能也远优于参数检验。

The answer to the second question was even more surprising. If the data do not fit the parametric model, how far off from the parametric model must they be for the nonparametric tests to be better? Pitman’s calculations showed that with only slight deviations from the parametric model, the nonparametric tests were vastly better than the parametric ones.

弗兰克·威尔科克森是一位化学家，他确信肯定有人在他之前就发现了这个简单的规律，而威尔科克森似乎偶然间找到了真正的点石成金之石。皮特曼的研究结果表明，所有假设检验都应该是非参数检验。皮尔逊发现的基于参数的统计分布仅仅是第一步。现在，统计学家们无需再为具体的参数而担忧，就能处理统计分布了。

It looked as if Frank Wilcoxon, the chemist who was sure somebody had made this simple discovery before him, had stumbled onto a veritable philosopher’s stone. Pitman’s results suggested that all hypothesis tests should be nonparametric ones. Pearson’s discovery of the statistical distributions based on parameters was only a first step. Now statisticians were able to deal with statistical distributions without worrying about specific parameters.

数学中蕴藏着精妙之处。威尔科克森、曼恩和惠特尼以及皮特曼等人的方法看似简单，实则暗含着对数据分布的假设。而这些假设的真正含义，又过了二十五年才被人们理解。第一个令人不安的问题，是芝加哥大学的R.R.巴哈杜尔和L.J.“吉米”·萨维奇于1956年发现的。几年前，我把巴哈杜尔和萨维奇的论文拿给一位来自印度的朋友看时，他注意到他们名字的巧合。巴哈杜尔在印地语中意为“战士”。正是这样一位战士和一位“野蛮人”，给非参数统计检验理论带来了最初的冲击。

There are subtleties within subtleties in mathematics. Deep within their seemingly simple approaches, Wilcoxon, Mann and Whitney, and Pitman had made assumptions about the distributions of the data. It would take another twenty-five years for these assumptions to be understood. The first disturbing problem was discovered by R. R. Bahadur and L. J. (“Jimmie”) Savage at the University of Chicago in 1956. When I showed the Bahadur and Savage paper to a friend of mine from India a few years ago, he remarked at the congruence of their names. Bahadur means “warrior” in Hindi. It took a warrior and a savage to strike the first blow to the theory of nonparametric statistical tests.

Savage 和 Bahadur 发现的问题，恰恰源于最初促使 Wilcoxon 使用非参数检验的问题：异常值问题。如果异常值很少且完全是“错误”的观测值，那么非参数方法可以降低它们对分析的影响。但如果异常值是数据系统性污染的一部分，那么转向非参数方法可能只会使情况变得更糟。我们将在第 23 章探讨受污染分布的问题。

The problems that Savage and Bahadur uncovered came from the very problem that had first suggested nonparametric tests to Wilcoxon: the problem of outliers. If the outliers are rare and completely “wrong” observations, then nonparametric methods reduce their influence on the analysis. If the outliers are part of a system- , atic contamination of the data, then shifting to nonparametric methods may only make matters worse. We shall investigate the problem of contaminated distributions in chapter 23.

C第十七章

CHAPTER 17

部分优于整体

WHEN PART IS BETTER THAN THE WHOLE

T卡尔·皮尔逊认为，可以通过收集数据来检验概率分布。他相信，只要收集到足够的数据，就能代表所有此类数据。生物统计学的记者会从古代墓地中挑选数百个头骨，向其中灌入铅弹以测量颅容量，并将这些数据发送给皮尔逊。还有记者会前往中美洲丛林，测量数百名土著居民的臂骨长度，并将这些测量结果发送到皮尔逊的生物统计学实验室。

To Karl Pearson, probability distributions could be examined by collecting data. He thought that if he collected enough data, he could expect them to be representative of all such data. The correspondents for Biometrika would select hundreds of skulls from ancient graveyards, pour shot into them to measure the cranial capacity, and send Pearson these hundreds of numbers. A correspondent would travel to the jungles of Central America and measure the lengths of arm bones on hundreds of natives, sending these measurements to Pearson’s biometrical laboratory.

然而，皮尔逊的方法存在一个根本缺陷。他收集的是现在所谓的“便利样本”。这些数据是最容易获取的，并不一定能真正代表整个分布。那些被打开以测定颅骨容量的墓穴，只是碰巧被发现的而已。那些未被发现的墓穴，其颅骨容量可能存在一些未知的差异。

There was a basic flaw in Pearson’s methods, however. He was collecting what is now called an “opportunity sample.” The data were those that were most easily available. They did not have to be truly representative of the entire distribution. The graves that were opened to find the cranial capacity of skulls were those that happened to have been found. The unfound ones could differ in some unknown way.

20世纪30年代初，印度就出现了一个典型的机会抽样失败案例。当时，孟买码头上堆放着黄麻包，准备运往欧洲。为了确定黄麻的价值，人们对每个包进行抽样，并根据样本判断黄麻的质量。抽样方法是将一把空心圆刀插入包中，从刀尖处取出少量黄麻。在包装和运输过程中，黄麻包的外层容易损坏，内部也越来越紧实，冬季甚至会夹杂一些冻伤的部分。抽样人员虽然能将空心刀插入包中，但刀尖会偏离包内较紧实的部分，导致样本几乎全部来自外层受损的区域。这种机会抽样方法存在偏差，使得在黄麻包整体质量较高的情况下，更容易抽取到劣质黄麻。

A specific example of this failure of opportunity sampling was discovered in India in the early 1930s. Bales of jute were assembled on the dock in Bombay for shipment to Europe. To determine the value of the jute, each bale was sampled and the quality of the jute determined from the sample. The sampling was done by plunging a hollow circular blade into the bale and pulling out a small amount in the core of the blade. In the packing and shipment of the bales, the outside of the bales tended to deteriorate and the inner parts tended to become more and more compacted, often including some frozen parts in the wintertime. The sampler would push the hollow knife into the bale, but it would be deflected from the denser part of the bale, and the sample would tend to consist almost entirely of the outer and damaged region. The opportunity sample was biased in favor of finding inferior jute when the quality of the bale was much higher.

加尔各答总统学院物理系主任普拉桑塔·钱德拉·马哈拉诺比斯教授经常用这个例子（他在铁路公司工作时发现了这个例子，当时铁路公司负责将黄麻运到码头）来说明为什么机会样本不可信。马哈拉诺比斯出身于加尔各答一个富裕的商人家庭，经济条件允许他攻读研究生和博士后学位，以追求他对科学和数学的兴趣。20世纪20年代，他前往英国，师从皮尔逊和费舍尔。像F·N·戴维这样的学生只能依靠奖学金生活，而马哈拉诺比斯在求学期间却过着贵族般的生活。他回到加尔各答总统学院担任物理系主任。不久之后，在1931年，他自掏腰包，在他家族的一处庄园里建立了印度统计研究所。

Professor Prasanta Chandra Mahalanobis, head of the Department of Physics in the Presidency College, Calcutta, often used this example (which he had discovered when working for the railroad that shipped the jute to the dock) to show why opportunity samples were not to be trusted. Mahalanobis came from a wealthy family of Calcutta merchants and could afford a period of graduate and postgraduate study as he pursued his interests in science and mathematics. During the 1920s, he traveled to England and studied under both Pearson and Fisher. Students like F. N. David had to subsist on scholarship aid, but Mahalanobis lived the life of the grand seigneur while he pursued his studies. He returned to head the Department of Physics at Presidency College. Soon afterward, in 1931, he used his own funds to set up the Indian Statistical Institute on the grounds of one of his family estates.

在印度统计研究所，他培养了一批杰出的印度数学家和统计学家，其中许多人后来在该领域做出了重要贡献，例如S.N. Roy、C.R. Rao、R.C. Bose、P.K. Sen和Madan Puri等等。马哈拉诺比斯的研究兴趣之一在于如何生成具有适当代表性的数据样本。显然，在许多情况下，几乎不可能获得所有测量数据。例如，印度人口众多，多年来，印度从未尝试过像美国那样在一天内完成一次完整的普查。相反，完整的印度人口普查需要一年多的时间，因为不同地区的普查工作在不同的月份进行。正因如此，印度的人口普查永远无法做到完全准确。在普查期间，会发生出生、死亡、迁移和身份变更等情况。没有人能够确切地知道印度在某一天有多少人口。[19]

At the Indian Statistical Institute, he trained a group of brilliant Indian mathematicians and statisticians, many of whom went on to make important contributions to the field—people like S. N. Roy, C. R. Rao, R. C. Bose, P. K. Sen, and Madan Puri, among others. One of Mahalanobis’s interests lay in this question of how to produce an appropriately representative sample of data. It was clear that, in many situations, it was almost impossible to get all the measurements in a set. For instance, the population of India is so great that, for years, no attempt was made to get a complete census on a single day—as is tried in the United States. Instead, the complete Indian census takes over a year, as different regions of the country are counted in different months. Because of this, the Indian census can never be accurate. There are births and deaths, migrations, and changes of status that occur during the time of the census. No one will ever know exactly how many people there are in India on a given day.[19]
