Women also played an important role in the development of theoretical statistics in the later half of the twentieth century. Recall L. H. C. Tippett’s first asymptote of the extreme that is used to predict “hundred-year floods,” as described in chapter 6. A version of that distribution, known as a “Weibull distribution,” has found important uses in the aerospace industry. One problem with the Weibull distribution is that it does not meet Fisher’s regularity conditions, and there are no clearly optimal ways of estimating its parameters. That is, there were no optimal ways until Nancy Mann at North American Rockwell discovered a link between the Weibull and a much simpler distribution, and developed the methods now used in that field.

威斯康星大学的格蕾丝·瓦巴采用了一组称为“样条拟合”的临时曲线拟合方法，并找到了一种理论公式，该公式现在主导了样条的统计分析。

Grace Wahba at the University of Wisconsin took a set of ad hoc methods of curve fitting, called “spline fits,” and found a theoretical formulation that now dominates the statistical analysis of splines.

伊冯娜·毕晓普是20世纪60年代末一个由统计学家和医学科学家组成的委员会的成员，该委员会试图确定当时广泛使用的麻醉剂氟烷是否是导致患者肝衰竭病例增加的原因。由于大部分数据都是事件计数的形式，分析工作举步维艰。此前十年间，人们曾尝试整理类似氟烷研究中那样复杂的多维计数表，但都未能取得显著成效。之前的研究者曾建议采用类似于费舍尔方差分析的方法来处理这类表格，但这项工作并不完善。毕晓普接手了这项工作，并研究了其理论意义，建立了估计和解释的标准。在完善了氟烷研究中的这项技术后，她发表了一篇关于此技术的权威著作。这种方法后来被称为“对数线性模型”，如今已成为大多数社会学研究的标准第一步。

Yvonne Bishop was a member of a committee of statisticians and medical scientists who in the late 1960s were trying to determine if the widely used anesthetic halothane was the cause of an increase in liver failure among patients. The analysis was bedeviled . by the fact that most of the data were in the form of counts of events. During the previous ten years, attempts had been made to organize complicated multidimensional tables of counts like those in this halothane study, but none had been particularly successful. Previous workers had suggested approaching such tables in a way that was similar to Fisher’s analysis of variance, but that work was incomplete. Bishop took it up and examined the theoretical ramifications, establishing criteria for estimation and interpretation. Having polished the technique in the halothane study, she published a definitive text on it. “Log-linear models,” as this method has come to be called, are now the standard first step in most sociological studies.

自斯内德科和考克斯时代以来，“伴郎”通常都是女性。

Since the days of Snedecor and Cox, the “best person” has frequently been a woman.

C章​

CHAPTER 20

一个普通的德州农家男孩

JUST A PLAIN TEXAS FARM BOY

我20世纪20年代末，当塞缪尔·S·威尔克斯离开德克萨斯州的家族农场前往爱荷华大学求学时，数学研究正攀上抽象艺术的高峰。符号逻辑、集合论、点集拓扑和超限数理论等纯粹的抽象领域席卷了各大高校。抽象程度如此之高，以至于任何可能启发这些领域最初想法的现实生活问题灵感早已消失殆尽。数学家们深入研究了古希腊欧几里得所宣称的数学基础公理，并发现了这些公理背后未言明的假设。他们清除了数学中这些假设，探索了逻辑思维的基本构建模块，并由此产生了非凡的、看似自相矛盾的想法，例如空间填充曲线和同时与所有点相交又不与任何点相交的三维形状。他们研究了不同阶的无穷以及具有分数维的“空间”。数学正处于一股包罗万象的纯粹抽象思维浪潮之中，完全脱离了任何现实感。

In the late 1920s, when Samuel S. Wilks left the family farm in Texas to study at the University of Iowa, mathematical research was scaling the heights of beautiful abstraction. Purely abstract fields like symbolic logic, set theory, point set topology, and the theory of transfinite numbers were sweeping through the universities. The level of abstraction was so great that any inspiration from real-life problems that might have given rise to the initial ideas in these fields had long since been lost. Mathematicians plunged into the axioms that the ancient Greek Euclid had declared were the foundations of mathematics; and they found unstated assumptions behind those axioms. They scrubbed mathematics clean of such assumptions, explored the fundamental building blocks of logical thought, and emerged with remarkable, seemingly self-contradictory ideas, like space-filling curves and three-dimensional shapes that touched everywhere and nowhere at the same time. They investigated the different orders of infinity and “spaces” with fractional dimensions. Mathematics was on the upsurge of an all-encompassing wave of sheer abstract thought, completely divorced from any sense of reality.

在美国大学的数学系，这种超越实用性的抽象追求达到了前所未有的高度。美国数学学会的出版物被公认为世界顶尖的数学期刊之一，美国数学家们不断探索着抽象的边界。正如萨姆·威尔克斯几年后遗憾地指出，这些系以其纯粹思考的诱人机会，吸走了美国研究生中最优秀的人才。

Nowhere was this drive into abstraction beyond practicality as great as in the mathematics departments of American universities. The publications of the American Mathematical Society were being recognized among the top echelon of mathematical journals in the world, and American mathematicians were pushing at the frontiers of abstractions beyond abstractions. As Sam Wilks was to state ruefully some years later, these departments, with their siren calls offering opportunities for pure thought, were siphoning off the best brains among American graduate students.

萨姆·威尔克斯在爱荷华大学的第一门研究生数学课是由当时该校数学系最负盛名的教授R.I.摩尔讲授的。摩尔的点集拓扑课程将威尔克斯引入了这个美妙却又充满抽象的世界。摩尔明确表示他鄙视实用性的工作，并坚持认为应用数学与洗碗扫街无异。这种态度自古希腊时代以来就一直困扰着数学界。据说欧几里得曾辅导一位贵族的儿子，并讲解了一个特别精妙的定理证明。尽管欧几里得热情洋溢，这位学生似乎却不以为然，并问这有什么用。欧几里得随即叫来他的奴隶，说道：“给这孩子一块铜板吧。看来他肯定能从这些知识中受益。”

Sam Wilks’s first graduate mathematics course at Iowa was taught by R. I. Moore, the most renowned member of the university’s mathematics faculty. Moore’s course in point set topology introduced Wilks to this wonderful world of impractical abstraction. Moore made it clear that he disdained useful work, and insisted that applied mathematics was on a level with washing dishes and sweeping streets. This is an attitude that has plagued mathematics since the time of the ancient Greeks. There is a story told of Euclid tutoring the son of a nobleman and going over a particularly beautiful proof of a theorem. In spite of Euclid’s enthusiasm, the student seemed unimpressed and asked what use this might have. At which Euclid called over his slave and said, “Give the lad a copper. It seems he must gain from his knowledge.”

萨姆·威尔克斯对实际应用的浓厚兴趣在他开始在爱荷华大学寻找博士论文选题时得到了他的导师埃弗雷特·F·林奎斯特的认可。林奎斯特曾从事保险数学方面的工作，他对新兴的数理统计领域很感兴趣，并向威尔克斯提出了一个该领域的问题。当时，数理统计在欧美大学的数学系中还略显逊色。R·A·费舍尔的伟大开创性工作发表在《爱丁堡皇家学会哲学汇刊》等“冷门”期刊上。《皇家统计学会杂志》和《生物统计学》等期刊也被视为对统计数据进行列表整理的出版物而不受重视。密歇根大学的亨利·卡弗创办了一份名为《数理统计年鉴》的新期刊，但其学术水准太低，以至于大多数数学家都对其不屑一顾。林奎斯特提出了一个有趣的抽象数学问题，这个问题源于教育心理学中使用的一种测量方法。威尔克斯解决了这个问题，并将其用于博士论文，研究结果发表在《教育心理学杂志》上。

Sam Wilks’s bent for practical applications was answered by his thesis adviser, Everett F. Linquist, when he began looking for a Ph.D. thesis topic at Iowa. Linquist, who had worked in the mathematics of insurance, was interested in the newly developing field of mathematical statistics and proposed a problem from that area to Wilks. At that time, there was something slightly disreputable about mathematical statistics, at least among the mathematics departments of American and European universities. R. A. Fisher’s great pioneering work had been published in “out-of-the-way” journals like the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh. The Journal of the Royal Statistical Society and Biometrika were looked down upon as publications in which statistical collections of numbers were tabulated. Henry Carver at the University of Michigan had started a new. journal entitled the Annals of Mathematical Statistics, but its standards were much too low for most mathematicians to take notice of it. Linquist suggested an interesting problem in abstract mathematics that emerged from a method of measurement used in educational psychology. Wilks solved this problem and used it for a doctoral thesis, and the results were published in the Journal of Educational Psychology.

在纯数学界，这算不上什么成就。教育心理学领域远不在他们的关注范围之内。但博士论文本应是踏入科研领域的初步尝试，很少有学生能凭借论文做出重大贡献。威尔克斯前往哥伦比亚大学攻读研究生一年（在此期间，他需要提升自己驾驭高深莫测的数学理论的能力）。1933年秋，他来到普林斯顿大学，担任数学讲师。

To the world of pure mathematics, this was not much of an achievement. The field of educational psychology was well below their horizon of interest. But a Ph.D. thesis is supposed to be a first tentative step into the world of research, and few students are expected to make important contributions with their theses. Wilks went to Columbia University for a year of postgraduate study (in the course of which he was expected to increase his ability to handle the cold, rarefied abstractions of important mathematics). In the fall of 1933, he arrived at Princeton University, having taken a position as an instructor in mathematics.

普林斯顿大学的统计学

STATISTICS AT PRINCETON

普林斯顿大学数学系和美国其他任何院系一样，都沉浸在冷峻而优美的抽象概念之中。1939年，普林斯顿高等研究院在附近成立，其首批成员之一是约瑟夫·H·M·韦德伯恩，他发展了所有有限数学群的完整推广。同样在该研究院任职的还有赫尔曼·韦尔，他因在无维向量空间领域的工作而闻名；以及库尔特·哥德尔，他发展了元数学代数。这些人对普林斯顿大学的教职人员产生了影响，普林斯顿大学也拥有众多世界知名的数学家，其中最杰出的当属所罗门·莱夫舍茨，他开启了代数拓扑这一新兴抽象领域的大门。[26]

The Princeton mathematics department was immersed in the cold and beautiful abstractions as much as any other department in the United States. In 1939, The Institute of Advanced Studies was to be established nearby, and among its first members was Joseph H. M. Wedderburn, who had developed the complete generalization of all finite mathematical groups. Also at the institute were Hermann Weyl, who was famous for his work in nondimensional vector spaces, and Kurt Godel, who had developed an algebra of metamathematics. These men influenced the Princeton faculty, which had its share of world-renowned mathematicians, prominent among them Solomon Lefshetz, who had opened the doors to the new abstract field of algebraic topology.[26]

尽管普林斯顿大学的教员普遍倾向于抽象研究，但萨姆·威尔克斯很幸运地遇到了数学系主任卢瑟·艾森哈特。艾森哈特对各种数学研究都感兴趣，并且喜欢鼓励年轻教师追随自己的兴趣。艾森哈特聘用威尔克斯是因为他认为数理统计这个新兴领域有着非凡的潜力。萨姆·威尔克斯带着妻子来到普林斯顿，他追求的应用数学理念使他与系里的其他人截然不同。威尔克斯是一位温和的斗士。他那“老实人”般的德州农家子弟作风能够消除所有人的戒备。他关注个体，能够说服他人追随他的愿景，并且极其擅长组织工作，以实现艰巨的目标。

In spite of the general bent toward abstraction among the Princeton faculty, Sam Wilks was fortunate to have Luther Eisenhart as chairman of the mathematics department. Eisenhart was interested in all kinds of mathematical endeavor and liked to encourage junior faculty members to follow their own inclinations. Eisenhart hired Wilks because he thought this new field of mathematical statistics held remarkable promise. Sam Wilks arrived in Princeton with his wife, pursuing a vision of applied mathematics that set him apart from the rest of the department. Wilks was a gentle fighter. He would disarm everyone with his “just folks” Texas farm boy attitude. He was interested in people as individuals and could persuade others to follow his vision, and he was extremely good at organizing work activities to accomplish difficult goals.

威尔克斯常常能抓住问题的核心，并在其他人还在努力理解问题时就找到解决方法。他工作极其勤奋，并能激励他人像他一样努力。抵达普林斯顿不久，他就成为了《数理统计年鉴》（Annals of Mathematical Statistics）的编辑，该期刊由亨利·卡弗创办。他提高了期刊的出版标准，并让他的研究生参与编辑工作。他还说服了约翰·图基——一位最初对抽象数学更感兴趣的新教员——与他一起从事统计研究。二战后，他培养了一批又一批的研究生，这些研究生后来在许多大学创建或组建了新的统计系。

Wilks could often get to the heart of a problem and locate a way to solve it while others were still trying to understand the question. He was an extremely hard worker and persuaded others to work as hard as he did. Soon after arriving at Princeton, he became editor of the Annals of Mathematical Statistics, the journal started by Henry Carver. He raised the standards of publication and brought his graduate students into the editing of the journal. He persuaded John Tukey, a new faculty member with an initial interest in more abstract mathematics, to join him in statistical research. He took on a sequence of graduate students who went out to found or staff the new departments of statistics in many universities after World War II.

威尔克斯最初关于教育心理学问题的论文促使他与美国教育考试服务中心（ETS）合作，在那里他帮助制定了大学入学考试和其他专业院校考试的抽样程序和评分技术。他的理论研究确定了加权评分方案在多大程度上可以存在差异，但仍能产生相似的结果。他与贝尔电话实验室的沃尔特·休哈特[27]保持联系，休哈特当时正开始将费舍尔的实验设计理论应用于工业质量控制。

Wilks’s initial thesis on a problem in educational psychology led him to work with the Educational Testing Service, where he helped formulate the sampling procedures and the scoring techniques used for college entrance and other professional school exams. His theoretical work established the degree to which weighted scoring schemes could differ and still produce similar results. He was in contact with Walter Shewhart[27] at Bell Telephone Laboratories, who was beginning to apply Fisher’s experimental design theories to industrial quality control.

统计数据与战争努力

STATISTICS AND THE WAR EFFORT

随着20世纪40年代的临近，威尔克斯最重要的工作或许来自于他为华盛顿海军研究办公室（ONR）提供的咨询服务。威尔克斯坚信实验设计方法能够改进军队的武器和射击理论，而他在ONR也得到了积极的回应。到美国加入二战时，陆军和海军都已准备好将统计方法应用于美国版的运筹学。威尔克斯在国防研究委员会的领导下成立了普林斯顿统计研究小组（SRG-P）。SRG-P招募了一些最杰出的青年数学家和统计学家，其中许多人在战后为该科学领域做出了重大贡献。其中包括约翰·图基（他完全转向应用统计学）、弗雷德里克·莫斯特勒（他后来在哈佛大学创立了几个统计系）、西奥多·W·安德森（他的多元统计学教科书后来成为该领域的圣经）、亚历山大·穆德（他后来在随机过程理论方面取得了重大进展）和查尔斯·温索尔（他后来以自己的名字命名了一整类估计方法）等等。

As the 1940s approached, perhaps Wilks’s most important work came from his consulting with the Office of Naval Research (ONR) in Washington. Wilks was convinced that experimental design methods could improve the weapons and the fire doctrines of the armed forces, and he found a receptive ear at the ONR. By the time the United States entered World War II, the army and the navy were both ready to apply statistical methods in the American version of operations research. Wilks set up the Statistical Research Group-Princeton (SRG-P) under the National Defense Research Council. The SRG-P recruited some of the brightest young mathematicians and statisticians, many of whom would make major contributions to the science in the years following the war. These included John Tukey (who swung over entirely to applications), Frederick Mosteller (who would go on to found the several statistics departments at Harvard), Theodore W. Anderson (whose textbook on multivariate statistics was to become the bible of that field), Alexander Mood (who was to go on to make major advances in the theory of stochastic processes), and Charles Winsor (who was to give his name to an entire class of estimation methods), among others.

当时在SRG-P担任研究生的理查德·安德森描述了当时人们为寻找摧毁地雷的方法所做的尝试。随着入侵日本的临近，美军得知日军研制出了一种非金属地雷，这种地雷无法用任何已知手段探测到。他们在日本海滩和所有可能的入侵路线上随意布设这些地雷。仅这些地雷造成的死亡人数估计就高达数十万。找到摧毁地雷的方法迫在眉睫。此前在欧洲尝试用飞机投掷炸弹摧毁地雷的尝试均告失败。安德森和SRG-P的其他成员着手设计利用爆炸索摧毁地雷的实验方案。据安德森说，美国之所以在日本投下原子弹，其中一个原因就是他们所有的实验和计算都表明，用这种方法根本无法摧毁这些地雷。

Richard Anderson, working at that time as a graduate student with the SRG-P, describes attempts that were being made to find a method of destroying land mines. As the invasion of Japan neared, the American army learned that the Japanese had developed a nonmetallic land mine that could not be detected by any known means. They were planting these mines in random patterns across the beaches of Japan and along any possible invasion routes. Estimates of deaths from those land mines alone ran into the hundreds of thousands. It was urgent to find a way to destroy them. Attempts to use bombs dropped from airplanes against land mines in Europe had failed. Anderson and others from the SRG-P were set to designing experiments on the use of lines of explosive cord to destroy the mines. According to Anderson, one of the reasons why the United States dropped the atomic bomb on Japan was that all their experiments and calculations showed that it was impossible to destroy these mines by such means.

该小组致力于研究防空炮弹中近炸引信的有效性。近炸引信会发出雷达信号，并在接近目标时爆炸。该小组协助开发了第一批可定向投送的智能炸弹。他们研究了测距仪和不同类型的炸药。SRG-P 的成员们在全国各地的军械实验室以及陆军和海军设施中设计实验并分析数据。威尔克斯协助在哥伦比亚大学组建了第二个小组，名为普林斯顿大学初级统计研究小组 (SRG-Pjr)。SRG-Pjr 催生了“序贯分析”。这是一种在实验进行过程中修改实验设计的方法。序贯分析允许的修改涉及正在测试的处理方法本身。即使在设计最严谨的实验中，有时也会出现这样的情况：新出现的结果表明，应该修改最初的设计以获得更完整的结果。序贯分析的数学原理使科学家能够知道哪些修改可以进行，哪些修改不能进行，而不会影响结论的有效性。

The group worked on the effectiveness of proximity fuses in antiaircraft projectiles. A proximity fuse sends out radar signals and explodes when it is close to a target. The group helped develop the first of the smart bombs that could be steered toward their target. They worked on range finders and different types of explosives. Members of the SRG-P found themselves designing experiments and analyzing data at ordnance laboratories and army and navy facilities all over the country. Wilks helped organize a second group, called Statistical Research Group-Princeton, Junior (SRG-Pjr), at Columbia University. Out of SRG-Pjr came “sequential analysis.” This was a means of modifying the design of an experiment while the experiment is still running. The modifications allowed by sequential analysis involve the very treatments being tested. Even in the most carefully designed experiment, it sometimes happens that the emerging results suggest that the original design should be changed to produce more complete results. The mathematics of sequential analysis allow the scientist to know which modifications can be made and which cannot, without affecting the validity of the conclusions.

序贯分析的早期研究立即被列为绝密。参与这项研究的统计学家直到战后数年才被允许发表论文。20世纪50年代，当第一批关于序贯分析及其衍生方法“序贯估计”的论文开始出现时，这种方法引起了人们的极大兴趣，并迅速发展起来。如今，序贯统计分析方法已广泛应用于工业质量控制、医学研究和社会学等领域。

The initial studies in sequential analysis were immediately declared top secret. None of the statisticians working on it were allowed to publish until several years after the war was over. Once the first papers on sequential analysis and its cousin, “sequential estimation,” began to appear in the 1950s, the method captured the imagination of others, and the field rapidly developed. Today, sequential methods of statistical analysis are widely used in industrial quality control, in medical research, and in sociology.

序列分析只是威尔克斯统计研究小组在二战期间众多创新成果之一。战后，威尔克斯继续与军队合作，帮助他们改进装备的质量控制，运用统计方法优化未来需求规划，并将统计方法融入军事理论的各个方面。威尔克斯反对那些沉浸于纯粹抽象理论的数学家，其中一个原因就是他们缺乏爱国情怀。他认为国家需要他们投入到这些毫无用处的抽象研究中的智力资源。这些智力资源需要被应用到其他领域，首先是战争，然后是战后的冷战。

Sequential analysis was just one of the many innovations that came out of Wilks’s statistical research groups during World War II. After the war, Wilks continued to work with the armed forces, helping them improve the quality control of their equipment, using statistical methods to improve planning for future needs, and bringing statistical methods into all aspects of military doctrine. One of Wilks’s objections to the mathematicians who continued to inhabit their world of pure abstractions was that they were not being patriotic. He felt the country needed the brainpower they were siphoning away into these purposely useless abstractions. This brainpower needed to be applied, first to the war effort and then to the Cold War afterward.

没有任何记录表明有人对塞缪尔·S·威尔克斯生气。无论面对的是新来的研究生还是陆军四星上将，他都以同样轻松随意的态度对待所有人。他总是暗示自己不过是个德州老农，也知道自己还有很多东西要学，但他不禁会想……接下来，他会对当前的问题进行一番深思熟虑的分析。

There is no record of anyone getting angry at Samuel S. Wilks. He approached everyone he dealt with, whether a new graduate student or a four-star general of the army, with the same informal air. He was nothing but an old Texas farm boy, he would imply, and he knew he had a lot to learn, but he wondered if … . What followed this would be a carefully reasoned analysis of the problem at hand.

抽象统计学

STATISTICS IN ABSTRACTION

山姆·威尔克斯致力于使数理统计既成为数学中受人尊敬的一部分，又成为实用的工具。他试图引导数学家们摆脱为了抽象而抽象的冷冰冰的思维模式。数学抽象的确蕴含着一种根本的美。它深深吸引了古希腊哲学家柏拉图，以至于他宣称，我们所能看到和触摸到的一切，实际上都只是真实存在的影子，而宇宙的真谛只能通过纯粹的理性才能发现。柏拉图的数学知识相对浅薄，许多古希腊数学中被奉为圭臬的纯粹性原则也已被证明存在缺陷。然而，纯粹理性所能发现的美，至今仍然令人着迷。

Sam Wilks struggled to make mathematical statistics both a respectable part of mathematics and a useful tool for applications. He tried to move his fellow mathematicians away from the cold world of abstraction for the sake of abstraction. There is indeed a fundamental beauty in mathematical abstractions. They so attracted the Greek philosopher Plato that he declared that all those things that we can see and touch are, in fact, mere shadows of the true reality and that the real things of this universe can be found only through the use of pure reason. Plato’s knowledge of mathematics was relatively naive, and many of the cherished purities of Greek mathematics have been shown to be flawed. However, the beauty of what can be discovered with pure reason continues to entice.

自威尔克斯担任《数理统计年鉴》编辑以来，发表在该年鉴[28]和《生物统计学》上的文章变得越来越抽象。美国统计协会杂志（其早期期刊致力于描述政府统计项目）和皇家统计学会杂志（其早期期刊包含列举整个大英帝国详细农业和经济统计数据的文章）上的文章也出现了这种情况。

In the years since Wilks was editor of the Annals of Mathematical Statistics, the articles that appear in the Annals[28] and in Biometrika have become more and more abstract. This has also been true of articles in the Journal of the American Statistical Association (whose early issues were devoted to descriptions of government statistical programs) and the Journal of the Royal Statistical Society (whose early issues contained articles listing detailed agricultural and economic statistics from throughout the British Empire).

数学统计理论曾一度被数学家们认为过于深陷于繁杂的实际问题之中，如今却已变得清晰明了，并臻于精炼，展现出数学之美。亚伯拉罕·瓦尔德通过创建一种被称为“决策理论”的高度抽象的概括，统一了估计理论的研究工作。在决策理论中，不同的理论性质会产生不同的估计准则。R.A.费舍尔在实验设计方面的工作运用了有限群论的定理，并开辟了比较不同处理方法的全新视角。由此诞生了数学的一个分支——“实验设计”，但该领域发表的论文通常涉及极其复杂的实验，以至于任何实践科学家都无法实际应用。

The theories of mathematical statistics, once thought by mathematicians to be too mired in messy practical problems, have become clarified and honed into mathematical beauty. Abraham Wald unified the work on estimation theory by creating a highly abstract generalization known as “decision theory,” wherein different theoretical properties produce different criteria for estimates. R. A. Fisher’s work on the design of experiments made use of theorems from finite group theory and opened up fascinating ways of looking at comparisons of different treatments. From this came a branch of mathematics called “design of experiments,” but the papers published in this field often deal with experiments so complicated that no practicing scientist would ever use them.

最后，随着其他人继续研究安德烈·柯尔莫戈罗夫的早期工作，概率空间和随机过程的概念变得越来越统一，但也越来越抽象。到了20世纪60年代，发表在统计学期刊上的论文开始探讨无限集合，并在这些集合上施加无限的并集和交集，从而形成集合的“σ域”——σ域嵌套在其他σ域之中。由此产生的无限序列在无穷远处收敛，随机过程在时间中不断穿梭于有限的状态集合，并注定要循环往复直至时间终结。数理统计学的末世论与任何宗教的末世论一样复杂，甚至更加复杂。此外，数理统计学的结论不仅是正确的，而且与宗教真理不同，它们是可以被证明的。

Finally, as others continued to examine the early work of Andrei Kolmogorov, the concepts of probability spaces and stochastic processes became more and more unified but more and more abstract. By the 1960s, papers published in statistical journals dealt with infinite sets on which were imposed infinite unions and intersections forming “sigma fields” of sets—with sigma fields nested within sigma fields. The resulting infinite sequences converge at the point of infinity, and stochastic processes hurl through time into small bounded sets of states through which they are doomed to cycle to the end of time. The eschatology of mathematical statistics is as complicated as the eschatology of any religion, or more so. Furthermore, the conclusions of mathematical statistics are not only true but, unlike the truths of religion, they can be proved true.

20世纪80年代，数理统计学家们逐渐意识到，他们的领域已经与实际问题脱节太久。为了满足应用方面的迫切需求，各大学开始设立生物统计学系、流行病学系和应用统计学系。人们试图纠正这一曾经统一的学科的分裂。数理统计学会的会议专门讨论“实际”问题。《美国统计协会杂志》每期都辟出一个版块来探讨应用问题。英国皇家统计学会的三份期刊之一就名为《应用统计学》。[29]然而，抽象的诱惑依然不减。成立于20世纪50年代的生物计量学会创办了一份名为《生物计量学》的期刊，专门发表那些在《生物计量学》中不再受欢迎的应用论文。到了20世纪80年代，《生物计量学》的内容变得过于抽象，以至于其他期刊，例如《医学统计学》，应运而生，以满足对应用论文的需求。

In the 1980s, the mathematical statisticians awoke to a realization that their field had become too far divorced from real problems. To meet the urgent need for applications, universities began setting up departments of biostatistics, departments of epidemiology, and departments of applied statistics. Attempts were made to rectify this breaking apart of the once unified subject. Meetings of the Institute of Mathematical Statistics were devoted to “practical” problems. The Journal of the American Statistical Association set aside a section of every issue to deal with applications. One of the three journals of the Royal Statistical Society was named Applied Statistics.[29] Still, the siren calls of abstraction continued. The Biometric Society, set up in the 1950s, had introduced a journal named Biometrics, which would publish the applied papers that were no longer welcome in Biometrika. By the 1980s, Biometrics had become so abstract in its contents that other journals, like Statistics in Medicine, were created to meet the need for applied papers.

当数理统计出现时，欧美大学的数学系错失了良机。在威尔克斯的引领下，许多大学建立了独立的统计学系。当数字计算机问世时，数学系再次错失良机，将其轻视为仅仅是进行工程计算的工具。独立的计算机科学系应运而生，其中一些是从工程系分离出来的，另一些则是从统计学系分离出来的。下一个涉及全新数学思想的重大革命是20世纪80年代分子生物学的发展。正如我们将在第28章中看到的，数学系和统计学系都错过了这一机遇。

The mathematics departments of American and European universities missed the boat when mathematical statistics arrived on the scene. With Wilks leading the way, many universities developed separate statistics departments. The mathematics departments missed the boat again when the digital computer arrived, disdaining it as a mere machine for doing engineering calculations. Separate computer science departments arose, some of them spun off from engineering departments, others spun off from statistics departments. The next big revolution that involved new mathematical ideas was the development of molecular biology in the 1980s. As we shall see in chapter 28, both the mathematics and the statistics departments missed that particular boat.

塞缪尔·S·威尔克斯于1964年去世，享年58岁。在过去的五十年里，他的众多学生在统计学的发展中发挥了重要作用。为了纪念他，美国统计协会每年颁发SS·威尔克斯奖章，授予那些符合威尔克斯在数学创造力和“现实世界”实践方面标准的人士。这位来自德克萨斯州的农家子弟，成就斐然。

Samuel S. Wilks died at age fifty-eight in 1964. His many students have played major roles in the development of statistics during the last fifty years. His memory is honored by the American Statistical Association with the annual presentation of the S. S. Wilks Medal to someone who meets Wilks’s standards of mathematical creativity and engagement in the “real world.” The old Texas farm boy had made his mark.

C第21章

CHAPTER 21

家族中的天才

A GENIUS IN THE FAMILY

T二十世纪初，东欧和南欧爆发了大规模移民潮，涌向英国、美国、澳大利亚和南非。数百万移民大多来自家乡最贫困的阶层，他们寻求经济机会，摆脱压迫统治者或混乱政府的统治。许多人定居在大城市的贫民窟，他们的孩子被寄予厚望，希望通过教育这根“魔杖”摆脱这种贫困的生活。有些孩子展现出非凡的天赋，甚至有些是天才。本文讲述的就是这样两个移民子女的故事：一个获得了博士学位和两个理学博士学位，另一个则在十四岁时辍学。

The first quarter of the twentieth century witnessed a mass migration from eastern and southern Europe to England, the United States, Australia, and South Africa. Most of these millions of immigrants were from the poorest classes of their homelands, seeking economic opportunity and freedom from oppressive rulers or chaotic governments. Many of them settled in the slums of big cities, where their children were urged to climb out of this squalor via the magic wand of education. Some of those children showed remarkable promise. Some were even geniuses. This is the story of two such children of immigrants, one of whom accumulated a Ph.D. and two Sc.D.s, the other of whom dropped out of high school at age fourteen.

IJ GOOD

I. J. GOOD

摩西·古达克对沙皇及其出生地——沙皇的波兰领地——毫无好感。尤其令他厌恶的是，他根本不想被征召入伍。十七岁那年，他与一位志同道合的朋友一起逃往西方。他们身上只有三十五卢布和一块大奶酪。由于没有火车票，他们只能睡在火车座位底下，用奶酪贿赂检票员。古达克抵达伦敦白教堂的犹太人贫民窟时，除了勇气和健康之外，一无所有。他开了一家钟表店，技艺是靠观察其他钟表匠在橱窗里（光线最好的地方）工作而得来的。后来，他对古董浮雕产生了浓厚的兴趣，最终在大英博物馆附近开了一家专门经营古董珠宝的店铺（资金来自他未来的妻子）。受雇在新店橱窗上绘制店名的招牌画家喝醉了，不懂得如何拼写Goodack，于是店名变成了“Good's Cameo Corner”，而家族姓氏则变成了 Good。

Moses Goodack had no love for the czar or for the czar’s Polish domain, where he was born. In particular, he had no desire to be conscripted into the czar’s army. When he was seventeen, he escaped to the West with a similarly minded friend. They had thirty-five rubles and a large cheese between them. Without railroad tickets, they slept beneath the seats of the trains they took and used the cheese to bribe the ticket takers. Goodack arrived in the Jewish slum district of Whitechapel, in London, with nothing but his pluck and his health. He opened a shop as a watchmaker, having learned his trade by looking at other watchmakers working in their show windows (where the light was best). He became interested in antique cameos and eventually opened a shop near the British Museum that specialized in antique jewelry (using money borrowed from his wife-to-be). The sign painter hired to paint the name of the new business on its window was too drunk to understand how to spell Goodack, so the business became “Good’s Cameo Corner,” and the family name became Good.

摩西·古达克的儿子，IJ·古德，于1916年12月9日出生于伦敦。他原名伊西多尔。年轻的伊西多尔·古德对城里到处张贴的名为《贤德的伊西多尔》的戏剧宣传海报感到尴尬。从那时起，他就被称为杰克，并以IJ·古德的笔名发表文章和书籍。

Moses Goodack’s son, I. J. Good, was born in London on December 9, 1916. His given first name was Isidore. Young Isidore Good was embarrassed by posters that appeared all over town advertising a play entitled The Virtuous Isidore. From that time on, he was known as Jack, and he published his papers and books as I. J. Good.

1993年，杰克·古德在接受大卫·班克斯采访时回忆说，大约九岁时，他发现了数字，并开始熟练地进行心算。当时他得了白喉，不得不卧床休息。他的一位姐姐教他如何开平方根。曾经，在学生学会长除法之后，开平方根是学校常规课程的一部分。它涉及一系列除以二和平方的运算，这些运算在纸上的排列方式与长除法类似。

In an interview with David Banks, in 1993, Jack Good recalled that, when he was about nine years old, he discovered numbers and became proficient at mental arithmetic. He had come down with diphtheria and had to remain in bed. One of his older sisters showed him how to extract a square root. At one time, extraction of square roots was taught as part of the regular school curriculum after the students had learned how to do long division. It involved a sequence of halving and squaring operations that were laid out on the paper in a fashion similar to long division.

被迫静静地躺在床上，他开始心算2的平方根。他发现这个运算会无限进行下去，当他把部分结果平方后，平方结果略小于2。他不断观察出现的数字，试图找到规律，但始终一无所获。他意识到，这个运算可以理解为一个平方数与另一个平方数的两倍之差。因此，只有当存在某种规律时，它才能表示为两个数的比值。十岁的杰克·古德躺在床上，完全依靠心算，发现了2的平方根的无理性。在此过程中，他还找到了一个被称为“佩尔方程”的丢番图问题的解法。尽管2的平方根的无理性早已被古希腊的毕达哥拉斯兄弟会发现，佩尔方程也早在十六世纪就被解出，但这丝毫不能掩盖一个十岁男孩在心算方面取得的非凡成就。

Forced to lie quietly in bed, he began to work out the square root of two in his head. He discovered that the operation continued indefinitely, and when he squared his partial answer the square of that answer was just slightly less than two. He kept examining the numbers that emerged, looking for a pattern, but was unable to find one. He realized that the operation could be thought of as the difference between one square and twice another square. Because of this, it could be represented as the ratio of two numbers only if there was a pattern. Lying in bed, working entirely within his head, ten-year-old Jack Good discovered the irrationality of the square root of two. In the course of this, he also found a solution to a Diophantine problem known as “Pell’s equation.” The facts that the irrationality of the square root of two had been discovered by the Pythagorean Brotherhood in ancient Greece and that Pell’s equation had been solved in the sixteenth century do not detract from this remarkable achievement of mental arithmetic in a ten-year-old boy.

在 1993 年的采访中，杰克·古德沉思道：“哈代（一位活跃于 20 世纪 20 年代和 30 年代的英国数学家）将其描述为古希腊数学家最伟大的成就之一，这并非一项糟糕的发现。被伟人预见到这件事现在对我来说并不陌生，但通常情况下，这种预见会早 2500 年。”

In the 1993 interview, Jack Good mused, “It wasn’t a bad discovery that Hardy [a British mathematician active in the 1920s and 1930s] described as one of the greatest achievements of the ancient Greek mathematicians. Being anticipated by great men is now familiar to me, but it was not usually by 2.5 millennia.”

十二岁时，古德进入了位于汉普斯特德的哈伯戴舍斯·阿斯克学校[30]，这是一所男校，校训是“服务与服从”。这所学校主要招收店主子女，办学标准十分严格。只有10%的学生能够升入最高年级，而其中只有六分之一的人最终进入大学深造。古德早年师从一位名叫斯马特先生的老师。斯马特先生经常在黑板上写一组练习题；有些题目难得令人抓狂，他知道学生们必须花时间才能解答，而他自己则可以在办公桌前处理文书工作。当他写完最后一道题时，年轻的IJ·古德宣布：“我做完了。”“你是说第一道题？”斯马特先生有些惊讶地问道。“不，”杰克回答说，“全部都做完了。”

At age twelve, Good entered the Haberdashers’ Aske’s School[30] in Hampstead, a secondary school for boys whose motto is “Serve and obey.” It catered to the children of shopkeepers and maintained rigorous standards. Only 10 percent of its students were able to make it to the “top form,” the highest grade, and only a sixth of those went on to university education. Early in his career, Good was taught by a schoolmaster named Mr. Smart. Mr. Smart would often write a group of exercise questions on the blackboard; some of them were so devilishly difficult that he knew the students would have to spend time working them out, while he was able to do paperwork at his desk. As he finished writing the last question, young I. J. Good announced, “I’ve finished.” “You mean with the first one?” Mr. Smart asked with some astonishment. “No,” Jack replied. “All of them.”

古德对数学谜题书籍非常着迷，但他更喜欢先看答案，然后再想办法从谜题推导出答案。面对一个关于铅弹堆的问题，他查看了答案，意识到这可以通过繁琐的计算得出，但他对推广这个答案的可能性很感兴趣。在这个过程中，他发现了数学归纳法原理。古德的数学能力突飞猛进。而在此之前，一些数学家仅仅在300年前就已发现了这一原理。

Good was fascinated with books of mathematical puzzles, but he preferred to look at the answers first and then find a way to get from the puzzle to the proposed answer. Confronted with a problem involving piles of shot, he looked at the answer and realized that it could have been arrived at by tedious calculation, but he was intrigued by the possibility of generalizing the answer. In doing so, he discovered the principle of mathematical induction. Good was improving. This was a discovery that had been made by earlier mathematicians only 300 years before.

十九岁时，古德进入剑桥大学，他数学神童的名声早已远播。然而，在这里他发现许多其他同学和他一样才华横溢。他在剑桥耶稣学院的数学导师似乎乐于展示那些经过精心修饰的证明，这些证明过于完美，以至于证明背后的直觉思想完全被抹杀。更糟糕的是，他讲解的速度极快，学生们很难在他擦掉黑板上的字迹开始新的证明之前将其抄写下来。古德在剑桥表现出色，引起了一些资深数学家的注意。1941年，他获得了数学博士学位。他的论文研究的是拓扑学中的部分维数概念，这是对亨利·勒贝格思想的扩展（还记得那位勒贝格吗？耶日·内曼曾非常欣赏他的工作，但当他与年轻的内曼见面时却对内曼非常粗鲁）。

At nineteen, Good entered Cambridge University, his reputation as a mathematical prodigy having preceded him. Here, however, he discovered many other students who were as brilliant as he. His math tutor at Jesus College, Cambridge, seemed to take delight in presenting proofs that had been cleaned up to such a degree of slickness that the intuitive ideas behind the proofs had been completely obliterated. To make things more difficult, he would present the proofs so rapidly that students had difficulty copying them down from the board before he erased the old lines to start new ones. Good excelled at Cambridge, catching the eye of some of the senior mathematicians at the university. In 1941, he emerged with a Ph.D. in mathematics. His thesis dealt with the topological concept of partial dimension, which was an extension of ideas developed by Henri Lebesgue (recall the Lebesgue whose work Jerzy Neyman had admired but who was so rude to young Neyman when they met).

当时正值战争时期，杰克·古德成为伦敦附近布莱切利庄园实验室的一名密码分析员，负责破解德军的秘密密码。密码是将信息中的字母转换成一系列符号或数字。到1940年，这些密码变得非常复杂，每个字母的转换模式都不同。例如，假设你想加密“战争开始了”这条信息。一种方法是给每个字母分配一个数字，这样加密后的信息就变成了：“12 06 14 09 06 23 11 19 20 01 13”。密码分析员会注意到数字06多次出现，并推断这是同一个字母的重复。如果信息足够长，再加上对被加密语言中不同字母的统计频率有所了解，以及一些运气成分，密码分析员通常可以在几个小时内破译这样的信息。

There was a war on, and Jack Good became a cryptanalyst in the laboratories at Bletchley Park, near London, where attempts were being made to break the German secret codes. A secret code consists of converting the letters of the message into a sequence of symbols or numbers. By 1940, these codes had become very complicated, with the pattern of conversion changing with each letter. Suppose, for instance, you want to encrypt the message “War has begun.” One way is to assign numbers to each letter, so the encryption becomes: “12 06 14 09 06 23 11 19 20 01 13.” The cryptanalyst would notice the multiple occurrence of the number 06 and conclude that this was a repetition of the same letter. With a long enough message, and some knowledge of the statistical frequency of different letters in the language being encrypted, along with a few lucky guesses, the cryptanalyst can usually decode such a message in a few hours.

在第一次世界大战的最后几年，德国人研制出一种机器，它能根据每个字母改变密码。第一个字母可能用数字12加密，但在加密下一个字母之前，机器会选择一个完全不同的密码，因此第二个字母可能用数字14加密，再下一个字母的密码又会发生变化，以此类推。这样一来，密码分析员就无法依赖重复的数字来表示相同的字母。这种新型密码必须能够被预期的接收者理解，因此机器在不同密码之间切换的方式必须具有一定的规律性。密码分析员可以通过分析统计模式来估计这种规律性的性质，从而破解密码。密码分析员的任务还可以进一步增加难度：一旦初始密码能够按照固定的模式进行切换，就可以采用一种更复杂的模式，使密码更难破解。

The Germans developed a machine during the last years of World War I that changed the code with each letter. The first letter might be encrypted with 12, but before encrypting the next letter, the machine would pick an entirely different code, so the second letter might be encoded with 14, with a change at the next letter, and so on. In this way, the cryptanalyst cannot depend upon repeated numbers as indicating the same letters. This new type of code has to be understood by its intended recipient, so there has to be some degree of regularity to the way in which the machine changes from one code to another. The cryptanalyst can look at statistical patterns and estimate the nature of the regularity and crack the code that way. The task can be made even harder for the cryptanalyst: Once the initial codes can be shifted by a fixed plan, it is possible to shift the plan by a superfixed plan, and the code becomes even more difficult to break.

所有这些都可以用一个类似于第十三章中分层贝叶斯模型的数学模型来表示。每一层加密的移位模式都可以用参数来表示；因此，我们有测量值、观察到的编码消息中的初始值、描述第一层编码的参数、描述这些参数变化的超参数、描述这些超参数变化的超超参数，以此类推。最终，由于代码必须由接收者破解，因此必须存在一个最终的层级，其中参数是固定的且不可更改的，因此所有此类代码在理论上都是可破解的。

All of this can be represented in a mathematical model that resembles the hierarchal Bayes models of chapter 13. The pattern of shifting at each level of encryption can be represented by parameters; so we have the case of measurements, the initial numbers in the coded messages observed, parameters that describe the first level of coding, hyperparameters that describe the changes in these parameters, hyper-hyperparameters that describe the changes in these hyperparameters, and so forth. Eventually, because the code has to be broken by the recipient, there has to be a final level of the hierarchy where the parameters are fixed and unchangeable, and so all such codes are theoretically breakable.

IJ Good 的主要成就之一是他对经验贝叶斯和层次贝叶斯方法的贡献，这些方法源于他在布莱切利庄园的工作。战时经历使他对数理统计的基本理论产生了浓厚的兴趣。他曾在曼彻斯特大学短暂任教，但英国政府将他重新招回情报部门，使他成为将计算机应用于密码分析问题的重要人物。计算机能够分析海量可能的组合，这促使他开始研究分类理论，该理论根据不同的“接近程度”定义对观测单元进行分类。

One of I. J. Good’s major achievements was the contribution he made to the development of empirical Bayes and hierarchal Bayes methods which he derived from the work he did at Bletchley. He emerged from his wartime experiences with a deep interest in the underlying theories of mathematical statistics. He taught briefly at the University of Manchester, but the British government lured him back into intelligence, where he became an important figure in the adoption of computers to deal with problems of cryptanalysis. The power of the computer to examine vast numbers of possible combinations led him into investigations of classification theory, where observed units are organized in terms of different definitions of “closeness.”

在英国情报部门工作期间，古德又获得了两个高级学位，分别是剑桥大学的理学博士学位和牛津大学的理学博士学位。1967年，他来到美国，在弗吉尼亚理工学院担任大学杰出教授，并一直任教到1994年退休。

While working with British Intelligence, Good picked up two more advanced degrees, a Sc.D. from Cambridge and a D.Sc. from Oxford. He came to the United States in 1967, where he accepted a position as University Distinguished Professor at Virginia Polytechnic Institute and stayed there until his retirement in 1994.

古德一直对数字出现时看似巧合的现象很感兴趣。“我于第七年第七个月第七天第七点抵达弗吉尼亚州布莱克斯堡，被安排在7号楼7号公寓……这完全是巧合。”他接着异想天开地说：“我有个不太成熟的想法，上帝越是怀疑祂的存在，祂就越会制造巧合，从而在不强迫人们相信的情况下，提供证据。”这种对巧合的敏锐洞察力促使他从事统计估计理论的研究。既然人眼能够从纯粹的随机数中发现规律，那么他问道，究竟在什么情况下，这种看似规律的现象才真正具有意义？古德的思维深入探究了数理统计模型背后的含义，他后期的论文和著作也越来越具有哲学意味。

Good was always intrigued by apparent coincidences in the occurrence of numbers. “I arrived in Blacksburg [Virginia] in the seventh hour of the seventh day of the seventh month of the year seven in the seventh decade, and I was put in apartment 7 of block 7 … all by chance.” He continued, whimsically, “I have a quarter-baked idea that God provides more coincidences the more one doubts Her existence, thereby providing one with evidence without forcing one to believe.” This eye for coincidences led to work in statistical estimation theory. Because the human eye is capable of seeing patterns in purely random numbers, at what point, he asked, does an apparent pattern really mean something? Good’s mind probed the underlying meaning of the models used in mathematical statistics, and his later papers and books tend to become more and more philosophical.

波斯执事

PERSI DIACONIS

1945年1月31日，出生于纽约的希腊移民家庭的珀西·迪亚科尼斯（Persi Diaconis）的人生轨迹与他以往的经历截然不同。和IJ·古德（IJ Good）一样，年轻的珀西也对数学谜题着迷。古德阅读的是H·E·杜德尼（H.E. Dudeney）的著作，杜德尼的数学谜题曾风靡维多利亚时代的英国；而迪亚科尼斯则阅读马丁·加德纳（Martin Gardner）在《科学美国人》（Scientific American）杂志上发表的“数学娱乐”（Mathematical Recreations）专栏。最终，还在读高中的迪亚科尼斯结识了马丁·加德纳。加德纳的专栏经常探讨老千的伎俩和各种使事物看起来不同的方法，这些内容引起了迪亚科尼斯的兴趣，尤其是当它们涉及复杂的概率问题时。

An entirely different career awaited Persi Diaconis, born to Greek immigrants in New York, on January 31, 1945. Like I. J. Good, young Persi was intrigued with mathematical puzzles. Whereas Good read the books of H. E. Dudeney, whose mathematical puzzles entertained Victorian England, Diaconis read the “Mathematical Recreations” column by Martin Gardner in Scientific American. Eventually, while still in high school, Diaconis met Martin Gardner. Gardner’s column often dealt with tricks of cardsharps and methods used to make things appear to be different, and these interested Diaconis, especially when they involved intricate problems in probability.

佩尔西·迪亚科尼斯对纸牌和魔术着迷不已，14岁那年就离家出走。他从5岁就开始表演魔术。在纽约，他经常出入其他魔术师聚集的商店和餐馆。在一家咖啡馆里，他遇到了迪亚·弗农，弗农当时带着魔术表演团在美国各地巡回演出。弗农邀请佩尔西加入他的表演团担任助手。“我欣然接受了这个机会，”迪亚科尼斯回忆道，“我直接就走了。我没告诉父母，就这么离开了。”

So intrigued was Persi Diaconis with cards and tricks that he ran away from home at age fourteen. He had been doing magic tricks since he was five years old. In New York, he frequented the shops and restaurants where other magicians gathered. At one cafeteria he met Dia Vernon, who traveled around the United States with a magic show. Vernon invited Persi to join his show as an assistant. “I jumped at the chance,” Diaconis related. “I just went off. I didn’t tell my parents; I just left.”

当时弗农已经六十多岁了。迪亚科尼斯跟随他巡演了两年，学习了弗农的各种魔术技巧和道具。之后，弗农结束了巡演生涯，在洛杉矶开了一家魔术商店，而迪亚科尼斯则继续进行自己的巡回魔术表演。人们很难念出他的名字，所以他改名为珀西·沃伦（Persi Warren）。正如他自己所说：

Vernon was in his sixties at the time. Diaconis traveled with him for two years, learning Vernon’s repertoire of tricks and devices. Then Vernon left the road to settle down with a magic shop in Los Angeles, and Diaconis continued a traveling magic show of his own. People had trouble pronouncing his name, so he took as his professional name Persi Warren. As he recounted:

那不算什么好日子，但也还行。在卡茨基尔山脉工作。通常的情况是，有人看上你，喜欢你，然后说：“嘿，你有没有想过来波士顿？……我帮你付机票和200美元。”……然后你就去了波士顿……住进了演艺人员的公寓。你在那里演出，也许经纪人会帮你找到另一份工作，等等。

That’s not a great life, but it’s OK. Working in the Catskills. What happens is that you get somebody who sees you and likes you and says, “Gee, would you ever think of coming to Boston? … I’ll pay your fare and $200.” … And you go to Boston … and you check into a show business rooming house. You do that date and maybe an agent will get you another job while you’re there, and so forth.

二十四岁那年，珀西·迪亚科尼斯厌倦了四处巡演的魔术师生涯，回到了纽约。他没有高中毕业文凭。他曾被安排跳级学习，十四岁辍学时，距离高中毕业只剩不到一年的时间。没有毕业证，他便进入纽约市立学院（CCNY）的通识教育项目学习。他发现，在他离家的这些年里，他收到了大量来自军队、各技术学院和大学的信件。这些信件都是邀请他入学的格式信函，开头都是“亲爱的毕业生”。原来，在他离家逃学期间，老师们还是决定让他毕业，并给他报了所有课程的最终成绩，这些成绩足以让他毕业。年轻的珀西并不知道，他实际上已经是纽约乔治·华盛顿高中的毕业生了。

At age twenty-four, Persi Diaconis returned to New York, tired of the traveling magician game. He did not have a high school diploma. He had been accelerated in school, and, when he dropped out at age fourteen, he had less than a year to complete his high school education. Without a diploma, he enrolled in the general studies program at City College of New York (CCNY). He found that over the years he had been away from home, he had been sent a great deal of mail from the army and from different technical institutes and colleges. They were all form letters inviting him to join their institutions, and they all began, “Dear Graduate.” It turned out that when he ran away from home and school, his teachers decided to put him up for graduation anyway, giving him final grades for the courses he was taking, which would have been adequate for him to graduate. Unknown to young Persi, he was officially a graduate of George Washington High School in New York.

他上大学的理由很奇特。他买了一本普林斯顿大学威廉·费勒（William Feller）撰写的概率论研究生教材。他觉得这本书很难理解（就像大多数试图读懂费勒那本结构严谨的《概率论及其应用导论》第一卷[31]的人一样）。迪亚科尼斯进入纽约市立学院（CCNY）学习，是为了掌握足够的数学知识来理解费勒的著作。1971年，26岁的他从纽约市立学院获得了本科学位。

He went to college for a strange reason. He had bought a copy of a graduate level textbook on probability theory by William Feller of Princeton University. He had difficulty understanding it (as do most people who try to make their way through Feller’s tightly written Introduction to Probability Theory and Its Applications, Vol. I[31]). Diaconis entered CCNY in order to learn enough formal mathematics to understand Feller. In 1971, at age twenty-six, he received his undergraduate degree from CCNY.

他被多所大学的数学研究生项目录取。有人告诉他，纽约市立学院（CCNY）的学生从来没有人能考上哈佛大学数学系（这并非事实），所以他决定申请哈佛大学统计系而不是数学系。他一心想去哈佛，后来他回忆说，如果他不喜欢统计学，“那就转到数学系之类的。他们会发现我其实很厉害……”然后他就会接受转学。统计学确实让他很感兴趣；1974年，他获得了数理统计学博士学位，并在斯坦福大学任教，最终晋升为正教授。截至本文撰写之时，他仍然是哈佛大学的教授。

He was accepted by a number of different universities for graduate studies in mathematics. He had been told that no one ever got into the Harvard mathematics department from CCNY (which was not true), so he decided to apply to the Harvard statistics department instead of the mathematics department. He wanted to go to Harvard and he thought, as he said later, that if he didn’t like statistics, “Well, I’ll transfer to math or something. They’ll learn that I’m terrific …” and would accept the transfer. Statistics did interest him; he got his Ph.D. in mathematical statistics in 1974 and took a position at Stanford University, where he rose to the position of full professor. As of this writing, he is a professor at Harvard University.

电子计算机彻底改变了统计分析的本质。起初，计算机用于进行与费舍尔、耶茨等人类似的分析，只是速度更快，功能更强大。回想一下（第17章中提到的），杰里·康菲尔德在求一个24×24矩阵的逆时遇到了困难。如今，我桌上的计算机可以求100×100阶矩阵的逆（尽管遇到这种情况的人很可能没有正确定义问题）。对于病态矩阵，计算机可以进行运算以生成广义逆矩阵，这在20世纪50年代还只是纯粹的理论概念。计算机可以对涉及多种处理和交叉索引的实验设计产生的数据进行大型、复杂的方差分析。这类工作涉及的数学模型和统计概念可以追溯到20世纪20年代和30年代。那么，计算机还能用于其他用途吗？

The electronic computer has completely restructured the nature of statistical analysis. At first, the computer was used to do the same sort of analyses that had been done by Fisher, Yates, and others, only much faster and with much greater ambition. Recall (from chapter 17) the difficulty Jerry Cornfield had when it was necessary to invert a 24 × 24 matrix. Today, the computer that sits on my desk can invert matrices on the order of 100 × 100 (although anyone who gets stuck with such a situation has probably not done a good job of defining the problem). Matrices that are ill-conditioned can be operated on to produce generalized inverses, a concept that was purely theoretical in the 1950s. Large, complicated analyses of variances can be run on data generated by experimental designs that involve multiple treatments and cross-indexing. This sort of effort involves mathematical models and statistical concepts that go back to the 1920s and 1930s. Can the computer be used for something different?

20世纪70年代，在斯坦福大学，迪亚科尼斯加入了一群年轻的统计学家，他们研究计算机和数理统计的结构，并提出了这个问题。早期的答案之一是一种被称为“投影寻踪”的数据分析方法。现代计算机的弊端之一在于，它可以构建规模庞大的数据集。例如，假设我们正在追踪一组被诊断为心脏病高风险的患者。我们每六个月带他们到诊所进行一次观察。每次就诊时，我们抽取10毫升血液，分析其中100多种不同酶的水平，其中许多酶被认为与心脏病有关。我们还对患者进行超声心动图检查，得出大约六项不同的测量结果，并进行心电图监测（例如，让他们佩戴一种可以记录一天内90万次心跳的设备）。他们会被测量、称重、戳刺、探查，以检查临床体征和症状，结果又进行了三四十次测量。

In the 1970s, at Stanford University, Diaconis joined a group of other young statisticians who were looking at the structure of the computer and of mathematical statistics, asking that very question. One of the earlier answers was a method of data analysis known as “projection pursuit.” One of the curses of the modern computer is that it is possible to assemble data sets of huge dimension. Suppose, for instance, we are following a group of patients who have been diagnosed at high risk for heart disease. We bring them into the clinic for observation once every six months. At each visit, we take 10 cc of blood and analyze it for the levels of over 100 different enzymes, many of which are thought to be related to heart disease. We also subject the patients to echocardiograms, producing about six different measures, and to EKG monitoring (perhaps having them wear a device that records all 900,000 heartbeats in a given day). They are measured, and weighed, and poked, and probed for clinical signs and symptoms, resulting in another thirty to forty measurements.

这些数据可以用来做什么？

What can be done with all this data?

假设我们每次门诊就诊时对每位患者进行 500 次测量，并且在整个研究过程中，每位患者共就诊 10 次。那么每位患者总共提供 5000 次测量。如果研究中有 20000 名患者，这可以表示为 5000 维空间中的 20000 个点。不要想当然地认为我们只能在四维空间中穿梭，这种想法在科幻小说中很常见。在统计分析的现实世界中，处理数千维空间并不罕见。20 世纪 50 年代，理查德·贝尔曼提出了一系列定理，他称之为“维度诅咒”。这些定理指出，随着空间维度的增加，获得参数的良好估计变得越来越困难。一旦分析人员进入十维到二十维的空间，少于数十万个观测值就无法检测到任何异常。

Suppose we produce 500 measurements at each clinic visit for each patient, and that the patients are seen at 10 different visits over the course of the study. Each patient provides us with 5,000 measurements. If there are 20,000 patients in the study, this can be represented as 20,000 points in a 5,000-dimensional space. Forget the idea of travel through a mere four dimensions that preoccupies much of science fiction. In the real world of statistical analysis, it is not unusual to have to deal with a space of thousands of dimensions. In the 1950s, Richard Bellman produced a set of theorems, which he called “the curse of dimensionality.” What these theorems say is that, as the dimension of the space increases, it becomes less and less possible to get good estimates of parameters. Once the analyst is out in ten- to twenty-dimensional space, it is not possible to detect anything with less than hundreds of thousands of observations.

贝尔曼定理基于标准的统计分析方法。斯坦福大学的研究小组意识到，真实数据并非像5000维空间那样杂乱无章地散布在各处。实际上，数据倾向于聚集到较低维度。想象一下，三维空间中散布的点实际上都位于同一个平面上，甚至在同一条直线上。这就是真实数据的分布情况。临床研究中每位患者的5000个观测值并非毫无规律地散布，而是具有一定的结构。这是因为许多测量值彼此之间存在相关性。（普林斯顿大学和贝尔实验室的约翰·图基曾提出，至少在医学领域，数据的真实“维度”通常不超过五维。）基于这一洞见，斯坦福大学的研究小组开发了计算密集型技术来寻找实际存在的低维度。其中应用最广泛的技术是投影寻踪法。

Bellman’s theorems were based on the standard methodology of statistical analysis. What the Stanford group realized was that real data are not scattered helter-skelter in this 5,000-dimensional space. The data actually tend to bunch up into lower dimensions. Imagine a scatter of points in three dimensions that really all lie on a single plane or even on a single line. This is what happens to real data. The 5,000 observations on each patient in the clinical study are not scattered about without some structure. This is because so many of the measurements are correlated with each other. (John Tukey from Princeton and Bell Labs once proposed that, at least in medicine, the true “dimensionality” of data is often no more than five.) Working with this insight, the Stanford group developed computer-intensive techniques for searching out the lower dimension that is really there. The most widely used of these techniques is projection pursuit.

与此同时，这种信息以庞大而无序的形式泛滥，引起了其他科学家的关注，信息科学领域也在许多大学应运而生。在很多情况下，这些信息科学家接受的是工程学训练，对数理统计领域的诸多最新进展并不了解。因此，计算机科学领域也出现了类似的发展趋势，它有时会重新发掘统计学的素材，有时则会开辟出R.A. Fisher及其追随者始料未及的新领域。本书最后一章将对此进行探讨。

In the meantime, this proliferation of information in large, ill-structured masses has attracted the interest of other scientists, and the field of information science has emerged at many universities. In many cases, these information scientists have engineering training and are unaware of many of the recent developments of mathematical statistics, so there has been a parallel development in the world of computer science that sometimes rediscovers the material of statistics and sometimes opens up new doors that were never anticipated by R. A. Fisher or his followers. This is a topic of the final chapter of this book.

C章22

CHAPTER 22

统计学界的毕加索

THE PICASSO OF STATISTICS

W1966年我完成博士论文后，便走访了多所大学，就我的研究成果发表演讲，并接受面试，希望能获得教职。我的第一站是普林斯顿大学，约翰·图基在火车站迎接了我。

When I completed my doctoral thesis in 1966, I made the rounds of different universities, giving talks about my results and allowing myself to be interviewed for a possible position. One of my first stops was Princeton University, where I was met at the train station by John Tukey.

在我的学习中，我掌握了图基对比、图基交互作用的单自由度、图基快速傅里叶变换、图基快速检验以及图基引理。但我当时还不了解他在探索性数据分析领域的工作，也不知道他那丰富的智慧将在未来几年里迸发出怎样的成果。约翰·图基是统计系主任（同时也在贝尔电话实验室任职），他亲自迎接我的到来让我感到十分意外。他穿着棉质斜纹裤，搭配宽松的运动衬衫和运动鞋或休闲鞋。而我则穿着西装打着领带。六十年代的着装风潮尚未席卷到教职人员层面，所以我的穿着比他更得体。图基陪我穿过校园。我们聊起了普林斯顿的生活状况。他询问了我撰写论文期间使用的计算机程序。他还向我展示了一些避免程序中舍入误差的技巧。我们终于到达了我要做论文报告的大厅。图基介绍完我之后，便走到房间最后一排的座位上坐了下来。我开始汇报我的研究成果。汇报过程中，我注意到他正忙着批改论文。

In my studies, I had learned about Tukey contrasts, Tukey’s one degree of freedom for interaction, Tukey’s fast Fourier transforms, Tukey’s quick test, and Tukey’s lemma. I had not yet learned about his work in exploratory data analysis or any of the work that was to flow from his fecund mind in the coming years. John Tukey was chairman of the Department of Statistics (and also held a joint appointment at Bell Telephone Laboratories), and it was surprising to me that he would personally greet my arrival. He was dressed in cotton chino pants with a loose sport shirt and tennis shoes or sneakers. I was dressed in a suit and tie. The sartorial revolution of the sixties had not yet risen to the faculty level, so my style of dress was more appropriate than his. Tukey walked with me across campus. We discussed living conditions at Princeton. He inquired about the computer programs I had written while working on my thesis. He showed me some tricks to avoid round-off error in my programs. We finally arrived at the hall where I was to give a talk on my thesis. After introducing me, Tukey climbed up to the last row of seats in the room. I began to present my results. As I did so, I noticed that he was busy correcting papers.

我的演讲结束后，听众中有几位（包括研究生和教职员工）提出了一些问题，或者探讨了我所做工作的潜在影响。当大家都没有其他意见或问题时，约翰·图基从后排走了过来。他拿起粉笔，把我的主要定理完整地写在了黑板上，连我所有的符号都一并复制了下来。[32]然后，他向我展示了一个我花了几个月时间才用自己的方法证明的定理的替代证明。“哇，”我心想，“这才是真正的高手！”

When I finished my presentation, several of the people in the audience (which consisted of graduate students and faculty members) asked some questions or suggested ramifications of what I had done. When it was clear that no one else had comments or questions, John Tukey walked down from the back row. He took a piece of chalk and reproduced my main theorem on the blackboard, complete with all my notation.[32] He then showed me an alternative proof of the theorem it had taken me months to prove in my own way. “Wow,” I said to myself. “This is the big leagues!”

约翰·图基于1915年出生于马萨诸塞州新贝德福德。他说话时带着浓重的波士顿口音。他的父母很早就发现了他的天赋，因此他一直接受家庭教育，直到离开家前往布朗大学，在那里获得了化学学士和硕士学位。他对抽象数学产生了浓厚的兴趣。之后，他继续在普林斯顿大学攻读研究生，并于1939年获得数学博士学位。他的早期研究领域是拓扑学。点集拓扑为数学提供了基本的理论基础。在拓扑学的基础之下，存在着一个被称为“元数学”的深奥哲学分支。元数学探讨的是“解决”一个数学问题的意义，以及逻辑运用背后隐藏的假设。图基深入研究了这些晦涩难懂的基础理论，最终提出了“图基引理”，这是他对该领域的一项重大贡献。

John Tukey was born in 1915 in New Bedford, Massachusetts. The lingering sound of his near-Boston accent salted his speech. His parents recognized his genius early, and he was taught at home until he left to attend Brown University, where he earned his bachelor’s and master’s degrees in chemistry. He had become intrigued by abstract mathematics. He continued his graduate work at Princeton University, where he received his doctorate in mathematics in 1939. His initial work was in the field of topology. Point set topology provides an underlying theoretical foundation for mathematics. Beneath the topological foundation is a difficult and arcane branch of philosophy known as “metamathematics.” Metamathematics deals with questions of what it means to “solve” a mathematical problem, and what are the unstated assumptions behind the use of logic. Tukey plunged into these murky foundations and emerged with “Tukey’s lemma,” a major contribution to the field.

然而，约翰·图基注定不会一直停留在抽象数学领域。塞缪尔·S·威尔克斯当时在普林斯顿大学数学系任教，他鼓励学生和年轻教师们进入数理统计的世界。图基获得博士学位后，被任命为该校数学系的讲师。1938年，在他撰写博士论文期间，他发表了第一篇数理统计论文。到1944年，他几乎所有的出版物都集中在这个领域。

John Tukey was not destined to remain in abstract mathematics, however. Samuel S. Wilks was on the Princeton mathematics faculty, pushing students and young faculty members into the world of mathematical statistics. Upon receiving his doctorate, Tukey was named an instructor in the Department of Mathematics at the university. In 1938, while still working on his thesis, he published his first paper in mathematical statistics. By 1944, almost all of his publications were in that field.

二战期间，图基加入了火控研究办公室，从事火炮瞄准、测距仪评估以及其他相关军械问题的研究。这段经历为他提供了许多统计学问题的实例，这些实例在他日后的研究工作中发挥了重要作用。同时，这段经历也让他对实际问题的本质有了深刻的理解。他以精辟的格言著称，这些格言往往能概括重要的经验教训。其中一条格言就源于他的实践工作：“与其对错误的问题给出精确的答案，不如对正确的问题给出近似的答案。”

During World War II, Tukey joined the Fire Control Research Office, working on problems of aiming guns, evaluating ranging instruments, and related problems of ordnance. This experience provided him with many examples of statistical problems that he would investigate in future years. It also gave him a great appreciation for the nature of practical problems. He is noted for his one-line aphorisms that sum up important experiences. One of these, emerging from his practical work, is “It is better to have an approximate answer to the right question than an exact answer to the wrong one.”

图基的多才多艺

TUKEY’S VERSATILITY

二十世纪初，巴勃罗·毕加索以其变化多端的创作震惊了艺术界。他曾一度沉迷于单色绘画，随后创立了立体主义，之后又探索了古典主义，最终转向陶瓷创作。每一次探索都给艺术带来了革命性的变革，即便毕加索之后转向其他领域，后人依然继续发扬光大。约翰·图基的经历亦是如此。20世纪50年代，他开始研究安德烈·柯尔莫戈罗夫关于随机过程的理论，并发明了一种基于计算机的分析长串相关结果的技术，即“快速傅里叶变换”（FFT）。如同毕加索和立体主义一样，图基的成就已臻化境，他对科学的影响也将是巨大的。

Pablo Picasso astonished the world of art in the early years of the twentieth century with his protean output. For a while, he played with monochromatic paintings, then he invented cubism, then he examined a form of classicism, then he went on to ceramics. Each of these excursions resulted in a revolutionary change in art, which others continued to exploit after Picasso went on to other things. So it has been with John Tukey. In the 1950s, he became involved with Andrei Kolmogorov’s ideas on stochastic processes and invented a computer-based technique for analyzing long strings of correlated results, known as the “fast Fourier transform,” or FFT. Like Picasso and cubism, Tukey could have accomplished nothing more and his influence on science would have been immense.

1945年，图基因战时工作来到新泽西州默里山的贝尔电话实验室，在那里他参与了各种实际问题的研究。“我们当时有一位名叫布登博姆的工程师，”他在1987年的一次录音谈话中说道，“他正在研制一种新型的、性能特别优异的跟踪雷达，用于跟踪空中目标。他想去加州发表一篇论文，需要一张图片来展示他的跟踪误差情况。”布登博姆在频域中构建了他的问题，但不知道如何获得频率振幅的一致估计值。虽然图基作为一名数学家熟悉傅里叶变换，但他当时还没有接触过这项技术在工程领域的应用。他提出了一种似乎能够满足这位工程师需求的方法（回想一下图基关于对正确问题给出近似答案的格言）。然而，图基本人并不满意，他继续思考这个问题。

In 1945, Tukey’s war work took him to Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, where he became involved in different practical problems. “We had an engineer named Budenbom,” he said in a conversation recorded in 1987, “who had been building a new, especially good, tracking radar for tracking aerial targets. He wanted to go to California to give a paper and he wanted a picture to show what his tracking errors were like.” Budenbom had formulated his problem in the frequency domain but did not know how to get consistent estimates of the frequency amplitudes. Although Tukey, as a mathematician, was familiar with Fourier transformations, he had not yet been exposed to the uses of this technique in engineering. He proposed a method that seemed to satisfy the engineer (recall Tukey’s aphorism about the usefulness of an approximate answer to the right question). Tukey himself was not satisfied, however, and continued thinking about the problem.

由此产生了快速傅里叶变换。这是一种平滑技术，用图基的话来说，它“借用”了相邻频率的“能量”，因此无需大量数据即可获得良好的估计结果。它也是一种经过精心设计的理论解决方案，具有最优特性。最后，它还是一种高效的计算机算法。在20世纪50年代和60年代，计算机速度远低于现在，内存也更小，因此需要这样一种算法。它一直沿用到21世纪，因为它的精度优于一些现在可以实现的更复杂的变换估计方法。

The result was the fast Fourier transform. It is a smoothing technique that, to use Tukey’s expression, “borrows strength” from neighboring frequencies, so vast amounts of data are not needed to get good estimates. It is also a carefully thought-out theoretical solution with optimum properties. Finally, it is a highly efficient computer algorithm. Such an algorithm was needed in the 1950s and 1960s, when computers were much slower and had smaller memories. It continues to be used into the twenty-first century, because it is superior in its accuracy to some more complicated transformation estimates that can now be done.

计算机及其强大的计算能力不断拓展着统计研究的边界。我们之前已经看到，计算机使得计算大型矩阵的逆成为可能（这如果用机械计算器，杰里·康菲尔德恐怕要花费数百年时间才能完成）。计算机还有另一个方面，它有可能颠覆统计理论，那就是计算机存储和分析海量数据的能力。

The computer and its capacities have continually been advancing the frontiers of statistical research. We have seen previously how the computer made it possible to compute inverses of large matrices (something that would have taken Jerry Cornfield hundreds of years of labor on a mechanical calculator). There is another aspect of the computer that threatens to overwhelm statistical theory. This is the capacity of the computer to store and analyze huge amounts of data.

20世纪60年代和70年代初，贝尔电话实验室的工程师和统计学家率先开展了海量数据的分析。对电话线路进行随机故障和问题的监测，使得单个计算机文件中包含了数百万条数据。此外，从探测火星、木星和其他行星的太空探测器遥测传回的数据，也产生了包含数百万条数据的文件。如何才能查看如此庞大的数据量？又该如何着手构建数据结构以便进行分析呢？

In the 1960s and early 1970s, engineers and statisticians at Bell Telephone Laboratories pioneered the analysis of huge amounts of data. Monitoring telephone lines for random errors and problems led to millions and millions of data items in a single computer file. Data being telemetered from space probes sent to examine Mars and Jupiter and other planets produced other files of millions of items. How can you look at so much data? How do you begin to structure it so it can be examined?

我们总能运用卡尔·皮尔逊开创的技术来估计概率分布的参数。但这需要我们对这些分布做出一些假设——例如，它们属于皮尔逊分布系统。我们能否找到一些方法，在不预设分布规律的情况下，检验这些庞大的数据集合并从中有所收获？从某种意义上说，这正是优秀科学家一直以来所做的。格雷戈尔·孟德尔进行了一系列植物杂交实验，观察实验结果，并逐步发展出他的显性基因和隐性基因理论。尽管大量的科学研究都涉及收集数据并将这些数据拟合到预先设定的特定分布模型中，但仅仅收集数据并仔细检查其中是否存在意外情况，往往也是有用且重要的。

We can always estimate the parameters of the probability distributions, following the techniques pioneered by Karl Pearson. This requires us to assume something about those distributions—that they belong to the Pearson system, for instance. Can we find methods for examining these vast collections of numbers and learn something from them without imposing assumptions about their distributions? In some sense, this is what good scientists have always done. Gregor Mendel ran a series of plant crossing experiments, examining the output and gradually developing his theories of dominant and recessive genes. Although a great deal of scientific research involves collecting data and fitting those data to the preconceived mold of some specific type of distribution, it is often useful and important just to collect data and examine them carefully for unexpected events.

正如美国数学家埃里克·坦普尔·贝尔曾经指出的那样，“数字不会说谎，但它们倾向于说真话，却意图欺骗。” [33]人类倾向于发现规律，并且常常会在只有随机噪声的地方看到规律。[34]

As Eric Temple Bell, the American mathematician, once pointed out, “Numbers do not lie, but they have the propensity to tell the truth with intent to deceive.”[33] The human being is prone to seeing patterns and will often see patterns where only random noise exists.[34]

在流行病学中，这种情况尤其令人担忧，因为数据分析常常会在特定地点或时间发现疾病的“聚集性”爆发。假设我们在马萨诸塞州的一个小镇发现白血病患儿数量异常高。这是否意味着该镇存在某种致癌因素？或者这仅仅是一个偶然出现的聚集性病例，同样也可能出现在其他地方？假设镇民发现附近城镇的一家化工厂一直在向池塘倾倒化学废料。假设他们还在白血病聚集爆发地的供水系统中发现了芳香胺的痕迹。这是否可能是导致白血病的原因？更广义地说，我们究竟能在多大程度上通过寻找模式来分析数据，并期望从中发现一些有意义的信息，而不仅仅是偶然出现的灵异现象？

This is particularly disturbing in epidemiology, when an examination of data often finds a “cluster” of diseases in a certain place or time. Suppose we find an unusually high number of children with leukemia in a small town in Massachusetts. Does this mean that there is some cause of cancer operating in this town? Or is this just a random cluster that happened to appear here and could just as easily have appeared elsewhere? Suppose the townspeople discover that a chemical plant has been dumping waste chemicals into a pond in a nearby town. Suppose they also find traces of aromatic amines in the water supply of the town where the cluster of leukemia occurred. Is this a possible cause of the leukemia? In a more general sense, to what extent can we examine data with an eye toward patterns and expect to find anything more than a random will-o’-the-wisp?

20世纪60年代，约翰·图基开始认真思考这些问题。他由此发展出一种高度完善的卡尔·皮尔逊数据分析方法。他意识到，观测数据的分布本身就可以被视为一种分布，而无需强加任何任意的概率模型。由此，他发表了一系列论文，在会议上发表了演讲，最终出版了专著，探讨他所谓的“探索性数据分析”。在研究这些问题的过程中，图基的表达方式也呈现出一种引人注目的原创性。为了促使听众和读者重新审视他们的假设，他开始使用与以往不同的名称来描述数据分布的特征。他还摒弃了以标准概率分布作为分析起点的做法，转而关注数据本身的模式。他研究了极端值如何影响我们对模式的观察。为了纠正这种错误的印象，他开发了一系列用于展示数据的图形工具。

In the 1960s, John Tukey began to give serious consideration to these problems. He emerged from this with a highly refined version of Karl Pearson’s approach to data. He realized that the distribution of observed data can be examined as a distribution, without imposing some arbitrary probability model on it. The result was a series of papers, talks at meetings, and finally books dealing with what he called “exploratory data analysis.” As he worked on the problems, Tukey’s mode of presentation took on a strikingly original form. To shock his listeners and readers into reexamining their assumptions, he began to give different names to the characteristics of data distributions than had been used in the past. He also moved away from standard probability distributions as the starting point of analysis and toward examination of the patterns within the data themselves. He looked at the way in which extreme values can influence our observations of patterns. To adjust for such false impressions, he developed a group of graphical tools for displaying data.

例如，他指出，用于展示数据集分布的标准直方图往往会产生误导。它们会引导人们的视线聚焦于出现频率最高的观测值类别。他提出，与其绘制观测值的频率，不如绘制频率的平方根。他称之为“根图”（rootgram），以区别于直方图。图基还提出，应将数据的中心区域绘制成箱形，并将极值绘制成从箱形延伸出的线条（或他称之为“须线”）。他提出的一些工具已成为许多标准统计软件包的一部分，分析人员现在可以调用“箱线图”和“茎叶图”。图基丰富的想象力席卷了数据分析领域，他的许多提议至今尚未被纳入计算机软件。他的两项发明已被纳入英语词汇。是 Tukey 创造了“bit ”（表示“二进制数字”）和“ software”（计算机程序，与“硬件”——计算机相对）这两个词。

For instance, he showed that the standard histograms for displaying the distribution of a set of data tend to be misleading. They lead the eye to looking at the most frequent class of observations. He proposed that, instead of plotting the frequency of observations, we plot the square root of the frequency. He called this a “rootgram,” as opposed to a histogram. Tukey also proposed that the central region of data be plotted as a box and that extreme values be plotted as lines (or, as he called them, “whiskers”) emanating from the box. Some of the tools he proposed have become part of many standard statistical software packages, and the analyst can now call for “box plots” and “stem and leaf plots.” Tukey’s fertile imagination has swept through the landscape of data analysis, and many of his proposals have yet to be incorporated into computer software. Two of his inventions have been incorporated into the English language. It was Tukey who coined the words bit (for “binary digit”) and software (computer programs, as opposed to “hardware”—the computer).

对图基来说，没有什么平凡到他不会用独到的见解去批判，也没有什么神圣不可侵犯到他不会质疑的。就拿简单的计数来说吧。大多数读者可能都接触过计数工具。我们这些代代相传的老师教给我们的最常见的计数方法是：先画四条竖线，再划掉前面的四条。读者们又见过多少幅漫画，画着衣衫褴褛的囚犯在监狱墙上用粉笔画着一连串这样的计数线呢？

Nothing has been too mundane for Tukey to attack with original insight, and nothing is too sacrosanct for him to question. Take the simple process of tallying. Most readers have probably been exposed to the use of a tally figure when counting something. The usual one, presented to us by generations of teachers, is to make four vertical marks and a fifth one crossing out the four. How many cartoons has the reader seen where a ragged prisoner has chalked a series of these tally marks on the wall of his prison?

约翰·图基说，这种计数方式很愚蠢。想想看，出错有多容易。你可能把叉号画在三条线而不是四条线上，或者画五条线后再画一个叉号。错误的计数很难发现。除非你仔细检查竖线的数量，否则它看起来就像是正确的。使用容易发现错误的计数符号更有意义。图基提出了一种十点计数法。首先，标记四个点，构成一个方框的四个角。然后用四条线连接这些点，完成一个方框。最后，在方框内画两条对角线，形成一个叉号。

It is a foolish way to tally, said John Tukey. Consider how easy it is to make a mistake. You might put the cross over three instead of four lines, or you might put down five lines and then a cross. The incorrect tally is hard to spot. It looks like a correct one unless you carefully examine the number of vertical lines. It makes more sense to use a tally mark where errors can be easily spotted. Tukey proposed a ten-mark tally. You first mark four dots to make the corners of a box. You then connect the dots with four lines, completing the box. Finally, you make two diagonal marks forming a cross within the box.

这些例子，例如快速傅里叶变换和探索性数据分析，仅仅是图基浩瀚研究成果的一部分。如同毕加索从立体主义到古典主义，再到陶瓷和织物，约翰·图基在二十世纪下半叶的统计学领域中不断探索，从时间序列到线性模型，再到对费舍尔一些被遗忘的研究成果的推广，再到稳健估计和探索性数据分析。他从深奥的元数学理论出发，转向实际问题的研究，最终关注非结构化数据的评估。他所到之处，统计学都发生了翻天覆地的变化。直到2000年夏天去世的那一天，约翰·图基仍在用新思想和对旧理论的质疑挑战着他的朋友和同事。

These examples, the fast Fourier transform, and exploratory data analysis, are just part of Tukey’s enormous output. Like Picasso going from cubism, to classicism, to ceramics, to fabrics, John Tukey marched across the statistical landscape of the second half of the twentieth century, from time series, to linear models, to generalizations of some of Fisher’s forgotten work, to robust estimation, to exploratory data analysis. From the deep theory of metamathematics, he emerged to consider practical problems, and finally to consider the unstructured evaluation of data. Wherever he put his mark, statistics was no longer the same. To the very day he died in the summer of 2000, John Tukey was still challenging his friends and coworkers with new ideas and questions about the old.

C第23章

CHAPTER 23

处理污染

DEALING WITH CONTAMINATION

T为统计方法的使用提供依据的数学定理通常假设科学实验或观察中的所有测量结果都同样有效。如果分析人员只挑选那些看似正确的数据，那么最终的统计分析结果可能会出现严重的误差。当然，这正是科学家们经常犯的错误。20世纪80年代初，斯蒂芬·斯蒂格勒查阅了18、19世纪伟大科学家的笔记，例如1907年因测定光速而获得诺贝尔奖的阿尔伯特·迈克尔逊。斯蒂格勒发现，所有这些科学家在开始计算之前都舍弃了一些数据。17世纪初发现行星绕太阳运行的轨道是椭圆的约翰内斯·开普勒，也是通过查阅从古希腊时期开始的天文学家的记录才得出这一结论的；他经常发现一些观测到的位置与他计算出的椭圆不符——于是他忽略了这些错误的数据。

The mathematical theorems that justify the use of statistical methods usually assume that the measurements made in a scientific experiment or observations are all equally valid. If the analyst picks through the data, using only those numbers that appear to be correct, the resulting statistical analysis can be in serious error. Of course, this is just what scientists have often done. In the early 1980s, Stephen Stigler went through the notebooks of great scientists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, like Albert Michelson, who received the Nobel Prize in 1907 for determining the speed of light. Stigler found that all of them had discarded some of their data before beginning their calculations. Johannes Kepler, who discovered in the early seventeenth century that the planets orbited the Sun in ellipses, did so by reviewing the records of astronomers going back to some of the ancient Greeks; he often found that some observed positions did not fit the ellipses he was computing—so he ignored those faulty values.

但如今，受人尊敬的科学家不再会丢弃看似错误的数据。统计学在科学领域的革命如此深远，以至于实验科学家现在被教导不要舍弃任何数据。统计学的数学定理要求所有测量结果都应被视为同等有效。那么，当某些测量结果明显错误时，应该怎么办呢？1972年的一天，一位药理学家带着这样一个问题来到我的办公室。他正在比较两种预防大鼠溃疡的治疗方法。他确信这两种治疗方法产生了不同的结果，他的数据似乎也印证了这一点。但是，当他按照奈曼-皮尔逊公式进行正式的假设检验时，结果却不显著。他确信，问题出在两只接受效果较差的药物治疗的大鼠的数据上。这两只大鼠都没有出现溃疡，这使得它们的治疗效果远优于另一种药物的最佳疗效。我们在第16章中已经看到，非参数方法是如何发展起来以处理这类问题的。这两个异常值位于数据的错误一侧，而且有两个，使得即使是非参数检验也无统计学意义。

But respectable scientists no longer throw out data that appear to be wrong. So extensive has the statistical revolution in science been that experimental scientists are now taught not to discard any of their data. The mathematical theorems of statistics require that all measurements be considered equally valid. What should be done when some of the measurements are obviously wrong? One day, in 1972, a pharmacologist came to my office with just such a problem. He was comparing two treatments for the prevention of ulcers in rats. He was sure that the treatments were producing different results, and his data seemed to tell him so. But when he ran a formal test of hypothesis following the Neyman-Pearson formulation, the comparison was not significant. The problem, he was sure, came from the data of two rats who had been treated with the poorer of the two compounds. Neither had any ulcers, making their results much better than the best result on the other treatment. We have seen in chapter 16 how nonparametric methods were developed to deal with this kind of problem. These two outlying values were on the wrong side of the data, and there were two of them, making even the nonparametric tests nonsignificant.

如果这事发生在100年前，这位药理学家会直接舍弃那两个错误数值，继续进行计算。当时不会有人反对。但他接受的是现代统计学测量方法的训练。他知道自己不能这么做。幸运的是，我桌上正好放着一本新书，我刚刚读完。这本书名为《位置的稳健估计：调查与进展》，介绍了一项由约翰·图基指导的、名为“普林斯顿稳健性研究”的大规模计算机辅助研究项目。他问题的答案就在这本书里。

Had this happened 100 years ago, the pharmacologist would have thrown out the two wrong values and proceeded with his calculations. No one would have objected. He had been trained in the modern statistical approach to measurements, though. He knew that he was not allowed to do this. Fortunately, a new book was lying on my desk, which I had just finished studying. Entitled Robust Estimates of Location: Survey and Advances, it described a massive computer-oriented effort known as the Princeton Robustness Study, under the guidance of John Tukey. The answer to his problem was in that book.

在这种语境下，“稳健性”一词对美国人来说可能有些陌生。统计学中的许多术语都源自英国统计学家，并常常反映出他们的语言习惯。例如，人们通常将数据中轻微的随机波动称为“误差”。[35]有时，数据中包含一些不仅明显错误，而且可以找出错误原因的值（例如，某块土地上的作物完全歉收）。R.A. Fisher 将这类数据称为“错误数据”。

“Robust,” in this context, may seem a strange word to American ears. A great deal of the terminology of statistics comes from British statisticians and often reflects their use of the language. For instance, it is common to refer to the slight random fluctuations in data as “errors.”[35] Sometimes, the data include values that are not only obviously wrong but for which it is possible to identify the reason why they are wrong (such as the complete failure of crops to grow in a given plot of land). Such data were called “blunders” by R. A. Fisher.

就这样，费舍尔的女婿乔治·博克斯用他自己的英式口音创造了“robust”（健壮的）这个词。博克斯的口音很重，这反映了他成长于泰晤士河口附近的背景。他的祖父曾是五金商，生意兴隆，足以供博克斯的几位叔叔上大学；其中一位后来成为了神学教授。然而，等到博克斯的父亲成年时，生意已经破产，他自己也放弃了高等教育，靠着店员的微薄收入勉强维持生计。乔治·博克斯上过文法学校，他知道家里永远没有足够的钱上大学，于是开始在一所理工学院学习化学。就在这时，第二次世界大战爆发了，博克斯应征入伍。

So it was that George Box, Fisher’s son-in-law, produced the word “robust” out of his own British usage. Box has a strong accent that reflects his origins growing up near the mouth of the Thames. His grandfather had been a hardware merchant, and the business did well enough to send Box’s older uncles on to university educations; one of them became a professor of theology. By the time Box’s father reached manhood, however, the business had failed, and he went without higher education, trying to raise a family on the salary of a shopkeeper’s assistant. George Box attended grammar school, and, knowing that there would never be enough money to go to a university, he began studying chemistry at a polytechnic institute. At that point, World War II began, and Box was drafted into the army.

由于他曾学习化学，所以被派往化学防御实验站工作。当时，英国一些顶尖的药理学家和生理学家正在那里寻找各种毒气的解毒剂。这些科学家中就包括约翰·加德姆爵士，他在20世纪20年代末将统计学革命引入药理学领域，并为药理学的基本概念奠定了坚实的数学基础。

Because he had been studying chemistry, he was sent to work at the chemical defense experiment station, where some of the leading pharmacologists and physiologists in the United Kingdom were trying to find antidotes for different poison gases. Among these scientists was Sir John Gaddum, who had brought the statistical revolution into the science of pharmacology in the late 1920s, and who had put the basic concepts on a firm mathematical footing.

博克斯成为了一名统计学家

BOX BECOMES A STATISTICIAN

乔治·博克斯的上司是一位上校，他正在研究大量关于不同剂量毒气对小鼠和大鼠影响的数据，却百思不得其解。正如博克斯在1986年回忆的那样：

The colonel under whom George Box was working was puzzling over a large amount of data that had been accumulated on the effects of different poison gases at different doses on mice and rats. He could not make any sense out of them. As Box recounted in 1986:

有一天，我对上校说：“你知道，我们真的需要一位统计学家来分析这些数据，因为它们波动很大。” 他说：“是的，我知道，但是我们找不到统计学家，没有合适的。你了解这方面吗？” 我说：“嗯，我不太懂，但我曾经试着读过一本叫《科研人员统计方法》的书，作者是R.A. Fisher。我没看懂，但我认为我明白他想表达的意思。” 他说：“好吧，既然你读了这本书，那就最好读读。”

I said [to the colonel] one day, “You know, we really need to have a statistician look at these data because they are very variable.” And he said, “Yes, I know, but we can’t get a statistician; there isn’t one available. What do you know about it?” I said, “Well, I don’t know anything much about it, but I once tried to read a book called Statistical Methods for Research Workers by a man called R. A. Fisher. I didn’t understand it, but I think I understand what he was trying to do.” And he said, “Well, if you read the book you’d better do it.”

博克斯联系了陆军教育部队，询问能否参加统计方法函授课程。当时并没有这样的课程。统计分析方法尚未纳入大学标准课程。取而代之的是，他收到了一份阅读清单。这份清单不过是当时已出版书籍的合集，其中包括费舍尔的两本书、一本关于教育研究中统计方法的书、一本关于医学统计学的书，以及一本关于林业和牧场管理的书。

Box contacted the education corps of the army to inquire about taking a correspondence course in statistical methods. There were no such courses available. The methods of statistical analysis had not yet moved into the standard university curriculum. Instead, he was sent a reading list. The reading list was nothing more than a collection of the books published up to that date. It included two books by Fisher, a book on statistical methods in educational research, another book on medical statistics, and one dealing with forestry and range management.

博克斯对费舍尔关于实验设计的理念很感兴趣。他在林业著作中找到了具体的实验设计方案，并将其应用于动物实验。（科克伦和考克斯的著作，其中包含大量精心描述的实验设计，当时尚未出版。）这些设计方案往往并不完全适用，因此博克斯利用费舍尔的一般描述，并在此基础上，设计了自己的实验方案。其中一项最令人沮丧的实验是，志愿者被要求将每只手臂上的小块皮肤暴露于芥子气和不同的处理方法中。每个受试者的双臂皮肤暴露情况是相关的，必须在分析中加​​以考虑。必须采取措施，但林业著作中没有相关内容。费舍尔在他的书中也没有讨论过这个问题。博克斯——这位只在理工学院修过一门未完成的化学课程的人——运用基本的数学原理，推导出了合适的实验设计方案。

Box became intrigued with Fisher’s ideas on experimental design. He found specific designs worked out in the forestry book and adapted these designs to the animal experiments. (Cochran and Cox’s book, with its large number of carefully described designs, had not yet been published.) Often the designs were not quite appropriate, so using Fisher’s general descriptions and building on what he had found, Box produced his own designs. One experiment that was most frustrating occurred when volunteers were asked to expose small patches of skin on each arm to mustard gas and different treatments. Each subject’s two arms were correlated, and something had to be done to account for that in the analysis. Something had to be done, but there was nothing like this in the forestry book. Nor had Fisher discussed it in his book. Working from the fundamental mathematical principles, Box, the man whose only education had been an incomplete course in chemistry at a polytechnic school, derived the appropriate design.

博克斯设计的强大之处可以从一个反例中窥见一斑。一位美国眼科医生带着他认为能完美解毒的解药来到实验站，这种解药能杀死路易氏剂，而路易氏剂只需一小滴就能致人失明。他曾在美国对兔子进行过大量实验，他那厚厚的论文证明这的确是完美的解药。当然，他对费舍尔的实验设计一无所知。事实上，他的实验都存在严重的缺陷：他无法将治疗效果与实验设计中杂乱无章的无关因素的影响区分开来。兔子有两只眼睛这一事实，使得博克斯能够设计一个简单的实验，运用他新提出的相关区组设计。这个实验很快就表明，他提出的解药毫无用处。

Some idea of the power of Box’s designs can be seen in a negative result. An American ophthalmologist arrived at the experimental station with what he knew to be the perfect antidote for the effects of lewisite, a small drop of which could blind a person. He had done numerous experiments on rabbits in the United States, and his sheaves of papers proved that this was the perfect answer. Of course, he knew nothing about Fisher’s design of experiments. In fact, his experiments were all badly flawed: The effects of treatment could not be disentangled from the effects of extraneous factors scattered helter-skelter in his designs. The fact that rabbits have two eyes enabled Box to propose a simple experiment that made use of his new design for correlated blocks. This experiment quickly showed that the proposed antidote was useless.

为了描述这些结果，一份报告被撰写出来。报告的作者是英国陆军的一位少校，而统计附录则由博克斯中士撰写，解释了这些结果是如何得出的。负责审批报告的官员坚持要删除博克斯的附录。他们认为附录过于复杂，难以理解。（也就是说，审阅者们看不懂。）但约翰·加德姆爵士读过原报告。他前来祝贺博克斯的附录，却得知最终报告将删去附录。加德姆爵士拉着博克斯，怒气冲冲地闯进大楼的主楼，径直走进负责审查报告的委员会会议室。用博克斯的话来说：“我当时感到非常尴尬。这位德高望重的先生，竟然对这些公务员大发雷霆，说：‘把那该死的东西放回去！’” 他们很快就照做了。

A report was prepared to describe these results. The author was a major in the British army, and Sergeant Box wrote the statistical appendix, which explained how the results were obtained. The officials who had to approve the report insisted that the Box appendix be removed. It was much too complicated for anyone to understand. (That is, the reviewers could not understand it.) But Sir John Gaddum had read the original report. He came by to congratulate Box on his appendix and learned that it was to be cut out of the final report.
